That grandson who frequently moves away and runs on tiptoes down the hallway with a finger in his mouth. And now he only has eyes for his mother, who talks with Silvia about having to get a move on with the potty training, besides the syrups and baby food from the store, and how little it costs to make this creature happy, who asks for the bracelet again. Yes, her mother says. She glances around the living room before leaving, and sometimes says: is everything okay? And Silvia nods her head yes and answers: as always. But today she is daring, tells her she shouldn’t buy such a thing because she has behaved badly and hit her grandmother on the arm because she was so angry she didn’t get what she wanted. Yes, Silvia knew her daughter would say something like: did you do that? And that would be it. But they are leaving, they go quietly and she is alone. She opens the door of the refrigerator, leaves two meals on the countertop, and while before she would have paid for a little silence, now she would pay not to have it. Will you make me something to eat? Gabriele said to her at least thirty hours ago. An eternity, and despite that sometimes she still turns, as if expecting to see her sitting at that same corner of the table where she had eaten lunch. And Silvia said, heating up a skillet before throwing in the ingredients she was chopping: now we’re alone. And Gabriele: then come closer. With that insolent gaze of hers, as if she didn’t believe what she was doing, however there she was, very close to her face and saying: I still feel you inside me. And Silvia thinks about that, while she sets the table and moves around, her hand grabbing an edge of the chair, then she moves away, as if she could still feel it, but not in the same way. She is startled with this silence that speaks inside of her, similar to the sound of a seashell, and if she looks again, a long golden strand of hair is in her hand, but then she loses it. Even turning off the stove she finds herself stuck at the part when she’d said to her: do you like it? Do you like it like this? And she had responded: I like men. But then one appears at the door.

 

Do you remember, Gabriele exclaims, when we were swimming in the pool and we had those neighbors, what were their names? It doesn’t matter, but while we were sunbathing we knew their kids were on the other side of the garden, with all that racket they made. I know, says Bill, they were unbearable. And us, she says, we looked at ourselves and said, you don’t know how good we’ve got it! Yes, Bill nods his head. He suddenly half-closes his eyes and remembers how he put sunscreen on her back. Gabriele continues, we thought more about the little time we could be together than about children; they would have tied us down and forced us to stay together, even if we didn’t feel like it. We didn’t have any ties other than what we felt for each other, we didn’t even promise to be faithful to each other, we pretended like we were free individuals, but at that time we were afraid that if we were together it would end, we were too vulnerable and we stopped trusting each other, admit it Bill, while it lasted we were more aware of who the other was working with than we were of ourselves, I should have implored for our marriage to last longer, but then I ask myself: what good would that have done, if it was going to end anyway? There was a moment, Bill says, when you put the divorce papers on the table, when I wanted to tear them up and kiss you. Well, she says standing up, I wasn’t aware of that at all. I know, he responds, also standing up, there were a lot of mixed emotions. Gabriele walks along this interior room that darkens in places. Only her silhouette moves away, stops, then returns. Well then, she says as she lifts one hand to his elbow, more or less like now. Yes, he says. Bill bends down to her, this woman who just a moment ago was able to stand firm on high heels, can you see? Now she has to hold onto him as tightly as she can. Other than this, there are just goodbye kisses. But look at Gabriele, she goes back to Bill’s mouth as if her train were about to leave, and then another, another, two more. Or him, in his way, encircling one woman in his arms although the other is not very far away. And Bill wouldn’t trade the other one for this one, he’s been settled for a long time. But one day in his memoirs, he might explain how he had the longest embrace that he can remember with Gabriele.

 

Mother, the daughter says to Marianne, we have to talk. She looks at the table that today seems to be full with her son-in-law and her grandchildren. No one wanted to miss how the old woman would react, and everyone is looking at her, as if she had the last word: in a home? You can see she has said it. Yes, the daughter replies, they just told us there is space. Marianne looks at one, then another, and at the step stair in height of the three grandchildren. And what am I going to do there? The woman asks, who then hears: nothing that you don’t want to do. And how is that? The old woman insists, who suddenly looks at the middle grandchild and says: what’s his name doesn’t come to the window anymore, I go for breadcrumbs, but I do this, that, and there’s nothing left for me, then my husband comes on the motorcycle, whistles through the window or accelerates in the street below, my God! Please don’t let him get into an accident, but look, I write and I write on the blackboard, something will work out if he doesn’t return, ah, but then he’s after me the whole day saying, Marianne do this, Marianne do that, Marianne, Marianne, what would you do in my place? Arthur has already died? What do you know, she replies to her grandson, you don’t know anything about anything, I’m the only one who knows, and then there’s the other one, the one with the cards, one day she comes and the next day… Fuck all of you! You, you, you, you and you.

 

Silvia fills her glass and drinks. Her husband picks up a slice of bread and says: you’re not going to be with your lover today? She puts the glass back on the table and shakes her head no. The man puts the bread on his plate, and as he does so he sees a crack on the edge, although the woman’s plate is also nicked, because these are old dishes, but they’ll make it a bit longer, they might even last a long time if they are treated with care. And the husband says: what’s going on, one day he comes, one day he doesn’t, or is he already tired of you? How do I know, Silvia exclaims. She fills up her glass again and drinks. The husband lifts his own glass. She passes him the pitcher, and then turns on the television. But it’s strange, the husband stands up, turns off the television and says: I see you sulking all the time so I guess your affair is over, and what do I do, put up with you, put up with you, and I support you. Silvia looks steadily at him and says quietly: thank you for reminding me of that. You’re welcome, the husband says with a gesture. And let me just make an interjection here, since it’s now out in the open: you’ve spent your whole life complaining, and look how well you live, I would like to spend the whole day sleeping, taking care of the grandchildren, talking with the neighbors, and when I’m not doing that looking for lovers. Silvia puts her hands on her head and says: I didn’t go looking for anything, it just appeared. How, the husband asks, how did it just appear? She clears away the plates and puts them in the sink, turns around, and looking at one of the chairs says: I miss Gabriele, that’s all. Yes, the husband does as well.

 

Marianne goes by car with an old suitcase in her lap. Just a moment ago the son-in-law ordered her to put the suitcase in the trunk. But in the end the old woman got her way. The daughter as well, who looks at traffic signals, or tucks a strand of hair behind her ear when she says: I hope we didn’t forget anything, we were in such a hurry. What does it matter, Marianne is indifferent if she takes two outfits or three, if she takes new or used underwear. It’s not like she’s going to show it to anyone. Ultimately, they end up labeling each piece of clothing in her suitcase, so when they do the laundry at the residence, someone will put it into the correct box when it’s dry. It’s like those campouts when she accompanied and guided the students from mountain to mountain with a compass in her hand so as not to lose which way was north, and on the way back, from town to town and by the road. They hung damp clothing on a long cord extended from one point to another, she and the other teachers, or some father who went along, or a mother, as usual, hanging their clothing by the seams so it doesn’t wrinkle. And Marianne looks from one side to another, she might have even walked down to the road, but Arthur didn’t appear, although he had promised, he didn’t go, not even if it was Sunday. That cold air did have beneficial properties, because her cheeks got pink, and as she breathed, it seemed like her heart might freeze. An agreeable sensation, as if nothing existed other than grass and clothing that the wind ruffled before a storm, not even her own children who were tumbling around down below, could manage to get her out of that type of lethargy, and everything seemed predictable, only the air was unpredictable, and the drops of rain when everyone got alarmed and ran to bring the clothing inside. And she watched from afar. But, the daughter says, who has suddenly picked up her suitcase, we’ll see you Sunday. Yes, Marianne has understood this. Arthur said that too, and look, it’s better not to think about that.

 

Cassandra snacks from different trays this evening, where there is plenty of food to choose from. Small delicacies that the palate tastes slowly and each time perceives a different taste, acidic, salty, bitter and sweet appear and get confused in her throat with the help of a good wine. Cassandra looks at her husband and gives him one of her newer looks, that of: I’ll give this back to you two-fold. And yet she leaves something for the other woman, the one who is visiting and whose presence somewhat changes the peace that had reigned in the house, although her relationship is solid, and if it cracks a bit from time to time, it is nothing dangerous. What can she do? Her face says with a grimace when she looks at Gabriele. In fact she should be grateful. Thanks to her, every hour Cassandra remembers how much she loves this man, how could she say it? No, she wouldn’t say that, not even with her last breath, because Bill would think she’d just lost her mind, and that it wasn’t her talking but senility. However, sometimes, being of sound mind she stops pumping the pedals of this relationship and does what she usually does in a situation like this, which is to let Bill make every error possible, and believe you me, there is no other woman who would want to be with this fading actor. Cassandra knew she was getting away with a jewel of a man, every woman was envious of her, of this man, some other man, the one who was famous so very long ago, and another, and yet another one, and now it so happens that all of those pieces have lost value, that her collection of lead soldiers is today a collection worthy of a museum. Leave it Bill, Gabriele says suddenly, I’m the one who acted in it, While the beasts slept was in seventy-two. He raises his glass, which is hand cut crystal, and the actor drinks slowly, looking through the crystal, so transparent and at the same time disconcerting when it captures the color of her hair, and no matter how he turns the glass, the hair is always dark. Although Gabriele is seated nearby, it is due to the light that the blonde doesn’t appear on the edge. Oh, the visitor exclaims, did you hear, Cassandra? Now he’s saying that John Faison was no good! And how he was in that movie, the other adds, even dressed in that tattered clothing of a fugitive he was splendid!

 

If anyone heard that, they would fall right over. Or even laid out in the bed, they would turn and look at Silvia’s hair, which looks shorter and fine and silky, as if she had just come from the hairdresser’s. Then she complains about the money. There isn’t enough, she says. And how she spends it! When it isn’t one thing, it’s another. The fact is that her husband needs her. Her husband has need of her. And the instinct of that species tells me that he won’t stop until he gets his way. Silvia doesn’t feel like it. No, she tells her husband. Leave me alone! She has tried that before, sometimes it was alright, although normally she ends up turning over and faking she is dead. Sometimes when he has touched her like an animal, she has thought about giving in to him completely, other times, for example, when the thing is precipitated with a little nibble on the neck, always softer than she expected. However, the man’s elbow starts to pin her arm down. She would like to remember both things: abandoning him for the damage he does to her. Although it looks like her memory is occupied, there are many fragments that mix together and go from one side of her brain to the other. Today she forgets what day it was that her daughter’s first tooth fell out; it is no longer important. And one day that was so unpleasant with her in-laws when they said she was poisoning them so she would get everything! In the end she only had this man, the rest of it the husband still has. He asks why his wife barely participates in this bed when love is something that two must take part in. And he begs, the man begs between gritted teeth for her to put some effort and will into this, as she would do if her lover were here now, how about you do that? Come on, Silvia says. She places her hand on the man’s back and breathes lightly, as if intending to help him in this final phase, and finish, finish already, it doesn’t have to go on all day, and from one moment to the next the weight of her husband will fall on top of her, he will come back to himself without any hurry, as she also does in the exact instant when he turns his back to her.

 

Bill prepares drinks for the two women who are seated in front of the fire. A CD ends and Cassandra stands up and chooses another. She walks in sock feet, and when she comes back she sits in her husband’s lap. It is nighttime, the floor trembles slightly, and as it does Bill says that the coyotes go by in packs not far from here. A bass melody fills this smallest room. Gabriele has a glass in her hand when she sees Cassandra’s arm run over her husband’s back, and she senses that Cassandra has just forgiven him. Yes, alcohol makes one forget sins. Although Gabriele’s look remains clear, this moment does not belong to her. She should think about it more deeply. The thing is, she always reaches the same conclusion: if she had just waited a few days to fill the side of the bed that had become empty. It had to be done. He had been the most common thing at hand, pruning the bushes close to her window, although it would seem that Bill was irreplaceable. Cassandra starts to hum a song, Gabriele recognizes the melody and joins in on the chorus. These two are such comedians, and it would seem that only Bill seems interested in laying aside his actor persona, that’s how he is. More bored perhaps, but at the same time an endearing boredom that ends up showing his smile, just in case destiny decides that this was the last night.

 

Sometime later, Gabriele turns on the light in her room and takes down the clothing that is hanging in the armoire; she takes it to one of the suitcases. Then she throws herself down on the bed, as if she weren’t actually going to sleep, but just to rest a while. There are those who would reflect at times like these, and who, like her, would look at her agenda and start to feel the unbearable weight of age. Can’t you see how it oppresses her? It is often in her throat and makes her cough. Or in her half-closed eyelids, then the agenda starts to slide. Her hearing is going too, but it’s likely that she is already dreaming. Not even in one of her most golden dreams could she foresee this heavy burden, to which she adds a suitcase that she drags over and leaves very close to the veranda, where Bill appears with his back to her. A magnificent sunset, don’t you think Mr. Miller? And she sees a blinking light. Then the actress opens her suitcase and lays out the contents on the railing. Better like this, she says. She raises her hand to Bill’s face and he says: you didn’t have to come. Strange that in this dream a sudden change in the script manages to wake her up, and she doesn’t know how it ends. Of course Bill wasn’t actually Bill.

 

Marianne’s bedroom isn’t her bedroom. But they wake her up, make her swallow a pill, and she goes back to sleep. Just like Silvia’s husband also isn’t the same man whose hand she took one day. How many broken promises, and how her husband said he would take care of her, like a porcelain doll he said, and look how it breaks, there isn’t a day that goes by that it doesn’t need an urgent repair with glue. But how she endures it, just by slowly opening her eyes as the first light of day comes in, she already seems willing to give this new day a chance, she hopes for a lot from anything, and then they come. Don’t think that by sitting down and waiting you achieve great things. Chance just asks you to be in the exact place at the precise time. And Gabriele looks chance head-on, even opening the curtains and yawning one more day on this balcony where she might meet up with Bill at any time. The location is the exact one, perhaps the moment is somewhat imprecise. What does it matter, she is leaving, and with two suitcases that she takes to the front door of the house. Strange that Cassandra embraces her and is worried about her in this brief half hour that remains. Even while they eat breakfast, the couple promises to visit her someday. Gabriele spreads butter on her toast, when she nods her head and says: I hope so. Then she places the knife on the edge of her plate and tells them the dream she had. Cassandra laughs and says: did Bill sound like me?

 

Marianne starts to realize that this door has been opened and closed without asking for permission. They get her up, take off her pajamas and take her directly to the shower. There she complains about the jet of water on her. No matter what she says, they are going to make her shower. Therefore it is better not to fight since they could say she’s crazy, the helper says to lather up with soap. Marianne gets quiet, and from time to time looks at this woman she does not know, and talks in a way she is able to understand. Lower your head. And she lowers it. Yes, it is very simple, they say, whoever behaves themselves gets prizes. What prize? Marianne says, who seems willing to behave herself in exchange for a few cookies. Yes, she used to have that for breakfast in the house where she was before, and her daughter was there of course, where did she get off to? She isn’t even here to dress her, or later to wipe her robe off with a napkin. Although they eat breakfast anyway, because more people like her sit down at the table, or at other tables with more people like them. Here, old age is concentrated in a room, in some corner of the garden, or near the door. Leave if you want to, says someone Marianne’s age. And she listens to the comments. Of course she immediately forgets this and doesn’t remember what she was going to do, how she got here, and if she was going somewhere she doesn’t remember where.

 

Gabriele’s agenda is misplaced, and suddenly, while she is standing in line with Bill and Cassandra at her side, she can’t find the ticket anywhere. Here it is, she finally says, as she puts her hand into the pocket of her coat. We’re going to miss you, they tell her while they wait in a café at the airport. Gabriele later says goodbye and goes up the escalator. One thing that is certain is that this trip is starting to look like those she used to take when her life was busy. And more so today, when they were talking with their glasses in hand, and people came up to her and took photographs with them. There were even some who said, Mr. Engel, would you sign an autograph for me? Mrs. Finn, would you sign an autograph for me? Then they looked at her, at her, some even tilting their heads, as if they couldn’t believe that she was there with them. And then they said, Mrs. Bates, would you sign an autograph for me? Or they went from her, or from Bill. The fact is that Cassandra is the most valuable of the three, also the one with the most awards. And today the most kissed, and the only one who had not said that she missed this. Bill doubted it as he signed: how was it, would you help me, Gabriele? And she has brought her hand to her forehead, as if she had doubted for a few seconds, then she said: if you’re going to kiss me, don’t ask me if I am leaving and not coming back. At the same time he was astonished, although moments later he said: it went like this, do it, just do it, it’s long overdue. Then he put his head down, as she did as well when she returned a signed scrap of paper to someone. Keep the pistol, gunfighter, Cassandra then said, and then she signed. Or Gabriele: strange that you ask me that line, I don’t remember it being mine.

 

Silvia goes down three stairs, looks both ways, tells her granddaughter to cross the street, and it seems she obeys and she goes off towards school alone. She goes back in, drags a box of toys to the kitchen and lets her grandson stack them. There, very good, says this woman who doesn’t let him out of her sight. She removes the tablecloth and scrubs four things that accumulated in the sink. She goes into the bedroom, takes off the sheets and puts on clean ones. What a beautiful truck! Silvia exclaims, coming and going as she makes the bed, and wipes dust off the nightstands. Then she takes the dirty clothes and makes two piles on the floor. One for the washing machine, the other will have to wait in the basket. Because now she sweeps, or picks up the mess of pieces her grandson throws down when he gets bored. Very quickly it seems, because he’s right behind her. Don’t stand there, she reproaches him. And she takes him back to the dining room. Pleasant company, she thinks as she looks closely at this creature who is kneeling on the rug and pushing a train with his diminutive hands. And what does the train do? She exclaims, putting the mop in the bucket and continuing with what she was doing. Later she kneels down on the rug, but she can’t get into that position, she lies on her back on the sofa and glances through some old magazine that later she will put back in the drawer of the coffee table. But her grandson discovers this novelty, which looks more interesting than the games that came from the other house, and this little one probably has in his head some drawer that he wanted to open, rummage around in, but he doesn’t know how. Mama, the little one says with a bubble of saliva on his mouth. And Silvia breathes deeply, puts a foot into her sandal and escapes. Although she isn’t going anywhere in particular, just from one room to another. And sometimes, within this strange space where there is not a speck of dust, it seems unbearable, or the end of the bed where she sits, comfortably, while she unties the knot in her robe and changes her slippers for shoes, which she buckles while she looks at her legs then stands up. Don’t hurry, she said. But wait! Silvia had said that day. It wasn’t so long ago, but this memory seems so distant it’s as if it never occurred, and Silvia wishes it hadn’t, because now she wouldn’t be thinking about this, and she would carry on with her daily tasks, from the supermarket to the bakery, from the kiosk to the drugstore, and on a day like today she would run her hand over the banister in the entryway, which has started to flake again. She should think about other things, she should think about her husband, and about what would make him happy and she shouldn’t be thinking about this, but about a good cut of beef, the butcher would cut it by hand while she said: cut that a little thicker for my husband. So many things she could do, like, for example, go out with a friend to some café where they would trade stories: yes, steaming fish has fewer calories, but it definitely loses flavor. Or today I’m giving you a needlework magazine, and no, you don’t need to return it to me. And so many, so many things, like the children, the children’s studies, the children’s work, the children’s cars, the children’s weddings, and then one day the grandchildren come. Grandmothers! Then happiness arrives with some other sort of complexity. But, as I said, now she should not be thinking about Gabriele, but getting ready to take her grandson to play at the park. The park isn’t very far away from here, but right now it seems so far away that it is better not to think about it, and although there comes a moment when her head and her body meet due to the insistent rubbing of her hand, she should continue saying that she shouldn’t be silencing her breathing in the pillow, she should be hanging up clothing, drying it, folding it and ironing it. And although she rubs her fingers together under the tap, nothing is as it was, only the crows’ feet around her eyes, and this strange wrinkle by her lip. And don’t go, like that voice that said: if you’re going to kiss me, do it before it’s too late.

 

Marianne is seated in a chair in the garden. Her silhouette in the shade lengthens along the stone walk. The sun fills this spot with life. The birds land on the fence and sing. Then they leave. Others come, but they also leave. The animal world is intelligent, it shows anyone that this fence is higher than it should be. Naturally these birds are not insinuating this. They are not philosophers or poets, and if they had the chance they would shit on anyone’s head. Although man is also capable of killing them in full flight, and I assure you that he is indifferent to what phase of life they are in, if they are courting, nesting, or if they are rebuilding their nest after a windstorm. Everyone has their own problems, like this geriatric assistant who talks about mortgages on her cell phone during working hours. The people from the bank tell her she should have built her house with twigs. No one asks Marianne, this woman who is half asleep. The light that was there not long before is fading, even Arthur who, while he eats, stops a moment and says: how on earth can you eat soup with a fork? And Marianne laughs when she responds fearfully: I hadn’t realized. And her husband shakes his head and goes on the attack again: do you even know where your head is? Simultaneously, she recedes with her back to him and is quiet. Dear Gabriele, she writes from dream to dream, you tell me about your debut in the theater and I take forever to respond because at the end of the day I realize there is much to add since your previous letter, and I try, I look for anything that won’t bore you, and in the end I always end up talking to you about school, this time to tell you that I am not exercising, the doctors wanted it like that, everyone says that I talk about something and I interrupt myself, like this letter, I spend weeks without realizing that the time has come to send it, I didn’t want you to think that I am ill, better to leave it with me being happy that you are back in the theater, it has rained a lot since that time you appeared in the theater class arguing that you deserved the leading role because you were the oldest, but I gave you the smallest role I could, it seemed to me the only way to show you that in a scene, every role has its reason for being.

 

Clouds and more dark clouds that Gabriele observes from the airplane with a snack in her hand. As she looks from one side to another, ongoing turbulence alarms the passengers, but not her, she seems calm while she folds up the table and fastens her seatbelt. She could talk about the ongoing turbulence she has experienced, and look, it continues here. One time she remembers best is when they were filming abroad, it was the first scene of While the beasts slept, suddenly, that miniscule black spot in the middle of the snow approached the town, and she left the basket on the ground and walked towards that man, who walked slowly as if he were wounded. And Gabriele ran, when the director suddenly shouted: Change of plans! Cut! And John threw off the blanket that was covering him and asked what was happening from a certain distance away. She stopped in the middle of the road, and panting, responded: he says we should forget the script. And the actor cussed: he told me to change the producer, when everything was going well with the other. Gabriele as well, who looked at her nails and said: yes, yes I know, in the other everything is singing and dancing, but here we work hard, Mr. Faison. Let’s do it again, the director said. And she reached a hand out towards the basket and went back to hanging clothing. The actor ran until he was so far away he looked like a flea, while she had to remain there and not move. And that’s how it was until John Faison, very close to the actress, fell on the ground. At the same time Gabriele looked at him without really knowing what to do, but then she turned him over and dragged him towards the house. This isn’t going well, the director exclaimed. And Bates collected the clothes again, took them to the basket, and then went back to hanging them up. John got up off the ground again and ran until the camera gave the signal, and he approached again. Then, in that scene where no one yet understood where the problem was and how to resolve it, in the middle of it Gabriele opened his shirt, and feeling his skin, discovered the wound from the war that he covered again, only now her pale hands were covered in blood. And although she wiped her hands with a towel that got more and more smeared with that edible product, the red ended up being the key in that scene. It was on the fugitive’s breath, it ran down her hands, penetrated her clothing, and no matter how much he scraped his feet, his shoe had a red line on it. The blood also dripped onto the snow, and symbolically, the trace of a past war, including when they started to film it, they lowered the camera to the level of the wound, as if the faces of the actors didn’t matter. Anonymous characters, the director finally decided. Later, in the next scene they had to lift the camera, something about the box office, orders from the higher-ups, who were more important than those from the afterlife.

 

And there was Ms. Bates’ face, lighting up when the actor awakened and coughed, or looked at the roof, that strange bedroom. While she boiled water over the fire, or scrubbed heavy pieces of cloth. A flirt, insinuated the women who were working as extras when she entered the doctor’s house, and without knocking on the door asked him to come right away. Hurry, she said to him while she went back down the street. The man didn’t have much time to live, a few days, the doctor predicted. She took out a man’s shirt, a shirt of the one who wouldn’t be back, and she dressed John as if he were her husband. We watched the actress cultivating the land, or going inside and carrying some container with boiling water while she disinfected the wound, changed the bandage, and suddenly the fugitive opened his eyes and looked at the ceiling. It seemed there was an angel before him when he took her arm and said: help me, for heaven’s sake, help me. At the same time Gabriele, who was in mourning, took his hand and said: rest. While the man shivered, was delirious and talked about grenades, trenches, bombs, and so many dead men in a matter of seconds. Gabriele’s hands were startled as they changed the bandage, then she covered the wound again. The director was the same with her, who on another day asked her to cover her hair with a black scarf. That angelic-looking actress with a rebellious nature was left behind, she seemed to be wearing an evening gown or a day dress that was equally as expensive. That wasn’t important, I wouldn’t know what to say, but there were other things, like the misery, the tiny crystals freezing on the window, the many chickens in the henhouse. Although very little remained of all that, only the hunger with which she grasped one, pressing it in her apron, then she brought it towards her torso, and knife in hand, another bloody scene as they say. The apron was so white and look how it ended up, soaking and with the soup almost ready for this moribund diner, who asked for a bit of heat. And Gabriele blew on the spoon, and not seeing another way to see if he was alive other than removing her scarf, she let her hair fall onto the chest of a stranger. At the same time, the director approached them and explained things that had to do with perspective and how they had to be shown, what feelings he wanted to arouse and which ones he did not want to see. And Gabriele, who had remained quiet up until now, had to burst into tears right at that moment. It was something that required huge effort, similar to the enormous vacuum that Bill had left, and he was starting to be replaced by a fugitive, someone who didn’t even exist. On the other hand, John Faison had also modified the distinctive trait that had defined him up to now, where he had shown that he knew how to sing and dance, although here they just asked him to be in agony in an arena in a take that was longer than usual.

 

Down deep, it is not necessary to be an actor to be able to show a feeling similar to that. The day-to-day gives a chance to everyone who has watched how doors close one after the other throughout a lifetime, and how time lets dry leaves pile up at the base of those doors, not even the years at one’s back could get into line without fighting. Hello love, Silvia says to her husband with a little smile on her face. Then she serves him one of those frozen dinners that he dislikes so much. But at least it is something hot to put in his mouth. And heating up frozen dinners isn’t as hard a task as the other. You take it out of the freezer, put it into the oven, and when the timer goes off, there is a long break. Of course this precooked food doesn’t have the characteristic taste of the house, but rather the feel of a whim, that of gaining time for oneself, although she spends it all on feeding this little one. But now Silvia eats slowly, and suddenly she brushes the leg of her husband and again sketches a smile, like before, with the beautiful moments that are now part of history. However others appear, better or worse, although perspective, no matter how it drags on, only provides minimal changes. Who would have thought that Silvia would be here at sixty-something eating at the same table, with the same man by her side, and with her grandson, the third one, and there might still be more to come, why not? A thrilling theme, reproduction, that starts how it starts and ends up in the worst way. Silvia is no longer able to reproduce, nature has said enough, and reluctantly there are many like her, more or less identical copies who are replicated all over the world, some sleep, others awaken, and one day it even gives one hope knowing that you are not alone. Because she is not, there are two here who don’t stop eating, then there comes a moment when they say to her that the dinner was worthless, just like her.

 

Gabriele appeared alone in the last scene, her lips pale as she whispered in front of a tomb, left flowers for a dead man, wiped her cheek… Then she leaned forward and asked forgiveness for taking a flower, which she tossed onto the other tomb, that of the fugitive, and the actress fell on her knees as she left, she moved away and the camera focused for the last time on that landscape that was so white and so very cold. That same afternoon Gabriele said to John, as she removed her makeup in her trailer, that when the filming was finished she was leaving, that she wasn’t accustomed to staying even one second longer, and as she said that her gaze dropped. The fact is that she didn’t have anyone waiting for her. And although suddenly she looked at herself again in the mirror, she unscrewed the mascara tube, put mascara on again, stood up and saw that John Faison was silently looking out the small window. But at what, exactly? She could only add that if one were looking through that opening, one would see how the cameraman moved away, then how he embraced his woman. And a little later, while the actress was changing her clothes, she saw how that couple had just gotten into the car and left. Finally, she thought as she pulled up her dress, an idyll has ended here. And for John as well, who looked at the man who had escaped him, not even saying goodbye. Because if the actress had had to speak at this precise moment, it would only be to say that she never would have thought that the cameraman liked men. And so many many things she should have sensed, but look, the only thing she said was: John, would you zip me up? And the actor approached, one hand on her dress. Then the actress turned to the side and said very low: zip me up. And suddenly, while Gabriele was fastening the cuffs she felt the actor behind her, his hands encircling her, and she added: I was sixteen years old and things were not going well, my parents didn’t have work, and whenever that happened there was nothing to keep them there, only the desire to see something new in the north, in the west, wherever they thought it would be good, but they were always wrong, and one day they would make the least correct decision of their entire lives, I rebelled against them and got a bottle of wine that a friend and I drank directly from the bottle, we finished the whole thing, we laughed, we cried, and the last night together we made promises that I didn’t keep, then, when it was time to say goodbye we were quiet, looking at the wall in the bedroom as if there was finally nothing left to talk about, and in fact everything was summarized in a small note that I folded and kept, although her hands were empty because I couldn’t give her an address or a telephone number in exchange, suddenly I looked at her, she looked at me and it seemed blurrier to me than a while ago, but we kissed, of course that startled her, she broke away and said it was because of the wine, but I would have sworn that alcohol was not the reason.

 

Gabriele continued talking with John in a restaurant on the edge of the city when she said: and one day Bill showed up, he was so strangely close that I clung to him with all my might, although we had an open relationship, because it frightened both of us to think that marriage condemned us to spend our entire lives together, although looking back, neither of us knew how to have a relationship like that, I was not unfaithful to him; I wanted too much, and he ended up leaving me for the first one who went after him. There comes a moment in life, Gabriele continued as she watched the actor, when I started to notice that everything depends on the moment and with which leg I stand on, and today I have just put that theory out into the universe, I thought that by this time my driver would already be taking me home, however everything seems to indicate that we are about to make a serious mistake. And the actress said as she picked up the motel key: nevertheless, to err is so very human… A little later, she ran her fingers over a piece of furniture and left the key there, as well as a couple of hairpins that she’d pulled her hair back with. She turned around and the actor was getting into the bed, and with a moribund voice started with: help me, for heaven’s sake, help me. And she got into the bed as well, the one who healed wounds, and who once the filming had finished seemed willing to continue binding wounds, she even put a handkerchief over her hair that he later removed, and a red tress fell onto his stomach when she said: you’ve been on the edge of life and death, but I knew you would survive. Then John took her hair in his hands and said: I’m alive thanks to you. And also this: now get on the cot. And she made room in the bed, although they had both stopped acting a few minutes ago. I’m sorry, John said, I thought I could. And Gabriele remembers this, as she goes down the steps of a plane, and then they take the travelers from the flight to the airport.

 

Silvia closes the door to the house, walks down three steps, and goes into the street. There are days she goes nowhere, she follows the lines on the sidewalk and simply pushes the pram to the left. She often returns to her starting point, which is this house, and sometimes with a couple of supermarket bags, which means taking them in and going back to her starting point, with the excuse of putting items away in the pantry and in the refrigerator, and a magazine that she leaves on top of the table, while her grandson goes to the small table and tries to open a box. She reads and looks at photographs on fashion, pieces of clothing that she could sew by hand if she looked carefully, she has thread and needles, but she doesn’t have the right fabric. At the same time she looks at this box where she keeps postcards, some from Gabriele. Down deep the actress never stayed long, not even this week, she came and went in the same way. And now she stores it away again and closes the box, locking it with a key. In no case can this little boy damage it; she wouldn’t forgive him. The grandson runs in circles, from the floor to the rug, from the rug to the armchair, from the armchair to the sofa. Silvia turns on the television and with the remote looks for some channel where they do drawings, something she also looks at from time to time, when she turns the page and lifts her gaze, although she sees too many colors, and if she drops her gaze it remains on this chair. So have I, Gabriele had said to her as she sat here, as if she could still see her. At this point she should make an effort so this image won’t recede, so the words won’t be erased. She tries to retain something that she no longer has. And she brings a hand to her forehead while she thinks, it will be longer, until her lover appears, her lover is something in the past. And if she dares to look farther back she will see how that man managed to get her out of that damn routine; he asked for a lot in return. As did Gabriele. Silvia now thinks that this was best for everyone. And finally she ends up taking that chair up to the attic.

 

Marianne picks up an eraser and puts it on the blackboard where the menu had been. Then she leaves the dining room and creeps into one of the elevators. But the door closes suddenly. It seems like it is going up, somewhere; she hasn’t pushed a button. Then she greets anyone who stumbles in with a nod of her head as it continues straight up, she might stop in front of a room and look to see if she can find hers. Apparently each room is the same as every other, only the number changes, although she’s been looking for a while, and she isn’t sure exactly which one it is. She has lost track of time, and the watch she was wearing, they failed to fasten it on her this morning while they were dressing her. But now it seems that someone finds her, takes her back to the elevator and puts her in this room where they eat. Today they help her, although it doesn’t seem that she’s very hungry, besides she would prefer to stand up and keep looking. Not for the room, nor for Arthur, but for the one who used to take care of her and who said: Mother, shall I turn on the television for you? But there is a television in this room as well, which she ended up finding while they bring the fork up and she chews. Images and more images, some disagreeable, others quite terrible. This world is always in a bad state, she seems to say with a simple glance. Marianne, the aide says suddenly, if you finish your dinner I’ll let you write on the blackboard. What would I write there? The woman says, looking back at the television. Then she shakes her head no, smiles, and says no again with her finger.

 

A taxi stops in front of the hotel. Moments later Gabriele gets out of the car. Someone takes her suitcases while she fills out the card at reception. Then she goes up to the eighth floor, walks along the corridor, and seems willing to tip this young man who is helping her; she gives him a good tip. Like when Mrs. Bates was at the top of her game. What does it matter, life is topsy-turvy, and this week has been non-stop. The actress opens the door to the balcony and looks at the number of cars that are driving on the main street at rush hour. When she goes back in, one might sense that she once thought about throwing herself into the void. That’s how life is, life is naked in this bed. She pulls her purse close and takes out a Kleenex; there are many who would love to be in her place. Fewer and fewer though. Women who, at another time had wanted to be her and who had dyed their hair blonde, but they could still never be her. Then the actress cut her hair, with bangs down to her eyebrows. And anyone who wanted to look like her cut their hair the way she had hers cut, some more successfully than others, depending on the skill with which the hairdresser wielded the scissors. But no matter how well they sported that cinematic look, neither Bill Engel nor John Faison would ever show up in any of those women’s houses. Not even if they got lost at some crossroads. There would be someone else there, however, someone not as handsome as John or Bill, and if someone wanted them they were easy to get. People who were definitively single and young, the type who wanted to eat the world up in two days, but then on the third day the world would swallow them whole without a second thought. But they carried on. Some better than others. As did Gabriele and John during a press conference, when they looked at each other and smiling said in unison: love, love at first sight! And the actress turned her glance a little later to the other side and showed her engagement ring, or she turned to look at John again, and taking his hand added: if I didn’t think he were the love of my life, I wouldn’t marry. A lot of people read that news. In his office Bill folded the newspaper; Cassandra unfolded it seconds later. During a break Marianne read the article of that day, and then took a piece of paper out of the box and wrote, although shortly thereafter she threw it into the trash. Marianne wasn’t her mother, nor her teacher, rather she was the director of a function which, based on many letters ended up being a good adviser, one of those who could look once more at that ostentatious ring with a magnifying glass and make her remember who she was and where she came from. But how could she say something like that without hurting her? The news only went unnoticed by Silvia, and it would echo a few months later, when they were right in the middle of filming a new movie, Magnolias and petals, it was called. It was one of the few films in which Gabriele did not give it her all.

 

The actress looks at the bathtub as she takes off her clothes. Very pretty and round, but no doubt at her age it could be a dirty trick being played on her. When she gets in or out, the wretchedness is whimsical, and here there is no when or how, not even the gravity of the illness. Oh, Gabriele did not want to fall so quickly. But why think about this as she steps into the shower? Years ago she would have thrown herself into the pool without thinking about the consequences, which were so rarely considered that there would be a point at which she and John came to believe that imbroglio, which seemed very much like a labyrinth. Because they went in and kept going down any corridor, one towards the left, the other towards the right, only a greenish thickness separated them, and even so, they still hoped to find the exit and get out unscathed. If love arrived in the form of a tray, every morning the sun appeared in that alcove behind the curtain, and that breakfast in the bed that they shared like two perfect idiots. You have marmalade on your cheek. And Gabriele moved closer, every day a little closer, until suddenly there was no space between them. And if she kissed him, he moved back with some excuse. In fact, John said, pouring coffee into the milk, the manager has called me, two times, it must be important. What does he want? She exclaimed. It seemed that it was important, the actor pointed out. And she got up and left, although she returned a short while later and went on her knees to the bed, but that mattress was against her. Because then John got up and said: I can’t do this anymore. And he got dressed in the best clothing he had and left. Gabriele remained in the bed, her mascara falling in drops while she smoked, her hand trembling. Not just her hand, it was her whole body trembling. And she closed John’s closet door and threw a shoe at it, of course later she would go and open it and pull down his clothing and put it on. Then just one thing was missing. Not even dressing like a man could she compete with them. But do you think it looked good on her? She put on a tie but the knot gave her problems, she wrapped it wrapped it, and in the end it was John who did the knot, he raised it slowly and only one thing was visible: that erection of a champion.

 

A bird sings at Marianne’s window in this bittersweet dream which, from time to time, surprises her while she is standing, seated, kneeling. Or in other uncomfortable positions when she falls out of the bed. And that one said: Mother, I am going to tie you with a tether so you don’t fall. And she said a lot of other things, gabbing the whole day, talking about this, that and the other thing. She certainly never had anything of value to say. Not even when she talked about trips, the poor wretch, she spent her life with the aspiration of going on vacation, but she never went. She always said: Mother, you know where I would like to be now? Or even, Mother, I would like to snap my fingers and be there. At least the only thing that Marianne hopes is that she can do it now. Even if it was only in her mind, she would send her to that white sand beach and wait for a coconut to fall on her so she would react. She would put someone else in her daughter’s place. And the daughter would put her in another place entirely. Besides, she wouldn’t want the little ones to miss her too much. Only what is necessary. Yes, when it is time to change rooms, and the oldest one says: this was my grandmother’s room. Or the little one, the one who laughed, cried, and punched the baby high chair with his little hands, but one day he realizes that something is missing in the dining room. Or ultimately the middle one, the grandmother’s favorite, who is also the only one who understood things right away, even when the woman didn’t know how to explain them, and even so the middle one said: grandmother wants water. She would gesture with her glance, he never was one for many words, but she was satisfied. Then there was that one, the one who pissed himself at school, but she doesn’t remember it now, people forget things, and it’s good to let some things go.

 

Silvia is seated on the sofa. She watches television, and her husband does the same. Both have ceased to be surprised by anything. An earthquake shakes the other side of the world, many buildings in ruins and so many dead, there are dead children, and the mud covers everything. The world is strange, a poorly made ball, it isn’t even round, but it revolves every day and the sun ends up hitting it on all sides, although interest wanes when the news is from far away. Because here in this house, not even a slight tremor has been felt. And life and death are observed through this somewhat oval-shaped screen, but someone is singing on another channel. If her husband keeps pushing with his finger he will find something where they are talking about the economy, or more news, on one someone is cooking, on another the public applauds on set, and although he clicks through channels with the remote, there is just one thing to note, Silvia moves closer to his arm and observes what happens in this position. One might say that the husband stops changing the channels at this moment, although if he could, he would put his wife on pause, he would have done it a long time ago, until he realized that without her life would be infinitely more painful. But the man contains himself, he doesn’t seem to be brave enough to push the button again and let his wife continue with what she was doing, she was about to close her eyes, but as I have already said, if he could, he would leave things like this for his entire life. At a minimum he would control her while he comes and goes from work, while he comes and goes from the room, and sooner or later there would come a point at which, sitting on the toilet, he would ask why there was no more toilet paper and where in the hell does Silvia keep more. And although he would find more in the closet, someday the refills would run out. Then he would have to go out and buy more, as well as things to eat that he wouldn’t have to cook, and it would be expensive, while he comes and goes from the sofa, always going back to sit in the same place where Silvia decided to sit one day. Besides, if he wants it reciprocated, he must find the point, somewhat more to the left, and the arm a little farther back, as it was on that day, with the remote in the other hand where he would eventually have to end up forgiving her, why not? Life was simpler before. And that’s how it would be, although only to reproach her that no one else could put up with her, and also, as he had said, who would want her at her age. And Silvia, who had her eyes half-closed, opens them, sees something on the screen, the actress has said before she can manage to change the channel with the remote: do it, just do it. Yes, well, Silvia has felt a certain weight at having missed that movie with that celebrated kiss that now she can’t stand to see. At another time she might have decided to record it, even if just to rewind to that phrase when she said: if you’re going to kiss me, don’t ask me if I’m back to stay. If I’m back to stay, do it, just do it.

 

Gabriele is seated on the bed, her back a little curved, her agenda in one of her hands. Today she is here, in this hotel room, the door out to the terrace ajar. She looks at the agenda, pages and more pages, telephones, addresses, it looks like a lot of people, and there, John Faison appears under the letter with which that city begins, under this name an address that he himself wrote with a black pen. Yes, today Gabriele is in this city where John lived, and where she lived for a while as well. But now she just picks up a felt-tip marker and blacks out that address, she goes over it several times. She also marks out the telephone number. And although she then puts the lid on and brings it up to her chest, nothing is as she remembered it. All of a sudden Gabriele yearns for that time when she was alone in the office of a private doctor, waiting for a simple piece of news, whether or not she was pregnant. An interminable wait when she ended up thinking about whether she would know how to bring up that child who might be on its way. Can you believe that she wasn’t very clear about that? Would she bring up the child, or would she let others do it? The only thing that would happen if she were pregnant is that she would have to say no to filming that new movie, and to do so as soon as possible, that way there would be no surprises and they could find another actress right away. It was true that she had already memorized that role. And frankly she needed something new, not just those new bangs that she blew upward while she was waiting, but a change in register where she could show that she knew how to dance a tango with John and no one would be able to tell that she was counting the whole time. She looked at her hands, and ended up crossing them on the table as the doctor approached her with an envelope in his hand. He sat down, and Gabriele imagined that John would play a great role as a father. They had never discussed children, but now a child could appear in their lives in a matter of months. I’m sorry, the doctor then said, you will have to keep trying. She went down a little later in the elevator of that building with a smile on her face, although it faded away little by little, and suddenly it wasn’t there anymore, just those light eyes that suddenly welled up, her face reddened, her mouth opened with a thread of saliva. You will have to keep trying, the doctor said. Well tell me how! She exclaimed a little later to her driver, that same man who took her wherever she told him to go. She usually sat in the back seat without saying anything. She crossed her hands, lowered her face, breathed in and said: take me home. And when she returned to that state in which she seemed engrossed, she lifted her gaze again, and looking out the window saw where she was and corrected him: you have misunderstood; take me to my house.

 

Gabriele has left the hotel and goes down different streets which, if her sense of smell is not wrong, will take her somewhere where she can have a drink. As she walks, she sees that many of the buildings that she remembered are no longer there, and there are others in their place. And if she went there or not, I don’t know, although she ended up finding some local joint that is still holding out, with other people, and all of the faces she sees are younger than she expected, some are even adolescents. Many new faces; she doesn’t recognize anyone. But the actress sits down crossing her legs, she puts a hand on the bar and hears herself say, pour me a whiskey. While she waits, even with her eyes only half open she can sense the number of men who frequent this bar. She opens her eyes completely and they stop on the face of the only other woman who, like her, is here on a Friday. Although if she loses her gaze in this drink, she could still remember the instant when she appeared in the studios where they were starting to film Magnolias and petals, and that immense smile of John’s when he saw her. That was a long time ago, and although the actress looks for the second time at the woman and crosses her legs, bringing them towards this side, Gabriele would say that the encounter when she kissed John did nothing more than drag out a relationship that was just barely hanging on. Because suddenly that actor approached her from behind and embraced her, she was seated with a script in her hands. Frequently, without even looking at his face, she would say: you’re late. But embracing her again, he would say: the camera is back. Then Gabriele looked at his profile, and looked again at that stack of papers which she slammed closed in a fit of pique. The things they could have said to each other or thrown in each other’s faces, and one looks to the left, the other to the right. The fact is that not even while practicing those first scenes could they manage to look fully at each other on the screen. Because during the first days of filming Gabriele seemed to get more involved alternating the poses that most characterized her in front of the camera, one after the other, than in trying to understand the character she was interpreting. I don’t know, maybe she said yes to a script she didn’t believe in, and although she looked for some point of affinity, the only thing that seemed obvious was that both the character and she had the same husband. Yes, yes, John, the same man who in that film, it would seem had more than one slip-up. And other than that there was very little that was remarkable, apart from some brush stroke in which the actress bore a point of lucidity as she said: he doesn’t need me for anything! She said, striking a very tragic pose. But not just that, the hours in which she was not filming she learned first-hand from an expert about that accelerated rhythm of steps, and she seized that dancer and let him take her, although the dancer was dealing with an actress who made him sweat and led him to tears, but he turned her into an expert. That’s what they paid him for, and everything was a question of practice, at least that was the impression that had to be transmitted to the viewers. In fact, the only thing that counted was that Gabriele follow the rhythm in step with her dancing partner, and lift her leg at the end. Of course, apart from this, the actress also had a quite notable part of the text. And so did John, who remained standing in that space against the wall, watching her and the dancer. In unison Gabriele was watching John as the dancer held her. And it was only when that expert very sensually directed her towards the actor, that one might say the dancer felt something like a punch in the eye. Although he stopped and listened, the voice of the actress exclaimed: it’s over for us, John. And now, as she pays, her gaze goes to that table, you will notice that she has been searching for a while and that it is not a good night. Some might say the woman could be her daughter. If she counted the fingers on both hands she could even be her granddaughter, and anyway she just met up with a friend and passed an ice cube in her mouth into the other girl’s mouth, and anyway, no matter what, she would never do anything with someone so much older. And the actress looks for someone else in this bar, until she gets tired, or until someone discovers that today she will pay for the drink of anyone who comes up to her.

 

Marianne said so many things in that school, and then suddenly one day she opened a textbook, and as she looked for the lesson, she realized that her cuffs were unbuttoned. Then, as she moved to fasten them, she asked the students who were closest to her where they were, what page were they on? She looked in the book until she found it, page one hundred and twenty-eight. Then, approaching the first row, she added in a low voice: is this where we left off? But she turned around, picked up a piece of chalk that fell through her fingers, and looking up, she put it back on the chalkboard. If she looked down she would see white powder on her hands, which she rubbed off with her thumb. She approached those young people and asked them simply: what am I doing here? A sentence that she repeats today, in this room where they are already opening the curtains. A great deal of light makes its way inside. A luminosity similar to that when she sat down during the weekends in that corner of the stairs and wrote letters. She often raised her glance from the paper and looked at that man, Arthur was his name, but he seemed to live with his back to her, and he worked on that motorcycle as if it were the only thing that mattered. But she kept looking at him, and if she heard that sound, the motor would malfunction for another day. So many things weren’t going well, it wasn’t hard to see that nothing worked anymore. She had to go over that letter that she wrote by hand and it was so often missing words, although she added them in as she could in the margins. Simultaneously she saw how the pen with which she was writing ran out of ink without any warning. And Marianne, as she brought a hand up under her chin, sensed that Arthur was seeing another woman. And if that were so, why didn’t he leave? She did not want to think about that; she preferred to think about those two children who had left the house and who hoped to create something similar to what she and her husband had started years ago. That genealogical tree that grew and continued extending out branches, generation after generation. Then, seated on that step where she saw everything from above, she was satisfied because she had fulfilled her mission. Her daughter came first, later the son that her husband so wanted, and in that order, one after the other. Marianne and her husband stayed in the house, sometimes spending entire days without speaking to each other. If he was seeing someone else, what kept him here? Now, however, that suffering seems to be a thing of the past. In its place are other things, as well as new faces, because here she is half undressed with an aide who is dressing her. She couldn’t say who it is. Someone in a big hurry comes in, cleans her, dries her, dresses her, and then she leaves.

 

Silvia opens her eyes in this matrimonial bed. Saturday. One of those days when her mental calendar tells her she doesn’t have the grandchildren. Instead there is this man who sleeps facing the other way. She could stay here a long time looking at the hair on her husband’s neck, which she will have to cut one of these days, although if she moves closer and breathes in the lotion smells stale, which takes her back to the starting point. Saturday. Silvia doesn’t have the grandchildren today, and although she turns over and yawns, she would have liked to have stayed here with this man who breathes in and out with a little snore from time to time, although lightly, intermittently. Just like this clock, as she listens to the seconds pass, one, another, and yet another, like the time lost in this house. On one hand it has flown, on the other, much still remains. Little by little the counting of the hands becomes superfluous; he sleeps, she thinks. No, he sleeps, she counts tiles. In a fit she grabs her underclothing from the bedside table, puts on her slippers and fastens her robe. Down deep, gravity keeps her occupied inside the house, she bends down to pick up a button that just fell off and goes in search of her sewing basket. The only thing that changes is that instead of sleeping, she threads the needle and stretches the thread out to stitch. After a while she lifts her gaze, looks at the empty space left by that chair, but how could I say it, no matter where she looks, for days this dining room has been a minefield.

 

Gabriele eats breakfast in the hotel’s café. Her hair is a little wavy, she gazes out one of the windows. Lifting her coffee cup she sees a line of parked cars, others stopped at the traffic lights, and people constantly passing by, from one side to the other, like she did yesterday when she returned on that same sidewalk, and the shadow of a street lamp outlined two silhouettes on the ground. Yesterday was yesterday, today her companion is just talking to hear himself speak. About the movies, oh, very well. This might go on for a while. She isn’t sure what she is hearing; the only thing that’s clear is that very soon she is going to get him out of her life. She doesn’t even remember how he got in. The young man keeps trying, counting this thirty-something-year difference, and when he finishes he brings the chair closer and wraps a blonde tress around his finger as he talks about short-term plans. He doesn’t seem to have had much motivation up until now, other than films of course, and he continues with: may I ask you a question? At the same time Gabriele gives him a very expressive look, as if she already knows the question before it is asked, while she crosses her hands on the table, and giving in, she lowers his arm, which seems detached from what he was thinking about saying. Can you get me a role? is what it seems like he just said. She looks at him steadily, then looks away, then looks back at him to say that she wouldn’t know how. She gives him the card of her most recent agent and looks out the window, just cars and more cars. Some come, others go, and she seems to say goodbye suddenly and she leaves.

 

But not Silvia, who is seated next to her husband while they eat breakfast, keeping each other company on this day off, although off wouldn’t be the right word. Today her husband is off work, that’s all. He works his entire life and sometimes is compensated in the form of milk, a sweet pastry, and orange juice. As he eats or drinks, he is aware that a large part of the savings in this house ends up being mashed up in his stomach. As she closes the orange juice bottle Silvia says that today is a splendid day. Then she remains in a thoughtful pose, as she stretches a hand out on the table, but away from her husband. An unimportant error, she could turn it the other way. It’s been a long time since the husband has looked at her; at this point he has seen her quite a lot. He might suddenly feel surprised by a hand approaching him, just that her fingers run over this tablecloth so slowly that it looks like torture. But Silvia’s hand is getting closer, and sooner or later it will come into port, that port that has a slew of boats in a deplorable state anchored there, as if for many years they had needed permits to return to sailing on the high seas. But look at the fingers of this woman that approach so slowly that one would need the help of a candle to see it properly. At the same time, as the husband helps her and takes her hand, that gesture might make her cry.

 

Gabriele returns to the large hotel room where she goes through closets and boxes, then she puts on her coat and closes the door from the outside, and she ends up leaving two suitcases at reception. A taxi is waiting for her outside, she gets in and gives him an address. Little by little the skyscrapers change to a series of suburban blocks. The actress looks back, she watches several children who are playing at the end of the street, and at the number of building facades that have been spray painted. The actress picks up her agenda again and looks at the careful handwriting with which John Faison wrote down his name, with a thick black line of ink. This is how it ends. Although that relationship continued after filming Magnolias and petals, just when they went on a trip and Gabriele was looking offshore, the breeze blew back her hair and her clothing, and she faked a smile, although things were becoming more serious. The air also agitated the actor’s blazer as his eyes reddened, but Gabriele had ceased to care about him. Because if she looked sidelong, the only thing she could add was: so many times I have caught you, and when it’s about me the only thing that occurs to you to say is don’t make a scene on this boat because the crew is watching us, you care so much what they say about us? You care so much that we have to keep faking our whole lives? Where is your head darling, a while ago I would have said this right into that damn camera, but look, in the end it was just one of many other than me, of course I was the only one willing to put up with this farce, but now it’s over. However, now is when Gabriele lifts her gaze and tells the driver to let her off here, so close to John, and at the same time so far away from him. She buys flowers at this stop, then she turns, breathes deeply, and grabs onto a large iron railing.

 

For a while Marianne has been walking from one end of the dining room to another, and it is obvious that today there are chairs missing. Or at least many are occupied, some by families who say a general greeting and go towards someone they know, the one they leave here, and oh, take that old man a shirt because that one isn’t his. Marianne goes from one place to another until she approaches the entryway. She isn’t the only one; others like her are waiting impatiently in front of the glass. Everything is similar to that school where she sometimes repeated this same scene with the youngest ones, some lived to go back home where there was a little snack awaiting them. No, the two things are not comparable, because here, as someone points out, they’ll come when we’re dead. And another agrees: we are worth more dead than alive. Marianne also listens to this, that, and the other thing until her breath starts to fog up this side of the window. She has gotten tired of waiting.

 

Gabriele has been looking for something for a while, although now her shiny shoes are very close together, behind her the leaves of the trees move gently, like a type of silent breeze. John, the actress whispers, lowering her sunglasses. Then she hastens towards the large marble slab, moves away a bunch of dry flowers, and leaves fresh ones in their place. Johnny, she repeats. She looks steadily at the hand-carved name, she looks at the next line down and reads that fateful date, which for her was a very normal day twelve years ago, as she was doing facial exercises behind some scenery, and as she was about to leave the set. As she was doing that someone approached her from behind and said into her ear that her second husband had been in a car accident. Then, although she continued acting that afternoon, and tried to pretend like nothing had happened, it had indeed happened. One could see it in her hand that clutched the armchair as she talked, or when she sat down, and suddenly she saw that first wave of the public approaching with a desperate look as she lifted a hand to her hair, which both in the afternoon and in the evening session was pulled back, but that night she passed her hand over her hair looking for the pins that held down every strand. That focus also blinded her, and her hand dropped to her forehead, and it progressively dropped down to her nose, she shakily bit a finger when it reached her mouth. Probably as she walked from one end to the set and back she had wanted to flee, or when she saw how the other actors looked at her, as if she had lost her way and had to orient herself again. But Gabriele went back to the script, and without help, as she went to sit back down in that velvety period dress, which was very confining and green, similar to the color of the grass growing in this cemetery, although now, some might say, it’s too late. John, the actress exclaims again, taking out a Kleenex that she wipes over the marble, then she sits cross-legged, very close to the photograph that she looks at carefully, although it wasn’t from those days. And the actress mourns in front of a picture, she says things like she would have wanted to hug him one more time, and to go over what had most united them, until one of them ended up mentioning that crossroads, after which their lives went in different directions. Now Gabriele adjusts her skirt, and sees from very far away the hubbub of some visitors who are leaving. When she looks back again, it seems that time has taken care of this wound. Her fingers pass tremblingly over a metal mark, they stop when she says he was impossibly handsome. Other than this, she also remembers several flashes at the entry to this cemetery. The next day she appeared dressed all in black on the front page of several newspapers. Then she picks up her purse, and it is more than likely that she has thought about leaving. Because no matter how many times she returns to this photograph, the only thing Gabriele might add is that her story ended up looking a lot like “While the beasts slept: I could never have you, and in the end I always end up losing you.”

 

Silvia and her husband return from their morning walk, which ended up in downtown, with so many stores and cafés they go by. As they return, if she dares to look up she will see that building where she used to enter and exit, climb and descend stairs, week after week, every Tuesday and Wednesday. She can follow the firm step of her husband, and at the same time keep her gaze absorbed until that building is behind them, because if she looks at any of the upper windows she might still sense a silhouette, a figure that is backlit, like a shadow behind the curtain. That’s how she remembers that floor, looking at the street in the same way and asking herself where all that was going. And if she turned her head towards her lover she didn’t ask anything else, because she sensed that he was starting to get tired of her. Of course she didn’t want to see it, and she took out a new shirt she had purchased for him from one of those window displays they are walking by today. Now she stops at one and her husband continues down the sidewalk, more lost and older than a few years ago, and it seems that her husband needs her, or that’s what Silvia thinks as she looks at prices, or as she looks at him out of the corner of her eye. And how her husband turns his head when he realizes that the other half is missing from his side, as if she must never get lost, not even on any old Saturday. But look, her husband returns to rescue her and takes her arm with an affectionate gesture. Hold me, the woman begged of a man she didn’t even know, and she also seemed like someone else, I don’t know, someone capable of spending a lot of money all at once in exchange for a simple: you’re welcome. Which she said with her gaze lowered, her mouth small and gesticulating with her arm, as if it weren’t important. But Silvia continues on this route, which always ends when she places her hand on the banister at her house.

 

Gabriele looks at leaves that are kicked up by the wind, as is her hair. She could spend hours like this. Yes, some day, but not now when she is still breathing in and out on her own; she closes her eyes and is filled with this cold air, similar to that breeze on the boat as they came into port. It had been a while since the actress had stopped looking at the sea, sometimes ferocious, others calm and monotonous from the cabin. And at one of those points John’s light eyes pled for another chance. Meanwhile, Gabriele pressed her lips together and gestured, closing her eyes, although there was nothing new, apart from John embracing her, and she only said: nothing you say will make me change my mind. And despite that she also hugged him, but more coldly than John expected, then it became clear that the actress had just thrown two years overboard, and she went below deck a little later accompanied by a woman. The actress told her driver from the back seat where to take her. The other woman added that they’d had a good time, but she wasn’t looking for anything other than some fun, then the actress became thoughtful, as if she were irritated or something. The other woman got out, Gabriele looked at the driver’s nape, then he turned around and asked if he should take her home. She was slow to reply, because John was sitting on a suitcase and waiting for another car. The driver, as he lit a cigarette and lowered the window, turned around to ask, and the actress hurried to open her door and asked John where they were taking him. When he got into her car, Gabriele received him with a smile and said: the more we draw this out, the more difficult it is, don’t you agree? John took off his hat, mussed his hair, and looking at her steadily, nodded yes with his head. The actress does the same, affirming it once and again, then she stands up, straightens her skirt, coat, picks up her purse, says his name once again, and walks towards the exit.

 

Silvia motions her husband to sit down and she drapes a towel over his shoulders. While the pot warms up to boil, she can cut his hair and shape his sideburns, although her husband isn’t aware of the scissors, and while she is busy combing and cutting his hair, he often lifts the robe, as if to pester her. As the scissors go through his grey hair, little by little the upper part that was curling is smoothed again, then she combs it and parts it, then she sweeps up the hair, using a dustpan. After a while she turns the stove off, and the husband reappears with a new look and his head damp just out of the shower, and he is waiting at the table, where Silvia places two steaming soup bowls. She sits down and there is an uncomfortable silence. Turn on the television, it seems like one of them says.
