Then they look at a simple screen, which today starts with breaking news, different every day, but despite that, repetitive. Just like the two spoons they raise before swallowing, then they return to the plate, and one or the other usually drinks some water, or he is dazed as he changes the channel and listens to some sentimental drivel. She also lifts her head. Have you heard? The man suddenly exclaims, they say there are people celebrating golden wedding anniversaries who love each other even more than they did on the first day. I’ve heard, she says. Of course we still have a few years to go, the husband says. Fortunately, Silvia replies, clearing away the plates and placemats, she changes the channel and sits back down. This position is also uncomfortable, too close to this man, as if in reality every corner of this house were closing in, and one day they both realized that the two of them fit into one square meter. Of course living like that the table wouldn’t stay straight, it would wobble on just two legs and the wood would fall into the woman’s lap, a good shake and the two plates would roll around, the water would overflow the pitcher, however they would remain connected by this unconnected thread of perpetual love, which seems to have gotten lost under the table, I don’t know, I would have to really look for it. Perhaps Silvia threw it into the trash without realizing it one day while she was sweeping, who knows. After all, love isn’t shaped like a heart they say, although the woman has seen quite a few in the shop windows, they put up I love you, and they must be soft to the touch, similar to that of a pillow where one could put their ear without expecting anything, not even for an internal mechanism to turn on, a blinking light that lights up the I love you, I love you, I love you, in a pounding silence until the battery says enough. Sometimes, however, love is briefer than the energy that causes a negative pole and a positive pole. That’s how it is, and if one awful day the heart gets broken, it is very likely that its interior is full of light plastic balls that fall willy-nilly to the floor. Love ends up being a tiny ball in the dustpan while the house is being straightened, and one might still be sitting in some corner. Why did you say fortunately? The husband asks later, while he peels the skin off a piece of fruit. And Silvia does the same thing, this skin that she peels off all in one piece, as if she had nothing to do other than that.

 

Marianne goes back to lie down in the dining room, which at certain times of the day is nearly deserted, because many people prefer a bed to a folding chair. Today there are also others who would prefer to be at home and not here. If people could get things just by asking, there would be those who would want to be young again, with strong legs and an athlete’s body, but not Marianne, she has already been on this long road of existence for a while, and it seems she has quite a realistic idea about it all. That once has been more than enough. Then there are those who indicate with their finger that life has flown by. And she also saw it like that some years ago, of course then that impression with which old age debuted with a big slice of cake was offset with accumulated experience, and she felt useless, and her advice was not always taken seriously. Now not even hunching her back in this chair or waking up could she add anything valuable that might interest anyone. Even so her eyes open slowly and she still does not know where she is. Neither by looking left or right can she remember this dining room where she had lunch today. There are so many things, like sensing if she is in good health, and look, it’s better this way. They often find her walking around on some floor, or standing at the window in some alcove, her fingers on the glass, and if someone calls to her she turns, aren’t you sweet?

 

Gabriele finishes a snack in an airport café, after a couple of seconds she folds the paper and takes the last bite. If anyone had told her this week would turn out like this, that she would spend so much time in the departure lounge, maybe she would have arranged things another way. She scrunches up a piece of paper and it seems like the entire week is squeezed with her hand. She picks up the bottle of water and takes a sip, she has already stopped thinking about that. Because now she marvels at the people who go through this place on a Saturday. Some are leaving, others have a layover, they come and go without cease, and suddenly she walks forward and mixes in with a crowd of people. Strange, and even so, it is so very little that she asks for. Sometimes she would like to go unnoticed and do what she could have done if she weren’t who she is. Of course maybe this sounds strange, she has been carrying this invisible burden of fame for a long time, like a halo around her face that distinguishes her from everyone else. Perhaps at this point she has become this, and she is no longer who she thought she was. But it doesn’t matter, others who have not risen so high have ended up discerning what they wanted and what they have become. There are even those who wanted everything except that. No, here Gabriele could digress and add that she does not see herself at that point, because it no longer bothers her if people recognize her, it would be the only thing she had gotten if everything were to shatter from one day to the next. Oh, the thing is that she doesn’t agree even on this, but having climbed so high and suddenly there was that white and immaculate descent in which she had decided to vanish off the map for a few days. And not to think about anything but skiing, and in that snow-covered mountain that yawed open before her. But down she went doing a type of zig-zag, as if just starting she were already thinking about slowing down, slowing down, only slowing down.

 

But now Gabriele goes back to the plane seat, although the airline is different and so is the journey. She stacks her last ticket into her agenda, assuming that a trip has ended although everything seems to be more disorderly than it was before, but at the same time she asks what will Marianne, Bill and Cassandra do. She even finds a moment for that idiot, the one who said today: can you get me a role? The actress seems to get involved with people who expect something in return. Nothing is free! Her eyes emphasize while the airplane takes off. At the same time she was accustomed to having that yearned-for role of protagonist as long as she could, just like Bill, John, or so many others. The actress was used to having suitors, so much so that when she was anxious to recover from the second divorce, nothing seemed to satisfy her. Not even that gardener who she fired one day without warning. Because she, unfortunately, had more time to water and prune the branches of those shrubs and plants that grew along the road. Practically up to the door of the house, but shortly thereafter all that vegetation started to wither. It was inevitable, one could see the actress’ state of mind from far away, there wasn’t even a need to go inside the gate. And the few who did, she received them all done up, is if that chaos were reorganized for a few hours while they taped her. Money isn’t everything, she said once. Love is like a balloon, she said in that interview, if you overinflate it, it will end up exploding in your face. If I haven’t had children yet, she exclaimed, why would I have them now? Cinema or theater, she answered that day, you make me choose between the only two things that have made me what I am. Would I like to be young again…? Gabriele, spoke again, does my age surprise you so much? That Mrs. Bates of the big screen no longer exists, sorry to disappoint you. We could talk and talk about Bates, but believe me, it is wearisome for me. Ah! She suddenly exclaimed, something I would like to do that I haven’t done yet, let me think, it’s possible that nothing will come to me because I’ve already done everything. Ha ha ha. Hee hee. And when the actress stood up, the journalist knew the interview was over. What I would like, the actress concluded, is to find an old friend of mine, I’ve never known how she was doing, we used to sew together, and there is no question that she sewed better than I, I was a complete failure in that area and in many others, are you aware that although you wanted to interview Mrs. Bates you ended up meeting Gabriele?

 

Look, the marble in the kitchen shines, so does each plate, each glass that is hidden behind the door of the upper cabinet, or the utensils that go back into the drawers in such an orderly fashion, spoons with spoons, forks with forks, and so on, until everything gets dirty again. Only this wood that Silvia hurriedly wipes with a cloth, taking with it any fingerprint, might indicate that this is an old kitchen that she keeps up day-to-day as best she can. She knows every last corner of this kitchen, especially those that have the greatest tendency to collect dirt, the most inaccessible ones, or those where you have to be careful when opening and closing drawers, on more than one occasion she has had to glue one, and the glue isn’t always strong enough and it detaches in just a few seconds. But pressing hard with her fingers sometimes it stays. Or this marble that has been eroding on the edges, and getting stained little by little. This is her drama, one day she makes a mistake, leaves some product she shouldn’t on top of the counter, and then she has to see that circle every day. Or one day she leaves a vase with plastic flowers, then the next day she waters the oily leaves under the tap. Also the wood on the closets and handles looks damaged, not to mention some point of the fingernail that gets twisted up when she trusts it and doesn’t wear gloves. Then, when her mission seems complete and she decides to sit down and file her nails, she will see how her little husband sleeps with the sound of the television in the background; she turns it off. There is something else, the hour hand of the wall clock is visibly trapped under the minute hand, tick, tock, tick, tick, tick, this is how the seconds pass in this house, similar to the sound of the woodworms inside the wood, the breathing of her husband while he sleeps, the beating of her heart as she observes, whatever.

 

There is someone up there looking at clouds who yawns; she takes off her mask and everything seems darker than before. Johnny, she whispered this morning before a tomb. And now everything indicates that she is thousands of kilometers away from where her second husband rests. Although she examines it and stops at that instant when Bill Engel had his face just a few centimeters away from her neck, she could still feel how that gallant got even closer until their breathing was intermingled, although hers seemed to get cut off momentarily. Gabriele joins the fingers of her hands together, then remembers what she said: they can see us I don’t know why she said it. She might have wished for that scene to have taken place many years earlier, probably during that party when the actress threw that drink on her rival’s dress, and in some corner of the stairs in that mansion, leaning up against the wall, with a smile on her face and her eyes half-closed, the thing is that just thinking it she would have said the same thing to Bill: they can see us. What does it matter now, they both had their chance, sequels are never very good. Gabriele knows it when they call her to film the sequel of Magnolias and petals, and she vocalized a resounding no before hanging up. Now she would even sign that document with her eye mask on and she would tell them they could count on her for a third, and she wouldn’t even ask if she had to get undressed again or not. Of course not. No one thinks of her anymore for nude scenes, others are now where she was years ago, rejecting scripts and going out with the most popular actors. And many have died before her, some much younger, and in the end she comes to the conclusion that drugs are a poor ally of this profession. But the actress thinks again with her eye mask on, opening her eyes from time to time as she remembers how she took off a pair of plastic gloves in that sink where she was washing Marianne’s hair. Was that Gabriele? The other said, looking dumbfounded into her daughter’s eyes, adding: was it? The actress’ eyes reddened as she stalled for time, as if throwing the gloves into the trash was a slow action, too slow to be real. As she placed her hand on the sink and opened the tap, it was obvious that Marianne was not going to return the letters to her because she didn’t remember her. She didn’t even remember herself. Neither did Silvia, who got into the guest bed, brought her head close to Gabriele’s cheek and stayed like that, more lost than ever she continued watching her grandson, but the little boy was stacking things up, and Gabriele also ended up looking towards him, who often picked up the wrong piece and used the smallest one as the base, but he kept stacking, starting from that initial error until it fell over. Of course! Like her when a little later she said I don’t know what, and then she heard: I like men. Ah, but the actress shook her head no and smiled at the ceiling. And how those pillars that held it up fell, they couldn’t even compete with that plastic game that fell with a clatter, and even though she very subtly lifted Silvia’s hands to her breasts, as if offering the best that she had, nothing but that was important any longer. Come with me, she said, throwing herself into the other one’s arms. But how can you ask that of me? Silvia exclaimed, ending the embrace. The pilot announces they are about to land, Gabriele cranes her neck and looks at the aerial view from a lower point, that point where the nearer buildings can now be seen clearly, as if the world were within reach of her hand, although it isn’t like that.

 

Marianne rummages through the boxes on her night stand, a bunch of disorderly pieces are starting to pile up on the bedspread. Even if she could, she would not change this diagram for where the course of life seems to have stopped, not even by pushing hard with her hands can she now change this direction. Although at the bottom of one box she finds a comb and she neatens her hair, the tines are on the opposite side of what she expects. However she gesticulates with her hand, as if she wanted to explain, or something like that. My dear students, it looks like she is saying at the moment at which she places this implement back on her skirt. Then she scratches her head with her fingers and a small drop of blood appears under her nail, which will dry while she watches it, each object, as if suddenly she intended to awaken from this deep sleep, in which nothing is as predicted.

 

As soon as she walks out of the airport she sees with a lightning glance the first parked cars, among which her driver approaches, he parks very close to the actress, then he gets out and puts her two suitcases in the trunk. Meanwhile, she gets into the back seat, this place where Gabriele has spent years constantly giving directions, sometimes without pause, directions and directions that this man must have memorized, other times having to slow down and turn halfway around, because nothing that the actress said should be taken literally. But now she is seated on the right and she leaves her coat and purse on the left side. Nevertheless she crosses her legs and tells the driver not to start the car yet. When he looks back at the actress, she sees signs of fatigue in him, as if the years that he has been taking her here and there suddenly appeared today. Perhaps it’s because she has not looked at his face for a long time, although they have had a professional relationship for many years. The actress drops her gaze and looks to the other side, you can see how this seat on the left side where she leaves things, has been empty for a long time. So long that she would have to make a big effort to try and remember who the last person that sat there was, and to be precise it could have been one of the agents. Yes, some of them spoke in code and never said anything worthwhile, as if the actress were a has-been, and suddenly the agent would talk about moving heaven and earth. But in the end, nothing. How long ago was that occupied and awakened mind, always from the studio to home, from the outside where they were filming to the hotel, from the opening to the press conference. And if one adds to all of this the fact that the driver has seen her at her pinnacle, and also at her lowest point, then one would have to ask the actress why she keeps getting into the back of this car. The driver asks where she wants to go, with the accumulated patience of a life spent serving others. And she breathes deeply and says: can you think of somewhere better than home? As she is saying this she glimpses movement at the window, as yet another part of a journey ends as the man leaves the highway and gets onto a thoroughfare, or even closer, drives through this tranquil area of residences, which seem to compete with each other, even the vegetation, and from time to time one breaks from the classicism and looks futuristic, worthy of some draftsman at his height. Because if the driver continues driving down streets until he parks the car, you will see how this actress picks up her coat, suitcase, and invites him in to have a drink. Because here, on the other side of the gate, it has been a long time since silence has been a good companion.

 

The actress places a bottle and two glasses on the classical-style table, elaborate, as are the crystal goblets with which Gabriele intends to toast repeatedly as if there were no tomorrow. Ah, but this man cannot live in the moment the way she can. He has a family and things to take care of. Nevertheless, if you were to think only about today, it is more than likely that he would end up with his head bent down in some pub, and in fact, I assure you that this will happen. But now he has the chance to talk with Mrs. Bates, who pays him cash every month, and sometimes on the third day of the current month, she doesn’t even remember. Increasingly, several days go by without her calling the driver, and naturally he has to find new income. Then on the day he least expects it, Gabriele dials his number and he must go, cancel other commitments, with everything that goes along with cancelling trips and having others irritated with him. Although sometimes when the actress gets into the car, she puts a hand on the front seat and says: dear Frank, take me here or there. Some of these trips she could go by metro, like anyone else would. But of course Mrs. Bates isn’t just anyone. A celebrity, the pundits say. Let’s toast to today! The actress exclaims again, who again fills up the glass of her private driver, and someone who she should have let go years ago, because she doesn’t need him because she hardly goes anywhere, but despite this, some place always comes into his head that makes him feel more useful than he really seems to be. Although today he looks like an older man, one of those who have seen from the shadows how some are fortunate, and if he had a chance to really look at it, this driver would not change the little that he has for this. But now Gabriele refills two glasses and asks him: are you one of the ones who think I left Ronald Parks hanging in the middle of a party? As she says this the actress raises the glass and takes little sips. He threw me out, she says seconds later. A powerful man, Mr. Parks, the actress says as she nods her head, he was capable of dialing those telephone numbers that used to hire me one by one. Silence. In fact, the actress continues while she leans back, everything that I had fought for throughout my artistic career, with effort and tenacity, in one day that director managed to destroy it all because of a matter of skirts. I should have guessed, she adds as she crosses her arms, that that woman who wouldn’t stop sending flowers to my dressing room was Mrs. Parks, but I didn’t. I didn’t guess! She says again without pause, if you knew how many times I’ve asked myself what she saw in me, why so many bouquets and cards if later, when she had shown what she felt for me, she wasn’t even capable of meeting me in a hotel, at home, she said to me, my husband is almost never there, and what a mess I got myself into, but do you know anyone who thinks with their head when they are in love? After her husband caught us, the actress says more slowly, I didn’t want to keep seeing her, she was involved with everything that was going on around me and that was no good. Love, lack of love, Gabriele concludes, tapping her nails on the table, we could talk until after midnight about them both, and we could only come to one conclusion, love through the spoken word is a sign of age, and I wear that badly.

 

Silvia wipes a rag over the bookcase where the dust accumulates day after day, it seems impossible that this dust could keep her on her toes her whole life, so frequently she still feels like she doesn’t get to all the corners. Look at the upper shelf in the dining room, she needs the help of a chair to access it, and then she forgets the rag and brings down a dusty album where she can see the lives of others more clearly than her own. And if, among this mess of clippings from newspapers and magazines, she hadn’t cut the photo off so low, she could now re-read the headlines. What does it matter, today there is an image that looks like it says Gabriele Bates is relaxing during a break from filming, while she talks with the director in a friendly pose. Or this other one where Mrs. Bates and her first husband are shopping hand in hand. If Silvia turns the page, she will very quickly realize that there are still quite a few images of the actress with Bill. She grows tired of it, closes the album, because if she thought looking at this would make her happy… one senses that this was not the case. I am sorry. For me, she can keep wiping off the dust and looking at more albums, for example the one of her wedding with that man who often passes by her and doesn’t even stop. Later he says he couldn’t live without her. A real romantic. One of those who buys bouquets of flowers with a few canned words on a note when it is already too late: your husband, daughter and grandchildren miss you. But now, how would it be now, who thinks of her? Apart from, apart from I don’t know what neighbor, as she whisks eggs, following some recipe in front of her that was given to her a long time ago. Silvia takes a chair and climbs up on it, wipes with the rag, gets down, and when she pushes the chair away and returns it to its place, the only thing that occurs to her is to turn on the oven and take another pot down from the cupboard.

 

Gabriele says goodbye to the driver shaking his hand. Something else that has never happened, this gratitude from one to the other. And now the actress unpacks her suitcase by herself, and does what she used to do when she got home after filming or from some distant voyage. Of course then she had an assistant and all the clothing she took out was taken to the dry cleaners first thing in the morning, including those pieces that had not even been worn. Those were other times. Gabriele has been hanging up her clothing herself for a long time in this walk-in closet that is immensely large now that you can see inside it, full of dresses she has not worn for years, some have gone out of style, others probably don’t fit her anymore, in any case any dress photographed by the press could never be worn again in public. A rich-people thing, an oddity of the artist, like those funny things she did when she received an award and walked backwards, so that she could stay on that streak. The telephone often rang in this house as if professional and personal calls had decided to call on the same day and at the same time. But now there are many makeup brushes on this dressing table where there are all types of powder from compacts, she traces a dark line on her eyelids, gives a little life to her lips, makes a part in the middle of her hair and pulls it back.

 

Silvia used to say so many things to her husband. An entire life jawing. He misses that. At a bad moment one day he said he was sick of so much chatter, and now there is always silence. Sometimes he feels like apologizing if he has done something to bother her, whatever it is, in truth, he asks forgiveness from the bottom of his heart, he didn’t mean to upset her. And Silvia, seated very close to this man, with an apron that she now smoothes with her hand, has the gaze of someone who is absent, as if she didn’t understand correctly what she just heard. Did the husband say forgive me? And she looks towards this man who was wearing a wedding suit that is now gone, but even so, at those moments, she saw him as daring and modern. Silvia ends up smiling at her husband. Then she looks the other way again. Her husband was useful for so many things, an entire life trying to arrange and fix things, when she could have bought something new. And she misses that clutter of keys, screwdrivers and hammers. At a bad moment one day she told him she was sick of how instead of fixing things, he made them even worse. Like now, the woman thinks as she goes back to her chair.

 

Gabriele buckles on a pair of high heels. Later she puts on one of those dresses she keeps for special occasions. She hesitates and finally decides on one that is sky blue with a plunging neckline that she covers with a matching jacket. She looks at herself from the side, straight on, and moves closer to the mirror when she says: winter of seventy-eight, we met at the same ski area in the Alps, ha ha, I see you have a good memory, it was spectacular how your son Tom went down the mountain, like an arrow, then you will also agree that one afternoon we met up on the same floor of the hotel, and rubbing your hands together you asked me, is your heat working? Gabriele buttons the jacket and looking at herself from the side says: my dear, how can I forget an unforgettable night? And the actress runs her fingers over her neckline to the last button. The unforgettable is definitively more than lost behind those mountains. But if you have a moment, Gabriele says looking at herself in the mirror, I would like to talk to you about something, no, it isn’t of the utmost importance, of course I can wait. And the actress puts a hand on her back and says: during that trip you told me that if I ever needed your help. That if I ever, she repeats when she leaves the room and goes down the hallway, one of her hands in constant contact with walls, doors, even in open spaces her hand remains raised, which she keeps like that until she puts it into her only pocket and where, to her surprise, she finds a bottle of sleeping pills. She is very close to the pool where the water rests tranquilly and makes little luminous moving points on the ceiling. She suddenly bends over, the reflection of her face silently breaks in the water, looking like a mosaic.

 

Water, so much water, just like this sink where the water is draining out with scraps of food and other crap. Then the television that offers those watching, waves full of fury slamming against the rocks, and it looks like a scene of more disquiet than what Silvia and her husband had imagined. In the worst case they didn’t imagine anything. The television relaxes them, so much so that it often has a hypnotic effect, just twenty steps separates them from the bed, but here they are fine, with this blanket that they sleepily share. There are nights when Silvia would like to stay like this much longer, with her gaze unfocused, as if it weren’t already because of this movie about pirates, and just looking at a bookcase she will ask what she has done with her life. She seems to have given added value to this accumulation of years that passes like a wound that weeps without ever closing. Days and days, an incessantly long intoxication in which she has scrubbed and ironed every day of the calendar. Her husband has also driven kilometers and kilometers along a closed road, if he had done so on the outside he would have gone around the world I don’t know how many times, although here, as you see him, he has never even seen the ocean. Silvia says while she watches the television, that Gabriele said that looking from the coast you can see that the earth is round. And what else did she say? Her husband asks curiously. A lot of things, the woman answers, because different from me she spends hours and hours reading, and she makes up stories, like one about when low clouds were passing everything seemed accessible, she said that anyone who could see figures through those spongy clouds would see their unfulfilled dreams come true, but what do I know, I haven’t ever looked at the sky, and anyway, what do clouds have in common with dreams? Silvia remains silent, she looks up as if just by looking at the ceiling a sentence would come to her. Look up high, Gabriele said to her, lifting her chin in that park, and look at how many low clouds we have today. Although you will tell me why she had to look at that, the only thing that she saw was how the other woman took off her sunglasses, and suddenly her gaze was more open than ever. And she explained many of those stories to us in class, Silvia says, later, when we were in the workshop she liked to tell us the role she would be playing in the theater, but in the end it wasn’t to be, Marianne wasn’t able to see her talent, she gave her the worst role she had, ah, but then she took our daughter and the entire elementary school to the movies, she just said how well she knew her and how much she had believed in her. Silvia looks at her husband as she starts to say: I would like… At the same moment her husband, intrigued: what would you like? For Gabriele not to have come back, she concludes.

 

And not too far from this city Marianne kisses a rosary, as if suddenly she has become religious. She would like to die, one can see her gaze is fatigued as she swallows a pill and leaves a bit of water in the glass. Her mouth feels dry and she presses her lips together and turns on her side in the bed, as if she were furious for being yet another night in this type of prison where this afternoon they put someone else into the room with her, as if she had asked for company or something. No, Marianne was fine as she was. And the other one, from the moment she opened her suitcase hasn’t stopped taking over space. In just a few hours this room has become a little monument to her family, there are photographs everywhere. But now, almost in the dark, Marianne closes her eyes and forgets this scenario. If she could snap her fingers and appear there, somewhere, anyplace would do, and she could breathe in fresh air, like a breeze that touches her face and fills her with life, as if looking up she could count the first drops. One, two, three, four. And those two children tumbling down the mountain. One, two, three, up to four tumbles one after the other. Or the cars parked very close to the highway, she counted at least four. Like the four days that Arthur promised to visit them, and he didn’t show up, but she counted minutes, one, two, three, four minutes that her husband was late at that lunch where everyone was seated at the table except her. Because she would have waited longer for him, I don’t know, perhaps she would still be there, just in case he decided to return today.

 

If today isn’t the day, it is more than likely that the actress won’t try it again. She has had two opportunities in life, and both times she ended up taking this step back that returns her to life, if this is what one could call life. A good life, many people would say. And those people, those people who wanted to be in her place, would swim from end to end in her pool and ask why Gabriele let go all the employees she had in this house. Many of them, climbing up the ladder, would here and now ask for some sweet cocktail with which they could taste how well some people live, and why they have only gotten crumbs. Money isn’t everything, the actress said once. And some of her most fervent admirers ceased to pay attention to her for uttering a phrase like that. As for her, money seems to be framed on the wall, many pictures which, if more money doesn’t start coming in, she will have to take off the wall and sell, one by one. It is difficult to decide which one will go first, which one last, and if she will be around to see the sale of the last one. In any case there are her heirs, if she has not changed her will, who are the children of a first cousin, very nice people who have been watching how the actress successfully reached menopause, and now it’s just a matter of waiting. Today, tomorrow, the day after, and people often wait longer than they expect to, time that is tacked on, like a piece of cloth on the cuff of a sleeve, because what one can see here in the form of paintings, her heirs could easily trade in for more cattle. But back to what I was saying, Gabriele ends up sitting in this armchair, her face somewhat crestfallen, as if she were looking in box after box for a script that she puts on the table, The long winter of Catherina Miller, one of those stories you can stay up half the night reading. No, the actress just turns the pages of this text that she should know by heart, every line and anything that would be indicated later, in the middle of filming, like this handwritten note: “would you be so kind as to have dinner with me tonight? Fondly, Bill.”

 

Look how placidly this man faces his alarm at this point when it looks like everything is falling, oh joy! He would like to share this with Silvia, because you never know when his half-erect member will cease to pester him from under the sheet, finding the body of the sleeping woman who he turns over to face him while he insinuates that all that he can offer her is this. Something that is not purchased with money and that she might be excited to receive after being awakened and having him force himself on her. Although she accepts it and even asks for something she desires in a low voice, which is for him to run his hand lightly over her body, touching her skin in such a way that just thinking about his hand makes her tremble all over. And everything he does, he thinks only of her. What is it she says? That if she wants something take note of it, because anyone who wants something must first earn it, the woman says, this same woman who today might get excited with very little. And her husband’s hand is already on its way down to her stomach, which is rough, but not insensitive. A hand that Silvia redirects, showing him what he hasn’t touched yet, until the husband understands how to thrill her body and where, where has this singular creature been until now? So many questions with no answer, and perhaps the only thing to add is that he should have spent more time learning every inch of her, centimeter by centimeter, now it’s a little late. Although Silvia wraps an arm around him and brings his head to her breasts, while she hopes to be desired and be reciprocated like never before, although she feels a little nip that she wasn’t expecting. No, not like that, gentler, like a butterfly that lands on a finger and folds its wings. And folds its wings! The woman exclaims, returning her husband’s hand to his erect member as she gets up. Not far from the room, Silvia picks up her toothbrush and squeezes the tube of toothpaste, then she brushes her teeth as if she hadn’t thought of that. Because if she were to look at life in the bed that is reflected in the mirror, she should throw confetti.

 

Good morning Marianne, says her roommate, who gets dressed and raises her socks to her knees by herself, wets her hair, and parts her hair herself. Yes, yes, apparently simple things, but here they tend to be valued and magnified, like having a sharp mind, like those who are always saying things like: Marianne, remind me that I still have to talk about this or that. Because if they look at each other, her mind isn’t far from a chalkboard on which she can write and write, and that damn illness always comes and erases it. And sometimes the eraser is so full of chalk that even after wiping it over the chalkboard there are still traces of some letters. Whatever the case, it is a disease that has not yet decided to use a damp cloth. In this room Marianne often watches the agile hands of the aide who showers her, dresses her, and brushes her hair in just a few minutes. And she would like to thank her, even if this worker didn’t help her every month. She is grateful, says thank you for dressing her, for serving her breakfast, thank you for being here. Don’t thank her for being here, her roommate says, who is now eating breakfast in front of her on this radiant morning. Very sunny, her roommate says, ideal for taking a walk. And so many things, some of them escape Marianne, they pass in front of her with a strange musicality: have you ever been married? And the woman nods her head yes, as if she thought about it for a good while. I am a widow, Marianne concludes. Like me, the other one says, who puts a hand in her pocket, and with the other, caresses the woman’s arm.

 

Gabriele is in the kitchen in a satiny bathrobe and white slippers, while she cooks something in a skillet, then with the help of a spatula she takes two eggs from the pan and she sets a place on a small table with two white chairs. The picture window treats her to a view thick with trees, although looking closely there is less light than she would like. Gabriele Bates is seated with one leg crossed over the other, putting bread on her plate, drinking orange juice, and a little later she passes her fingers through her slightly messy hair, which nonetheless was treasured during the last week. In such quiet moments there is only the sound of the spoon stirring the coffee, and she feels some nostalgia for having wasted some days far from here, to later come back to the starting point. But she doesn’t eat eggs every day. No, that is something from this trip. Like she also does not look out the picture window every morning searching for some thread of light between the trees, nor is she reflected in the glass until she is certain that these crows’ feet around her eyes will continue to sharpen the area, and opening up even farther, as if this tap of life never had enough. But she could stretch out that area with her thumb and index finger, then her upper lip, or seconds later when she reaches her neck, something that has deteriorated over the years, which has been covered over with adhesive tape at the nape whenever some agent made their routine visit, a surprise visit, or whatever you want to call it. And look, the one who is most surprised is her. She puts both hands on the table, and for the first time she has it in her mind that she won’t continue acting.

 

Every day, every day, this woman says to Marianne, I greeted my husband with a kiss on the nose, then my sister said oh, now I understand why you have so many children, seven in total, one died at birth, but the rest left the nest, as it should be. Now children are spending more time with their parents, when it shouldn’t be like that, says this woman as she walks with Marianne down this hallway that ends at the entry door. When someone is waiting behind the door, they should say who is waiting, and if they come, how long they are staying, the woman says who talks and talks and takes Marianne outside. That is life, she says moments later. And so many things, how walking is very healthy, or that she has been drinking tea her whole life, and she won’t stop doing it here although the doctors prohibit it, although she has to go out to buy it, and she doesn’t really know where there is an open store. No, nor does Marianne, who walks, does not listen, and at some point along the way stops, as if this woman was an angel on earth.

 

Silvia takes out a pair of her husband’s pants which she lays on the bed, also a shirt and a blazer. She has been doing this for her husband a long time, and if it were necessary she could even dress him herself. They had said in sickness and in health, and they both assented with their heads, as if that wedding were so disquieting, and once that moment arrived they were not awaiting that solemn part. Although now her husband is sitting on the bed, and Silvia pulls up her stockings, or watches how he puts on his shirt. She has seen this many times, she thinks as she passes her hand over the bedspread in this bed without wrinkles, where this bedspread has been for at least thirty years, if a day. Silvia’s somewhat rounded back continues what she was doing, she was going to fasten her shoes. The man pulls up his trousers, passes a belt through the belt loops, and buttons his shirt up to the last button. Simultaneously she puts on a skirt that today matches that blue blouse that her husband has already seen on some occasion, half hidden between pieces of clothing, between skirts and trousers. You will understand how this woman squanders his money. One hundred percent natural silk, it says on the label that he folds down from her nape, as if he would drown her in a fit.

 

Let’s sit here, Marianne’s roommate says. Then a waitress comes out from behind the bar. A Sunday like any other, the woman replies, just like in the city, of course Sundays are days of rest, although I used to live in the country, and the country never rests, you have to take care of the cattle every day, if you go half a day without feeding the cattle you’ll see how they complain, the beasts are always begging. And they sleep, Marianne says, who looks at the table and another glass of milk appears. Exactly, the other says, they also sleep, if the animals are grateful, especially the ones being fattened, those are the ones that eat and eat, and when they least expect it, they’re on their way to the slaughterhouse, otherwise we wouldn’t be able to eat! And this woman talks, she has said her name on repeated occasions but she can’t remember it. But here, as you can see, there are people who spend time in the only bar that is open, and some park the car in front of the residence while the whole family tumbles out.

 

Silvia and her husband walk on a pedestrian mall. Then they continue on the sidewalk, or they change streets and go downtown. Then they go in this door that doesn’t seem to have any control about who enters and who exits, because it is always open. They both go up the elevator, she smoothes his lapel, then looks to the other side. He opens the door and pushes the button for this floor where for one day they are the guests. Mother, Father, the daughter says inviting them to come in. Then she escapes to the kitchen, while she peels onions, because now is when the children go back to being the grandparents’. Hello beautiful, Silvia says to this creature who, if she moves an arm, already has what she wanted. And what a pretty bracelet! The grandmother exclaims. And this little one as well, who crawls or stands, according to the visit. But he crawls in the direction of the woman, the one who brings baby food from the store, the one from the park and the slide. Although he is little, he knows her better than he knows his own mother, the one who wipes a rag over her eyes and continues cutting onions. Sometimes Silvia passes through the dining room, and not finding a single free space, ends up in the kitchen. And she helps more than she should. She gets down plates, stacks them up and hears: is everything okay? Everything is fine, she answers, without looking away from what she was doing. Then she gets down glasses, gets the utensils from the drawer, and sees the envelope that her daughter places in her apron. Silvia could open it and say it is a lot of money. The daughter also adds she told her she would pay her for her time, and she just did. I don’t know where my head is, Silvia exclaims suddenly, I left the desserts at home. We left the pie in the oven, the woman repeats as she fastens her coat.

 

Gabriele was reading when the telephone rang. That happened a while ago and she is still talking to Bill, the one of: would you be so kind as to have dinner with me tonight? That’s how it started, even when it seemed like it was over and done with, it was consolidated during that week, but now they just chat, like two old friends who listen to each other, to anything the other one wants to say, like: Cassandra and I would like to visit you. I hope you will, Gabriele says with a smile. When we come there might be three of us, we have started with the adoption process. What wonderful news! The actress exclaims. Yes, he says, the only issue is that they will think we are too old. Well I think you look very good, the actress says. The one who many years ago read a note in a dressing room, would you be so kind as to have dinner with me tonight? She only remembers that while she read it she furrowed her brow, as if to say: should I? And on another piece of paper she responded with a simple: it depends on where you’re going to take me to eat. And Bill had to spend a lot, of course that wasn’t important, only what they said and how they said it was important. I can imagine, she said, that taking me to such a fancy place you will expect something of me in return. I don’t, Bill answered, I have already tasted your kisses, you kiss like a fly. And the actress raised an eyebrow as she said: if you want to kiss me today, don’t go there, you might end up with wine on your face. The other actor said: does it change so much because I haven’t kissed you yet? And Gabriele, crushing the napkin in her hands: Mr. Bill Engel you irritate me, thinking everyone is after you, and I have the highest hurdle to overcome. How high is it, exactly? The actor asked, full of curiosity. Here, she said seated and lifting her arm. One and a half meters off the floor? He said with a smile. Then Gabriele stood up and said: here. And Bill looked at her and shook his head no, because if he had thrown himself into it that day he would have brought down her arm energetically and without a doubt he would have kissed her.

 

I slept in a chair in the hospital, the woman says, for two months, oh Marianne, what else could I do? My husband was dying, the doctors didn’t have any hope, and he was suffering, suffering like hell he said, and I suffered for him, and he only thought about the cattle, one day I got really upset, I rented a car, went back to the village and sold them one after the other, of course I didn’t tell him, but I had paid someone to take care of them and he was starting to sleep, they gave him stronger and stronger medications, sometimes he spent the whole day sleeping, then he would wake up and I would fear that he wanted to return to the village, I was losing him and I was just afraid that he would want to go back home, that he would ask me with that weakened voice to let him die at home, but then I saw how he slept and thought, perhaps he won’t wake up again, and look, everything that I feared ended up happening, we took him home, but all he wanted to do was get up and say goodbye to those beasts, tomorrow, I told him, and I laid down by his side, and the next morning they called me to the door to tell me that they had found my husband dead in the fields, what do you think of that? I would like another tea, the woman says to the waitress.

 

Gabriele hangs up the telephone and looks back at a page, the one marked in the book. Down deep, no matter how much time goes by, the actress always goes back to the classics, she has a predilection for old books, which she collects, some in a terrible state of repair. Some see value in something that others sell to antique dealers at an abysmal price, then the seller of old things puts another price on it, and in the end it is the auction that has control. Two thousand, this actress said in one. Thirty thousand, she said in another. Two hundred and fifty thousand dollars was her limit. And here is the painting hanging on the wall. Sometimes she should have kept her mouth shut, and done what she usually did in a situation like this: let someone else win. But here, as you see, she did not think of that. Consequently there are alarms on the door and on every window. She is not the only one. In this area any intruder could be stopped by the police and accusations made by the owners. A zone that is constantly patrolled. And on the other side of the river there is terrible poverty. Gabriele often lifts her gaze from her reading, which today seems heavier than usual, as if she remembered that this book had captivated her one day and now it doesn’t seem the same. Nothing is as it was before. She returns to the line where she left off, passes her finger down the page, but at the same time she feels incapable of continuing.

 

Look how the little ones play in the park, the woman starts talking to Marianne again, the little ones fly kites, they enjoy anything, later they grow up and have to start making decisions, childhood passes quickly, and youth, what you haven’t done by the time you’re twenty, don’t expect to do it when you’re seventy, and I married young, I would like to think that everything that I didn’t do was because I didn’t have to do it, but if only I could still parachute, they say the view from the air is fantastic, oh, but then if the children found out they would think I was crazy! Two of my children, the woman says, started to get concerned about me when I was widowed, they were afraid that I would die of sadness if I stayed alone, they believed I would die of sadness, and what affected me the most was having them in front of me discussing which one of them was going to take me into their house, those rotten kids, I had them one after the other with chicken pox with fevers of forty degrees, and I spent entire nights without sleeping a wink, and when they had to make an important decision, they were incapable of making it without fighting. I ended up making it for them, she adds as she watches Marianne, and here you have me. I could tell you a lot of stories, the woman concludes, but now they are waiting for us to eat.

 

Gabriele has a particular liking for some neighbors two streets up, who she often visits on Sundays, and they fire up the barbecue and eat outdoors. The actress usually arrives with a bottle under her arm, the owner takes it and puts it on the table. Meanwhile, the actress looks at the meat, and if it needs it, she flips it over. A simple couple, like her, she sees toddlers over there. The owner comes up to her and she greets him, and as she does this he brings a hand up to her back. We haven’t seen you all week, the husband says. And the wife repeats this later. Lovely people, as is this actress, who sits between two children who affectionately squeeze in towards her. But now everyone eats. Then one of them brings up their fork, and looking at the actress, asks: and this smile? What smile, Gabriele says as if she were distracted. The one on your face, they say to her. Look, one says to the other, she’s turning red, as if we didn’t know you, let me guess, you’re in love. In love with life, the actress says lifting her glass. Then she drinks, and remembers something she once said to a reporter: don’t think for a moment that because we just finished the longest interview I’ve ever done that you know any better than you did when you sat down with me, no one knows Gabriele Bates, and that’s how it will be until I die, then someone else will appear, book in hand, they will explain from beginning to end who Mrs. Bates really was, and who Gabriele really was, how she acted in public and how she acted in private, and then do you know what will happen? Mrs. Bates will start making headlines with all sorts of anecdotes, a life that was never boring, but even so, never hinting what she was really like. Gabriele puts her glass on the table and says: today I got up, ate breakfast, and I saw clearly that at sixty-two years of age, no plastic surgery in mind, I have decided to retire.

 

But where were you? Marianne’s daughter says, we were all worried, we looked for you everywhere. You did? Marianne says, sitting at the table and putting a napkin on her blouse. Then there’s the other one, the daughter, who takes the friend’s chair and sits down. Now the friend will have to find somewhere else to sit, although she looks, watch in one hand, spoon in the other. Mother, can I help you with your soup? Mother, let’s see, you’re going to spill on yourself. Mother, have you missed me? And the grandchildren? And my husband? Marianne thinks about this one. Does he still sing that what was it called? The woman asks, looking again at her daughter. And which in a question like the preceding one obtains a response: I think she looks worse, don’t you think? Bread crumbs, Marianne says, and otherwise, sit in the armchair, it never fails. The daughter raises a hand to the woman’s hair: Mother, I don’t understand what you’re saying. No? The old woman says, making a gesture of disgust between spoonfuls. And now she starts with the second course, this veal that the daughter cuts into tiny pieces, as if she felt more useful doing this. Mother, Mother! The daughter says. What do you want? The other says. If you need more water, I will pour more water for you. Then, as she drinks, everyone watches her. But now she chews slowly, lifts her gaze, observes that woman in the back, who is eating like her, and when she isn’t, she talks with other people, like she is also doing.

 

That, I don’t yet know, Gabriele says as they start on dessert. Travel, the actress says, I’ve already traveled a lot, and I could write my memoirs, but I wouldn’t want to, it’s better like it is, everything as it appears, every instant as it is, with one part in the light and part in the shade, although right now there isn’t much shade, or air, and a slight movement of the leaves is perceived, and everything seems to be stopped like in a photograph, in front of me two old friends who have hands under their chins as if showing excessive interest in everything that I say, because Gabriele always speaks correctly, and if she makes a mistake no one can tell her, I am accustomed to feeling observed in this way, to being admired like an object that is within reach of very few, a woman who comes and goes, and not always with her head high, many mistakes made, and no one seems to be brave enough to tell me to my face, sometimes I wish I hadn’t been so successful, or hadn’t learned so much, because now I would still have some dreams to fulfill… instead, I would like to buy more flowers and create a real garden. Somewhere I can spend every afternoon without haste, until I see where the smallest life starts. I would like to have seen less and to still feel like leaving the house, but instead, I want to shut myself in and have some young person take care of me. Someone to fall madly in love with me. Someone who I can say to every day that they are wasting time with an old woman. I still haven’t decided if it would be a man or a woman. There comes a point where I can’t bear men, and there’s a point at which women absolutely can’t stand me. But there is the memory of every nude body I have been with. I would like to be ninety years old and still have a sharp mind, I would like to get to ninety looking like I do now. But look, could you stop nodding? What is reasonable about old age?

 

Mother, the daughter says to Marianne in her room, I have brought you socks and new stockings, and this skirt that we’re leaving, a coat for if you go out into the garden, and some very comfortable slippers. Mother, the daughter says, we’re leaving now, your grandchildren want to go out with their friends, if it were up to me I would stay longer, but you know that the driver is in charge. Go then, Marianne says, who watches her roommate hanging up photos on the wall. The daughter starts to cry, although neither Marianne nor her roommate could say why. She opens the door, goes down the elevator, pushes open the exit door, gets into the car, and thinks that she has already forgotten her. And there is Marianne, who picks up a sock, a stocking, tightens her hand and says: don’t die before me. And the one who is hanging a picture lowers it again, opens the nightstand, puts it into a drawer and says: who can say what will happen?

 

Gabriele has returned home, she gathers up branches and digs a shovel into the earth. She tries to make a row to handle as many flowers as could be planted here, probably many. As you can guess, she wasn’t born a gardener, and her parents never watered anything, a family with little understanding of things of the earth. Much was left undone when they departed, as if her family was a family of fugitives. And how they left, they didn’t even say where, but they survived wherever they went. Sometimes it seemed like Gabriele’s father was throwing everything overboard, later he would throw himself into drinking and give the impression that everything that happened to them wasn’t the least bit important, as if emotions were something fleeting, something of women, and his wife, it would seem, was also not worth much, they threw themselves off a cliff. I don’t know why she is thinking about this now, as she digs and finishes this row, or goes to the faucet and washes her hands. She would like to think of nothing, and for life to flow like she has said she thought it would. The afternoon light fades, the shade slowly takes over the open space, the air agitates the leaves on the closest trees, the birds fly by and move away slowly, she can close her eyes and let herself get drunk on this strange sensation where her heart sets the pace, also the sweat that runs down her neck as she strains her head upwards, like when they told her she had to keep her back straight for many hours, and she could remain like that today, until her body loses balance, but she can stay like this for a long time while her mind bestows images, words, anything on her. Now we’re alone. We are alone. Alone. And she keeps her back straight while she stretches, opens her eyes, and sees Silvia on the other side of the fence.

 

◆ ◆ ◆ ◆ ◆

2023-03-01 00:39:44 INFO - pageSourceWithSettingsEnd+++++++++++++++++++++++++ 2023-03-01 00:39:45 INFO - Step 15 - I restart app 2023-03-01 00:40:09 INFO - Step 16 - I open Books 2023-03-01 00:40:09 INFO - Button 'tabBooks' :: Clicking 2023-03-01 00:40:10 INFO - Step 17 - Open EBOOK book with READ action button and 'bookInfo' bookInfo on books screen 2023-03-01 00:40:12 INFO - Label 'lblBookName' :: Clicking 2023-03-01 00:40:13 INFO - Step 18 - Click READ action button on book details screen 2023-03-01 00:40:14 INFO - Button 'Read' :: Clicking 2023-03-01 00:40:15 INFO - Step 19 - Font size 'fontSize' is increased 2023-03-01 00:40:15 INFO - context - NATIVE_APP 2023-03-01 00:40:16 INFO - context - NATIVE_APP 2023-03-01 00:40:17 INFO - context - NATIVE_APP 2023-03-01 00:40:17 INFO - context - WEBVIEW_org.thepalaceproject.palace 2023-03-01 00:40:19 INFO - pageSourceWithSettingsStart+++++++++++++++++++++++++ 2023-03-01 00:40:19 INFO - Lowering Clouds

Lowering Clouds

Núria Añó

 

Marianne was seated in the armchair when the doorbell rang. Her feet were slower than usual, as if it were a great effort to wear footwear and today she no longer remembers that they put shoes on her, which look polished; or that three-inch heel, how many steps has she taken in those shoes, and look, now she can’t even remember how she got here, also the pointy toe is really mashing her toes together. And the way she looks at the time, she looks at the wall clock for a good while and gets the impression that the woman they just told to come in has arrived late. She wouldn’t say it, but forty years without seeing her… Was their communication really that bad? And what on earth is happening now, with the visitor wasting time greeting members of the family, as if she didn’t remember her, Marianne thinks while she curves her back and pulls down the hem of her skirt. The actress simultaneously reaches a hand towards the door, and anyone can see that she is about to go in, but they hold her up on the other side of the window. How many words could be spared between them, how was the trip, you look so good, and the palm of her hand sliding down the window towards the knob; she will soon have to decide whether or not to enter. The daughter has already blundered, which Marianne didn’t want, what do I know, here is a seated woman who had never expected to hear the name of that disease, but now what’s done is done. Not even by embracing it can she fight that word, but how would she say it, the arm of the actress is again very close to the glass, although they make her turn around with their silliness, that of course she deserved the statuette for I don’t know what movie. C’est la vie, she says, her hand approaching the knob. And if I had the chance to be with the most desired men, why hide it, says the daughter of this old woman, as if seeing them still sends a shiver down my spine. Is that true? Not anymore, the actress says. Then she opens the door and finds the other woman standing. It’s about time, Marianne exclaims, taking a step forward. It was more than time, Gabriele finishes with a hug.

 

And why don’t you stay here? Marianne’s daughter says, I could get a room ready for you right now. Gabriele sits down in a nook when she hears this, the daughter’s slim hand just like Marianne’s, as if the words are too much, but in their place her eyes are very attentive, she sees something shiny on each side, although the actress breaks the moment and inclines her neck slightly to say that she already has a room in a hotel. In a hotel, and why, the daughter exclaims, when you could stay here as many days as you like! Gabriele looks again at the hand that is just like Marianne’s, lifts it towards her face and kisses it. I’m only going to stay three days, the actress says, turning around again as if she will always have things to do; projects, after all. Yes, Marianne has an idea of what that means. The projects all ran together for her, like the steam of a pressure cooker, something seen and unseen. And the daughter speaks into thin air: I’ll get a room ready for you. And the grandson: shit, give her yours. And the daughter: you want to be quiet? Can’t you see she can hear us? The actress crosses her legs to the side to affirm that she has already paid for the hotel. Arthur died, Marianne says suddenly. I know, Gabriele says, but that was years ago. Yes, the other woman says in a low voice. She looks towards the window and sighs. If you see the light on in the garage, go back in a little while, tell Arthur to come in, he spends his life in there and we hardly see him. Gabriele then rises from the sofa and adds: but you don’t live in that house now. I don’t? the other woman asks.

 

Sunday afternoon Gabriele says, as she goes down the stairs and looks up. Then she hesitates for a moment on the last step, thinking about whether or not to go back to the hotel. Nevertheless she decides to take the next step in the opposite direction. Her expression is a bit grim, as if she were expecting better weather. Probably because this city holds intense memories, as if the passing of the years could erase so many gray days from the calendar, and now she has to raise the lapels of her overcoat and breathe through her nose while she looks at this city that is so different from her adolescent memories. I don’t know… where she was expecting houses there are probably very tall buildings, and where she expected there to be open space, everything is stacked on top of each other, as if there weren’t already enough blandness here. Of course she is not an architect, nor did she want to be; she became an actress instead of an architect, but now you’re going to see where she’s headed. Or maybe she is surprised walking around or waiting at some streetlight, as if this city isn’t the one she grew up in, and there are just so many new streets where that city once was. She lifts a hand and signals a taxi, what a mess, she hadn’t planned on getting lost here.

 

Gabriele! Sílvia says as soon as she opens the door; she often speaks in code and now just emphasizes it in the middle of the hug: you should have let me know earlier. The actress enters and hangs her overcoat on the coat rack, then heads towards the living room and rubs her hands together saying: if I had planned it I wouldn’t have come. Silvia invites her to sit down so she does, and they are very close to each other at this table where they both cross their arms, one with her face a little downcast. What’s going on? Gabriele asks, lifting her chin. Nothing, the other one says. She gets up and takes two glasses out of the cupboard and slowly pours them each a liqueur. That’s not what it looks like, the actress says, lowering her gaze. And Silvia cries, I don’t know, she decides to cry at a bad moment, although she finds a handkerchief in her apron, then she exclaims: I thought you had forgotten about me! Oh! And why wouldn’t you forget? You have a great life, and you’ve gotten everything you wanted, married and divorced twice, and with such men! They run circles around mine, but everything here is exactly the same, like a clock that has stopped, you did well getting out of here, I told you once and I’ll repeat it again: here, there is nothing but misery. That’s not how I remember it, the actress says after a sip. Silvia sketches a slight smile, then drinks. Better? Gabriele asks. Better, Silvia says. Are you okay, comfortable, do you want something to eat? As the actress sits down and shakes her head no, what is strange is that as she does this Silvia captures the non-verbal communication, but I was lost with the first movement of her head. But where was I going, ah yes, Silvia’s husband appears, and look who’s here at the table! He’s never believed in miracles, but now, now he would fall right over if he weren’t already hugging Gabriele. He is a little nervous, he didn’t expect to find this woman from the silver screen right here, this woman who his wife said had once lived here, three or four streets away from where he had lived! And the way things are, well, he didn’t remember her from when she was young, but she has immortalized herself in I don’t know what movie, he’ll know, but on more than one occasion she served as the image for him to jerk off to while his wife was sleeping. Or maybe his wife wasn’t sleeping? Anyway, a man who was closely connected to Gabriele, one could say. Intimately, although today he looks at her from a hand’s breadth away and he could weep at the cruelty of the passage of time. Not even with a knife and cruel spite could such an effect be achieved. Silvia, the husband says, stay where you are and I’ll take care of dinner. And what can be guessed from her surprised look, as if she can’t believe what she just heard, and she has to see with her own eyes how he takes things out of cupboards, and how, looking to the other side, the actress takes her overcoat from the hook and asks: so, tomorrow? Silvia also thinks for a few seconds, and says: call me mid-morning.

 

Why didn’t Gabriele stay for dinner? The husband asks Silvia. Does she seem to you like someone who could be served four half-raw vegetables and a burned cutlet? She scoffs. Plus one can’t just appear out of nowhere on a Sunday as if it’s nothing and expect to be invited to dinner. You see what we had for today, Silvia starts in again, she’ll be used to eating, I don’t know, caviar, and we’re an old married couple that has to get by on one salary, if you had just let me work when I could have, then we’d have two pensions now, and we could have invited Gabriele out to eat at some restaurant, I’ve already thought about it, but if we’d done that it would make the rest of this month difficult. Let’s see how much is left, she’ll stay a bit although for her sake I hope it won’t be long, I wouldn’t want it to look like her friend is squandering every day that passes! But no doubt she has an idea when she sees me trundling the grandchildren all over the place! I don’t know why she’s come, the first thing she’d say to me if she were to pull back the curtain and see an old woman waving from the closest telephone booth, at the exact moment I raised my hand in greeting I would say: Earth! Swallow me up! And then she goes and says that she came to visit Marianne, when it was really me that was her friend, she was older than us, Marianne was already engaged while we were still in school. But if she loves Marianne, she concludes while she brings a glass of water to her lips, well, her corpse is here.

 

Gabriele is in her hotel room, telephone in hand. Whoever it is doesn’t answer. Well then, she will just have to continue with what she was doing, a dinner she decides to have here, in the intimacy of two overhead lights and the incessant colors that flicker on the television. No matter how many times she blinks her eyes, she is in the middle of a city where she has not set foot for a very long time. An eternity. Answer, answer, she whispers into the mouthpiece again. And finally that voice appears, which she tries to keep on the line without speaking a word. I’ll give you the telephone number of the hotel, the actress says to her agent, with a card in her hands. I’ll leave the fax number in reception if you can’t reach me. When are you going to buy a cell phone? The young man cuts her off. Would you take me more seriously if I had one? Gabriele rebukes him. And how it makes me feel when you get annoyed. The actress brings the device up to her ear, as if there is something important to be said, while at the same time she looks up, and she might wish to hear so many things, but now she just listens, yes and no, it depends. A modern woman, Gabriele, who turns her gaze towards the bed and makes a moue of disgust, as if she had a lot of contained rage, but instead, she stretches out the cord while she laughs coquettishly, and the look is gone from her face completely. Then she lowers a foot from the bed, grabs her agenda from her purse and looks at the mountain of white pages that is her future. White, like the blouse that someone puts on over Marianne's head. Or like the cotton with which Silvia removes two layers of nail polish with acetone, to stall for time. Time that passes for Marianne as she is laid out in a bed that isn’t hers, to which she is attached with a tether so that she can’t escape, fall, or even move. A woman who, in a lucid moment, remembers the flurry she is causing, and cries. As Gabriele does now.

 

You can already hear the early-morning shift of the grandchildren, the grandson, the youngest, who doesn’t have to do anything but arrive and already he is squealing with happiness. His nine-year-old sister is with him, she’s old enough to take care of him; the school isn’t so far away. Silvia lets them in. They eat breakfast at the house of these very kind grandparents who always have something interesting to tell them. Silvia’s daughter also comes in and mentions that she got an appointment with the doctor for the little boy, he has a cough and is stuffed up, tell them to be sure and examine him thoroughly, well! I’m going to be late. Silvia and her husband know this “well!” all too well. One leaves. The rest stay at the table where they finish another bottle of milk. I’m going, the other two say getting up from their chairs. What does it matter, the grandchildren fill their hearts with youth, like spring after a long hibernation, when the arms extend out like those of a scarecrow to embrace them. Like Silvia’s husband would stretch his arms around Gabriele’s waist if she would let him embrace her. A man who already had a chance yesterday and today he could collect the little pieces with the broom and the dust pan. No, he isn’t Gabriele’s type. Nor could his hands that do hard work every day contend with the satiny hands of the woman who yesterday had a hand on his shoulder, yes, while she gave him a kiss on each cheek. No one would believe him if he told everyone at work today. Anyway, he would have to say things like: yes, man, Gabriele Bates! The one who was in this and that film, come on, how can I not remember the name, tell me the name of one, no, an older one, to see if between us we can remember. And that’s how it would be if someone came up with it, mouth ajar, as if that title sounded to them like an old movie. Say another one. Come on now, let’s go, the work won’t wait. Not even arriving home exhausted does he rest. There is always another shift after his. The fact is that one day as Silvia’s husband is lifting kilos and kilos of weight with the machine, he suddenly realizes that he prefers this to being at home.

 

Gabriele arrives at the park that Silvia had indicated, and when she sees her, lifts her arm in the distance, even though she is alone here, with a grandson who gets out of the baby stroller. The actress approaches the boy and says something to him with a smile, but from all appearances it seems like the little one is just waiting for them to let him loose and he marches towards a swing set. Gabriele settles onto a bench. She is wearing a blouse of an intense blue with a type of decorated cuff at the wrists, under a light-colored coat. Neither her baggy pants nor her shoes with their little heel can match anything the other woman is wearing, Silvia thinks while she presses her fingers against her purse. This is the youngest, she adds like a good grandmother, but my daughter has two more, one who is nine who is very studious, and the oldest who is seventeen and shameless, they had the three children with several years in between, as if they didn’t dare do it all at once, and as you can see I have to take care of this one all day as a precaution, because in fact I already took him to day care but when they saw he was coughing they told me to take him, as if I didn’t have anything else to do, but anyway I’m happy you’re here. So am I, Gabriele answers, it is truly wonderful that you have grandchildren, I couldn't have children, there was always some project or another, and later, when everything was less hectic I couldn’t have them anymore; it was too late.

 

After a little while the grandson runs back to his grandmother’s arms and she takes the moment to shake out his scarf. He pats her skirt. Gabriele caresses his head, and again he goes towards the slide, from there he climbs the ladder slowly, as if by stepping firmly he can’t fall. A boy immune to falls, because the grandmother shouts: Be careful son. Then Silvia turns towards Gabriele and suddenly asks in a low voice: You don’t wear earrings? The response comes in the same tone of voice: When have you ever seen me wear earrings? I don’t know, Silvia says, and turns to look at her purse, but just once more she lifts her chin. Look up high, she says to her, and look at how many low clouds we have today… Why? Silvia seems to cut her off, as if she’d just blundered. Can you believe that? The actress adds while she takes off her sunglasses and puts them in their case.

 

Silvia examines the case a little later and says: A few years ago I had a lover, my God, you’re the first person I’ve told this to. Simultaneously her gaze goes to Gabriele and she speaks again: I didn’t go looking, it was something that just happened, he had the strongest muscles I’ve ever seen in my life and he embraced me wildly, although we didn’t talk, we only screamed, we scratched each other, we bit each other, sex with him was strange, frivolous at first, ardent until the end, but since things are as things are it ended, I still can’t explain it to myself, I climbed to the last floor of his building, rang the doorbell again and again, but he didn’t answer. Gabriele answers: He must have left. Yes, I thought about it, we are in a city that people just pass through, people come and people go.

 

Leave me be, Marianne gesticulates with a hand to her daughter. If she’d said it, it wasn’t long ago, I don’t know exactly when, because now her notion of time is a factor of little weight, light as the flight of a feather, but she said: An entire life dressing myself and putting on my own make-up, I just hope you never find yourself in a situation such as mine, it is humiliating to have to see my own daughter pulling up my socks, I can still do it myself! And the daughter leaves, a little lost, while she listens to reproaches: her and her rushing around, because let me tell you, I have all the time in the world and then some, yes, now that I’m in this house she doesn’t let me do anything, not even watch the grandchildren, she said that one fell while I was watching them and everyone is furious with me, although he’s running around constantly from one end of the hall to the other, all day long using the fucking walker, when it’s been so long since he took his first step, yes, I do like children—that’s why I became a teacher! But I detest these children, good morning I say to them every day, and look, they don’t even turn around, one day I heard one of them say, ‘what granny, you haven’t died yet?’ And he says to me: I slept in that room until you came, now I can’t even go in because the smell of urine impregnates the wallpaper, how disgusting, get control of yourself you old bat, I had finally gotten my own space, and now I’ve had to go back and sleep with my siblings. Let me tell you child, one day you will miss me, life is fragile like a piece of porcelain, one day you’ll kick a ball, it will bounce off the wall and miss smashing into the pitcher, and then the boy gets angry, runs out of the house slamming the door because he doesn’t like the answer, if he’d wanted to break the pitcher he could have done it by doing it more directly, delicately pushing it with his index finger until he starts to bend over from his ass, and believe you me, I hope that someone pushes me out of the bed, it doesn’t matter who does it, and when it happens, every day I pack a suitcase, I walk down the hall and I trust that one day no one will be here… Outside the window Marianne sees a feather rising and falling, and she raises a hand to the glass and says: … the door.

 

Damn! Isn’t that woman coming towards us Mrs. Bates, the actress from While the beasts slept, Margaritas and petals, The long winter of Catherina Miller?, says someone getting out of a bus carrying retirees. Yes, it looks like it, says another one approaching the window. Oh, but she looks just the same! What in the world is she doing here? And what was that she used to say, do you remember Anna? “If you’re going to kiss me, don’t ask me if I’m back to stay, do it, just do it.” Would you say that for us, Mrs. Bates? I’m sorry to disappoint you, Gabriele responds, that scene caused more than one surprise with my fans. Would you believe that sometimes they didn’t even let me finish before they were all over me. And not surprisingly, one of the women says, unlike us you are aging very well, have you had any work done? I read in a magazine that you’d had bags removed and that you’d had something, I don’t know what, Botox, around your eyes, to me you look beautiful, there is no reason to be embarrassed, I would do it too if I could.
