The magazines, Gabriele adds, only write stupid things, and now, if you’ll excuse me. Of course, but at least let us take a picture with you, someone call the driver over!

 

Don’t you think we should take a trip one of these days? Silvia says to her husband while she serves him a piece of meat and sits down at the table. To travel someday like Gabriele does, the woman continues, to travel purely on a whim, you know that we are at a risky age, or maybe you don’t realize that something could happen to us any day? The husband picks up the pitcher and pours water into his glass, and insinuates: and who would pay me, you? And there it is, exclaims Silvia, I was just saying that we have spent our whole life here, and here there are nothing but obligations, when it isn’t this it’s that, you with your work, me taking care of the house, we have to clear things up with our daughter, oh so you think it’s free for us to feed her children! And how they eat, it’s lucky that the oldest eats lunch at school, but as you see the youngest doesn’t like the baby food I make for him, I have to go to the store to get a little jar of food and who knows what it’s made of, remember the baby’s mother ate everything, at first she spit it out, but I insisted with the spoon until she stuck it all the way in there, and now she tells me that the boy only eats food from the store, and she doesn’t leave us any little jars of baby food, she leaves us this picky boy with a cough that chokes him, if I had wanted more children I would have had them, but at this point who would want another child? Well, the husband says looking at his watch, it’s nearly time to go back and I still have to change.

 

Gabriele is lying down on the hotel bed, her eyes closed, the window ajar, a fresh breeze moving the curtains slightly. The actress dreams, in a manner of speaking. There are people who have dreamed about her. Or people who for years have not hesitated one second to submerge themselves into that body and to be her. They would have even paid with money, and in the worst cases with their souls, if they’d had them, the very stupid ones. Perhaps some of them find some point of connection in their dreams, yes, in that state of silence in which Gabriele turns over and pulls the sheets up a little. Women who would have the letter V in mind, and with their legs in that position, they would wait impatiently for one of Bates’ ex-husbands. There are even some who would think of the letter M, a more daring position, and they would fight to be in a place as precise as on bottom or on top, as long as they could squeeze those men and… let the money flow freely! Money isn’t everything, Gabriele said on one occasion. And perhaps one day Silvia will look over that album of news clippings about her, although, frankly, I don’t advise it. The past is the past. Gabriele is present. She seems to have already opened her eyes and stretched, but she is a little disconcerted, as if at first she doesn’t remember where she is. As she begins to remember her face gets moist and she washes it with some product out of her cosmetic bag. She picks up her toothbrush. She rinses out her mouth and looks at the fine skin around her eyes; she makes a moue with her mouth, as if she were going to get even closer to the mirror. She is still rinsing when she says: do it, just do it! Mob of idiots, she says after spitting and hanging the towel back up on the rack.

 

Mother, every time I see you you’re looking out the window, shall I turn on the television for you? No, says Marianne. A response that is good for almost everything, the old woman thinks while she tries to unfasten the buttons on the cuff of her blouse. Are you going to let me help you shower? No. Can I help you get dressed? No. Do you like vegetables? No. Do you want to lie down for a while? No. Do you feel like telling me anything? No. Not even anything about my father? No. Or Gabriele? No. Shall I buy you a notebook with addition and subtraction equations so you can practice? No. Shall I bring you some skeins of wool? No. Would you rather crochet? No. Will you come with me to pick up the children? No. So that’s how it is! The daughter exclaims, hurt. She crosses her arms and thinks. This middle-aged woman has a need to talk with someone, at a café or the hairdresser, and how long has it been since she’s been anywhere like that? A woman tied to this house. A flower in the middle of a barren wilderness. She might wither before her time, she thinks disgustedly. That’s how it is, she does everything for everyone else, and what does she get in return? Just a little savings account from which she can’t even withdraw money without the presence of the account holder. And she will get even less in a future that may or may not be too distant, when her brother, who lives in some town nearby, won’t hesitate to be a mooch. Once all is said and done, the inheritance, if there is one, will be for them both, that’s what I say. The daughter doesn’t want to think about that right now. The daughter goes out on the balcony and approaches the railing. It seems that Gabriele is later than usual, she tells her mother as she comes back in, I would have liked to have seen her again, but look what time it is already, I have to go.

 

Just a moment ago Silvia had a free hand, but now the granddaughter grabs onto it. A hand she presses when they go by the bakery. I want a croissant, she says to the grandmother with a very tiny moue she puts on when she wants something. Who can resist? A hand she can press again at any time as they walk and force her to go back. I want that pen that has a light. The granddaughter is a good student, Silvia says to the shopkeeper. How nice, the shopkeeper responds indifferently when giving her the change. Her hand is usually on the stroller, if they leave her be. But the granddaughter is very aware of the grandmother, and it is not always possible. She sees there are crumbs and traces of sugar on the tips of her fingers. Grandmother, this little girl who has already had communion, says sometimes. Grandmother. And Silvia moves her hand to her purse as if looking for a handkerchief by touch. The granddaughter only demands a little attention. Grandmother! Will you test me on the multiplication table? And how much is this times that? An outing that becomes long with this haphazard melody, the woman thinks, who in her day-to-day uses a calculator. A woman who in general spends more time subtracting than adding, and dividing rather than multiplying. She really only thinks about multiplying abstractly when she purchases a scratch card and wins. And do you see? Even the greyish particles that remain on the coin say the same thing; she’s already gone back to subtracting. For a moment Silvia distracts her granddaughter away from the numbers and makes her look towards the other end of the street. Look who’s there! She exclaims with surprise. Although in reality she did not say that to the granddaughter. She is also unaware of what she said. Perhaps it was her heart that said it, this palpitating organ in a gush of life, yes, she pulls down the outer corner of her eye and erases every other visible thing. But the creature is already returning, she stretches it out from her coat with great effort: I want this computer. She just barely manages not to let go of her hand. Just when Gabriele and Marianne cross to the other side of the street. What could Silvia say, that the tear that did not fall has witnessed happiness and misery at the same time?

 

The light in this dining room where Marianne used to live a long time ago now illuminates Gabriele’s hair, which is a very light brown, similar to her natural color when her curled highlights shined on the screen. But now she distances herself from those thoughts and decides to sit on Arthur’s rocking chair. A rocking chair that Marianne observes at length with a slight movement of her head, while the actress says that she passed the best years of her youth in this city. Also that this morning she wanted to go by the house where she used to live, here’s your little surprise; the thing is, she prefers to remember how it was, although now she just says that the glass in the window to her room is broken, and she is sorry to find it in such a deplorable state. Yes, Marianne explained to her once that this had happened after they left, and a family with children lived there for a decade, maybe more; now she doesn’t remember that family’s last name. It doesn’t matter, houses age when no one inhabits them, just like with Marianne, something dusty with the smell of being closed up, although there might still be some picture album to show. Gabriele turns the page and looks at each photograph, and suddenly lifts her gaze and asks: What’s with Arthur? He didn’t like being photographed? Arthur, Marianne rebukes, was a lovely person. Then the actress glances quickly at the rest of the photographs and settles herself in the armchair, although suddenly she can’t find the right place, and consequently is uncomfortable and finds another position, but it doesn’t matter how she moves or scoots back, and she finally says: happiness is so subjective, isn’t it? And Marianne is as well, who at that moment is looking at a specific photo, and now she gives the impression that she is about to add something. Or maybe she is thinking about whether or not she should respond to this insolent comment pointed at her like a bullet, but she is lost in the middle of this dining room, and later, as she closes the album, she forgets it.

 

Silvia’s daughter parks the car in front of the entryway. The grandchildren were falling asleep on the sofa. Silvia covers her hair with a towel and leaves the bathroom in a bathrobe while they explain to her that the youngest has a bit of fever. Both put their hands on the child’s forehead. Yes, he has a fever, the mother says, who then picks the boy up in her arms and carries him out. Towards the car, like the granddaughter, who sits in the back seat by herself. Silvia goes down to the entryway, puts a hand against the car’s window and asks her daughter to roll it down. Then she says: tomorrow I have things to do and I won’t be able to help, I’m going to meet up with Gabriele. What? The other asks. Well yes, Silvia says again, tomorrow I won’t be able to take care of them. The daughter gets out of the car, one hand on the door, and exclaims: but what on earth are you saying to me, that I have to take a day off because you’re going to be with Gabriele? Do you think it’s easy for me to leave my children with the only person I trust? Not that watching them should bother you, at the company we have work piled on top of us, listen, listen! I’ll pay you for the time you spend with them. But how can you tell me now to go find a babysitter! She says sorrowfully, if they charge me more for a few hours than I make in a day, and my children weren’t even a bother, I would remind you that it was you who said you’d help me because you said you weren’t feeling happy, well, at least that’s what you said, that you were bored and depressed and the grandchildren made you happy.

 

Gabriele returns to her room. Just fifteen minutes ago she said goodbye to Marianne, who she left with an entire family. She could have stayed to eat dinner; they had invited her. But what difference does it make? Here she can change her clothes while waiting for dinner. Fruit, she said at reception. And things are what they are, now they’re ringing at the door, she is just now feeling hungry. By all accounts, it made her remember how Marianne returned to that floor and took an astonished look around before everyone else there, asking tremulously what she was doing there, if Arthur wasn’t waiting for her at home. But now Gabriele sits on the bed, brings one of these red fruits close to her mouth, which she bites into slowly while she turns on the TV and changes the channel. Until she tires of it. Although look, how suddenly her right eyebrow lifts and she instinctively leaves the remote on the bedspread, because there is Bill, Gabriele’s first husband, with a galloping horse in a movie from the sixties. How she instinctively picks up the remote again, raises the volume, and nips one of those exquisite cherries which, looked at closely, didn’t have a pit? Did it? And Gabriele chokes while she pounds on her chest and coughs before this Bill who dismounts from his horse and shouts: Ben, Ben, where have you gotten off to? A woman who, before she dies, gets down on her knees and remains on all fours. On her face one can see a rivulet of saliva and she is turning blue, while someone is pounding on her back with their fists. You called me, sir? Simultaneously Gabriele’s arms suddenly lose their equilibrium and they slide to the foot of the bed, where her body is when she gives in, and… that’s all! Although she has always been stronger than she looked, and she weakly lifts an arm up. Appearances are deceiving, and she does a lot that is deceptive, but in this scenario it is her, no one is going to take the protagonist role away from her. Even though her body is fragile and she falls back onto the bedspread, which was clean and now is a complete mess. There are some who see a tunnel when they die. There are also those who are lucky and hear the sound of life. That damn little cherry pit could have been the death of me!

 

Mother? Oh my, she’s sleeping on the sofa! And what time is it? We have to do something, Marianne’s daughter says, turning towards her husband, things can’t go on like this, today I’ve called two nursing homes but they haven’t been able to confirm if there is a free space for this month. Well, I already explained it to you and you didn’t pay attention, the husband insists, the nursing homes will bankrupt you, at least here we get her retirement money. Look, you don’t know anything, the daughter retorts, you just call in at home, today I nearly hit her, but suddenly I saw myself with my hand raised and I thought: what on earth am I doing? You don’t see how things are because she said she wanted to get all dressed up, you can’t even imagine, you already have me taking off the clothes that were used for the first time at the little one’s baptism, all rumpled up and needing to be ironed, I don’t know, there is a time for everything, there are times when I talk to you and it’s like the same old thing, ah, and wait, her friend tells me that if I hadn’t told her, she wouldn’t have noticed, she says they talk, but I ask myself: what can my mother possibly talk about? If it isn’t about one of us, I don’t explain it, it should be the other one who talks, that Gabriele is in such a big hurry, of course she is younger than mother, but you know, today I think she looks older, don’t you?

 

The actress goes to her agenda, and then dials a telephone number. Mrs. Bates speaking, she says, while she adjusts the telephone. Then someone calls Bill. Yes, yes, I’ll wait, Gabriele responds. She takes the moment to leave the telephone on the bed, drink a bit of water and clear her throat. And she asks: Hello? She opens a bottle of lotion that she spreads on her legs. Then it seems that they have called her, and she brings the telephone close to her ear again: yes? After the first few polite greetings between them she exclaims: dearest Bill, how could we throw away what we had?

 

Silvia is lying on her back when she stops her husband’s hand, the fingers she knows so well. It is this wordless communication that causes slow and monotonous waves along her spine, and which always end where they end. Where everything begins. Where her husband gets tired and turns her over face up. She could rebel with a kick, or by biting his shoulder while he moves on top of her. She could even try to strangle him when he tries to enter her. But Silvia breathes, like something that appears from the back of her mind. A very cerebral woman who reads women’s magazines in which she turns the pages and always comes across an idea about basic clothing that, as a rule, every woman should have in her closet, even if she is sixty-two years old and the collection is unfinished, or she might even turn another page and find some lipstick with the exact color that would match how she is currently breathing, her breathing that she controls like a diver who has been underwater for several minutes, and she cries out briefly, which will of course wake the neighbors, who will go back to sleep, as the man will do as soon as he finishes and turns over. This also won’t last her whole life, the husband will find this out. Although while he penetrates her he asks her if she is faking. Is there anything worse than being inside someone who doesn’t even seem to be there? Doing it with an inflatable doll, one of the husband’s friends might say with a beer in his hand. Or they laugh at you, his boss says to a stranger in a stopped elevator. Make sure you have the pen in your hand when they hand you the autographed photo, Gabriele would resume, they could bind me, hang me, they can look at me however they wish, but they will never have me.

 

Did you know that Gabriele was at a station with a suitcase on her lap when everything began? One witness said that when he saw her from the other platform, he looked at her from the side with his arms crossed. It would seem that after observing her for a good while the man wanted to approach her, and when he was a hand’s width from her face, this caused her to look to the other side, he raised her chin, he asked her to stand up, and at that moment the young girl began to run. The man remembers that he followed her for quite a ways, then later found her again in some city square where she had her back to him, and that was where he said it. A manager? She responded without even turning around. Dear John STOP I have found a possible Catherina Miller for you STOP She does not want to be an actress STOP Prepare the straightjacket STOP Affectionately STOP Dani Capozzi. A man who held her prisoner for a couple of months, forcing her to practice footing from the time the sun came up, and receiving training from sunup to sundown to remove her expression of: is this for real? Yes, in summary, that look when they served her another of those strict diets after an awful day, at those times she would not have said that she wanted to rise so high. And they refined everything about her, don’t point with your knife when you talk to me, it is very tacky. Or that script that she read every afternoon, because the only thing that could be said is that it seemed that mountain of paper was the only thing that kept her there, her eyes shining from everything that this character Catherina had in common with her, with the only difference being that Catherina was rich. Yes, well, that changed everything, but at the same time she identified with that character who was frightened by conventionality, so much so that she knew the role had to be hers no matter what. And so, crossing her fingers, she went to the studio with her manager, still carrying the suitcase, the same one from two months earlier, just in case everything was a disaster. A suitcase that they also used so that Gabriele could carry it onto the set, which she did with difficulty, because someone took the trouble to add papers, folders, scripts. Although as she carried it she thought the weight of just being there was the real burden, and that she should have said no. She’d already said no to some things; why not this one? It doesn’t matter, she would leave the suitcase on the floor, she would approach the railing and she would see how, from that screen, red clouds did not appear no matter how long she waited for them, I don’t know, maybe she was hoping to find something similar to what was in the text, but just then she breathed deeply and with a questioning expression said: a magnificent sunset, don’t you think Mr. Miller? And Bill turned towards her, as if he couldn’t speak, because he was an actor who was quite contained, although he had a vein in his forehead that showed his feelings. He was an actor who, according to notes in the script, every afternoon looked out at the horizon and saw in the sunset what his life could have been with Catherina, but who left nevertheless, and furthermore had left without even saying goodbye. Despite that, here she was again, with that blonde hair blowing in the breeze from the fan that the technicians had turned on. There are too many words. But the actor’s vein was apparent, as was Gabriele’s hand as she placed it on the railing, as if to say: Catherina would touch this railing, and even among all this enveloping darkness, he would know she had come back. Also, when he addressed her, as if he didn’t understand, it’s you, you’re back to… And she, looking at the actor and thinking, what am I doing standing in front of Bill Engel, what am I doing in front of this actor, somebody pinch me and tell me I’m dreaming. She in turn struck an insolent pose, looking at the camera in such a way that her glance crossed the irises of those watching, while she turned to face this man and she said: if you’re going to kiss me, don’t ask me if I’m back to stay, do it, just do it.” Gabriele was not even able to finish saying her lines. The manager remembered that an eon ago that scene had not been filmed, because no one who was there had any confidence in her, they said she was still very young, that her look was too angelical, but I saw her in that station waiting for a train, she would have been able to drag that train towards her, she could have done it, that was the true Gabriele Bates, the rest is just fiction.

 

Marianne is lying on the bed with her eyes open, her arms on top of the sheet. The tenuous glow of the day is filtered by the curtains in this room, forming several symmetrical lines on the wall. She hears life on the other side. The voice of her son-in-law shouts behind the partitions, and then her daughter replies. It seems to be about money. Here we go again, Marianne thinks, rolling her eyes. Now she has to pretend she’s still sleeping, and pretend for another day she isn’t there. Even when it seems impossible to go unnoticed, the bed squeaks as she turns over. An uncomfortable position, as if she were imprisoned, because she would like to get up and do what she was doing. What was it? Oh right, she had been sitting on the bed, letting her feet touch the floor, although looking at them from here the tiles are cold, strange, and their design is different from what she had been accustomed to seeing her entire life. Even so, I am very afraid that she won’t be able to do this. It’s not me saying this, it is because of the belt that is pressing on her stomach and keeping her in that position. Sometimes we might idealize something nearly inexplicable, such as getting up and being able to go urinate in the toilet first thing in the morning. Or while the alarm clock was ringing, and she saw how Arthur was sitting there in his white shirt, although he frequently looked like his mind was far away as he yawned. This memory sometimes comes up when she asks herself who is going to come today to free her. Will it be her daughter who, as she loosens the tether, fusses at her because her urine has wet the mattress? Or perhaps the husband who is walking by her room, and hearing her entreaty walks on by? Will it be one of her grandchildren who, in an act of sympathy while awaiting their turn in the bathroom, ends up in this bedroom and provides a real solution to her situation, using terms like leaving her in a home, abandoning her at a petrol station, or throwing her into a ravine? Get a grip on yourself, her grandson says, I used to sleep here. You know, her daughter says a few minutes later, if you realized how much of a slave I am to you, maybe you wouldn’t be so concerned about this damn tether, you know? It’s already off so now you can go wherever you like.

 

Her husband says goodbye to Silvia with a kiss. She stirs her cup and listens to the sound of the door without blinking. She has just passed a hand over her cheek, and she is now stretching out her legs under the table, although today it is just so she can feel that she isn’t bumping her feet into those of her granddaughter. She looks at the highchair and finds it empty and abandoned. Well, having said that, Silvia clears the table and washes four things that she had placed in the sink. Washing up is a moment when there are no secrets. That’s it. The secret is in not opening her eyes more than necessary, in doing everything as if she were sleepwalking. Otherwise she wouldn’t do it. There are so many things she could do in that state that people would marvel at, for example the line in her pants that she is shaping on the ironing board from the hem to the pocket. Then she continues with her skirt, she sees a small spot that won’t come out, no matter how much she steams the area or scrubs it with a clothing brush before putting it on a hanger. She does the same thing with the blouse, pressing the neckline carefully with the iron, as if this were what she was born to do. A quick glance shows that she does this because someone else taught her how so many years ago. Her mother, who in turn learned much farther back, with an iron actually made of iron that they left on the fire. And ironing clothing! Who would have said that one day she would detest this life, just seeing her squandering it with an iron in her hand, with a brush, with a sponge, a rag, a sewing needle! Not to mention the wax strips, the dye, the lotion for cellulitis, the firming cream for the chest and neck. So that when everything falls apart, at least it does so with dignity. And all so her husband won’t leave her for a younger woman, someone who might be more grateful than Silvia, someone who could give him unlimited love. Although it is obvious that she has an advantage in this terrain; she has already had a lover. Someone who she grasped onto, to unsuspected heights, in exchange for a better life. Do you believe that her somewhat-hidden face is unaware of this when she dials a telephone number and asks to speak to Gabriele?

 

Marianne looks out the window, small drops decorate the glass. Mother, the daughter begins, I was thinking that this year we might go on vacation. The other raises her head. As I said, the daughter continues, it’s been so long since we went, the youngest hasn’t been anywhere, it would do us all good to change the scenery for a few days, look, I’ll show you the guide book of where we would like to go, which doesn’t mean that we’ll actually end up going, but look at the landscape, doesn’t it make you want to touch it with your fingers? Kilometers and kilometers of palm trees, white sand, the hotel right on the beach, eight hundred and fifty per person, but that’s what money is for, what do you think? Fine, says Marianne. And just for a moment she softens at the engrossed look of her daughter. And how words are difficult for her, so much so that the old woman ends up caressing her arm and saying: my daughter, I can already see that I would be the discordant note in such a paradisiacal place. The daughter takes her hand, a hand that she kisses, as if suddenly she were crazed with happiness. Mother, she exclaims again, would you like to come with us? Marianne lowers her chin, she would like to say yes, who wouldn’t? But this is her response: I would only get in your way. But you know, I’ve thought about it, the daughter says, it would do you good as well to take a vacation somewhere. At the same time the daughter lifts a hand to her apron where she carries the leaflet of a residence that she put in her pocket. Two days of carrying this leaflet upstairs and downstairs, you can’t imagine how it gets heavier and heavier, and in a moment of carelessness she puts a damp hand on the apron and thinks, but why does it cost so much! Marianne gestures with her jaw, in this far-off place where she frequently asks herself why she is here, because she had been fine where she was, in that house where she lived by herself. And now every day she has to say yes, no, gabbing every damn day, when she had become accustomed to the silence.

 

Silvia goes to the hotel. She only says hello to people now and again, or she waits at the back of the school where the children are playing and shrieking in the yard. She watches. Suddenly, she hopes no one will notice her. But her granddaughter’s head turns, she is counting and jumping rope by herself! A girl with dark hair who, suddenly, fixes her gaze on Silvia when she slows her pace. Grandmother! And the granddaughter places her hand on the metal railing where the woman’s hand is, she is very well dressed today, with makeup on her eyes and lips, with her hair straightened using the blow dryer, as if she were going to a party. What are you doing pretty one? Silvia says while she takes her fingers. Not much, the trellis has a very ornate design, and she doesn’t want to get caught on it. She spent a large part of her childhood in that same school. And the truth is that it’s the last thing she wants to do now. Of course those were other times, when your ears were boxed to discipline you, when you were hit on the hand with a ruler, or you were put in a corner of the classroom with several books on your head, due solely to the fact that you came from a humble family. There is no other explanation, Silvia thinks, while the bell rings and they remove their hands. The granddaughter has said nothing other than: today lunch is disgusting, soup and pork. But then, as Silvia walks backwards, she takes her leave with a question: is it true you don’t love us anymore? Then she turns and leaves with the rest of the students who abandon the balls that are still spinning on the field. Back to studying! Silvia leaves with a tap-tap of her heels. The sky turns gray momentarily.

 

There is a folded newspaper on the table where Gabriele has sat down. Also, whoever turns their head towards her, or whoever approaches her with a bag in their hand, greets her and surely takes away some souvenir they did not have. Some photograph signed by Mrs. Bates with a green marker, that someone keeps carefully in their agenda before returning to whatever table where they usually talk of business. One can mistake the table, and suddenly find oneself listening to words like budget deficit. Since when? This hotel is in the middle of a lost city, just like the gaze of the actress, although she changes her position while she waits, alone or accompanied. Because she’s already signed an autograph for someone else, someone who asks her to dedicate the autograph to his father, who he says is a huge admirer of hers. No matter that many lost track of this actress a long time ago, as if the earth had swallowed her up or something. Even Silvia, who opened her umbrella on the way here, and who has now just entered the hotel’s café with her hair somewhat curly from the humidity, looks for Gabriele, this phantasmagoric character who has appeared out of nowhere on a Sunday, and who acts as if nothing has happened, after forty years of silence. Just one postcard that the mailman left in the mailbox in the early years of her family, a greeting that she opened eagerly, she left it under the tree, and one day she put it together with the rest of the envelopes that the celebrity sent. But now Silvia takes off her coat, leaves it on the seat to the side, and sits down in front of her. For a moment she doesn’t seem so unknown.

 

Gabriele pulls her chair a little closer when she says: a dozen men and just the two of us, which would be six each, do you see? Silvia looks back, just a bit horrified, although looking carefully they all look like they do well, no doubt they have full wallets, she thinks, as she turns her face back to Gabriele. Then she exclaims: but I can’t even with mine, if he gets on top of me with his weight sometimes it feels like he’s going to crush me, one of these days I’m going to break a rib, for a while it’s been difficult for my husband too, and he needs to take that blue pill from time to time, oh, but he’s sure that I’m the one to blame for all of this, I guess it’s hard for all of us to accept that we’re not so young anymore. The waiter leaves two cups on the table. Gabriele pours in a half envelope of sugar and stirs. Silvia also lifts her cup and drinks. Can I confess something? She returns her cup to the table. When you left, Marianne wanted to see every letter of yours in those early years, you know I wasn’t much for writing, but I still remember the first postcard I received, I ran to show it to Marianne, even if just to rub it in a little, but what did she do, she looked it up and down and said to me: well, she said the same thing to you that she said to me, but the postmark shows that she sent mine one day earlier, she copied yours from mine. That happened a long time ago, Silvia continues, and I don’t want to waste my time talking about Marianne or the letters that neither you nor I wrote. Silvia, Gabriele says, that’s why I’m here. When Silvia hears this she drains her cup and a drop falls on the table, but she takes napkins from the dispenser and wipes the low wooden table. Meanwhile, the actress watches this nervous woman who should have been watching her grandchildren, she would like to know the excuse she used to be absent, and now she is here, grabbing another paper napkin and drying her hands, which look different from how the actress remembered them, plus there is a wedding band, for Gabriele rings lasted one or two years, depending on how long the contract with the producer lasted. Sometimes she could even feel the crying of her second husband, who whimpered as he said: but how can they keep telling me that I am a homosexual if I’m married now, and to Gabriele Bates, what more does the press need, for me to go out on the balcony and proclaim that I’m happy? The actress turned toward the actor and asked: but you’re not happy? And he, with his eyes a little stunned, responded: happiness is so subjective, isn’t it?

 

Mother, the daughter says as she enters the dining room, don’t you remember you have to take the pill? The daughter fills a glass, opens a drawer where she keeps an arsenal of medical products, breaks the seal for a tablet and turns back around. Marianne is seated in a strange position, her eyes closed, her arms in her lap, her head pointing up, her mouth ajar. Mother? The daughter cries as she grabs her arm. Mother? But she has already awakened brusquely from this deep dream that invades her both day and night, any old time seems suitable. Better asleep than awake, Marianne thinks, putting the tablet on her tongue. And now drink all of the water, the daughter indicates while she looks at the time on a large antique clock that is hanging on the wall and chimes every hour; it is one of the few of her own possessions that Marianne keeps in this house, apart from her suitcase. But now she’s no longer thinking about leaving, although her days in this room are slow and strange. And you might not believe it, but just one minute earlier Arthur had been in the garage repairing a motorcycle, she was seated on some old-fashioned steps with a notebook in her hand, writing a letter that said: “Dear Gabriele, I often surprise myself with chalk in my hand, while I write on the chalkboard and think, how will this movie that you are telling me about end? Every day I walk by the cinema to see if the marquis is up, as I’ve already told you many times, Arthur goes with me the first time, and I always bring handkerchiefs just in case I cry, although I enjoy the second time better, when I go with all of my students and later we discuss it in class. They often ask me things that I answer happily, such as, for example, why do I only take them to see movies with this golden-haired actress? Because we are friends, I tell them, and you don’t know how that affects them, sometimes they won’t stop wasting time until I get annoyed, this year I’ve taught a complex and playful course, but they’ve gotten on my nerves. Also things with Arthur are not completely wonderful, do you know that he has spent the entire winter trying to repair his motorcycle? And every night I tell him that dinner is on the table, and he throws his greasy rag on the ground and comes in. It’s been days since he last spoke to me, strictly speaking, why would he have to? Is there anything worse than spending hours and hours in the kitchen, and suddenly finding myself opening the lid of a pot and realizing that there is nothing other than boiling water? Sometimes I feel displaced, as if someone were taking the trouble to tear my life slowly away, sometimes I see how it escapes, it runs into the dark like a fugitive and goes through doors and walls. On the other hand, everyone makes the effort to assure me that Arthur has died. From time to time they take me to the cemetery and I even leave flowers on a tomb, and I look closely at the photograph that has become faded by the passage of time, but when did he die, I ask, disconsolate. Little by little a muted image of Arthur comes to me, I see him stretched out on the floor, but I am incapable of remembering how long ago that was. Every day I look out the window, disconcerted, as if my place were no longer here. Then I look up and I find myself in this apartment with all sorts of unknown people around me, there’s one who tells me, I’m her husband, and I say oh, okay fine, and I laugh, but then the same one turns around and says: look at how she laughs, I would like to laugh like that, of course you have nothing to be worried about, right? And then I get irritated, I lift my finger, but suddenly I don’t know what I was going to say. Well, in any case, this is your friend signing off, waiting impatiently for news from you.”

 

The actress stretches her feet out on the dark-tiled floor where it seems they are alone. Finally, Silvia exclaims, after all of these years I have to admit that your parents left because there wasn’t as much work here as they said there was. It’s true, the issue of the factory, Gabriele adds, crossing her arms, and how many people didn’t have work. But look, she starts again as she rests her elbow on the armchair, a lot of people found other jobs, not necessarily here, but in this area, mine got the idea in their head that they wanted to go back up north, it didn’t matter what I said to them, they believed the time to leave had come, they packed up their things and I remained, they were never aware of how much that change affected me, luckily I escaped shortly thereafter. But it is strange, she adds, sometimes I stop to think what would have happened if my parents had not had the accident, that one event disrupted everything, I spent many months working, saving and counting the days before I could return here, but suddenly my empty seat was even farther away; I should have taken that train and I didn’t. However now, Silvia says, whatever you might have thought, that wasn’t your train, you don’t have to be a genius to see it, because you already had your own personality from the time you were a little girl. Plus, Silvia says, seriously, what would you have done here? You would have ended up in the clothing industry like so many of our generation, many hours of work and very poorly paid, and then if you get involved with a man who tells you that he doesn’t like his wife to work for someone else, plus if you have a boss who doesn’t understand the boundary between putting his hand on the cloth or putting it on the blouses of the workers, do you understand what I’m trying to tell you? If you had taken that train, then now you would be like I am. Silvia stands up for a moment, takes a general look around and picking up her purse and asks: what about staying here to eat? Gabriele is looking at her when she asks: I thought you were going to have lunch with your husband. No, Silvia says, I left him a note on the microwave.

 

There is the sound of a door. Then the elevator descending. Marianne is alone, the television on, the volume somewhat low. Her skin transparent under this stocking that sometimes itches, and she starts to scratch it with her other leg, she rolls up the hem of her skirt and continues scratching with her nails, she presses her fingers against a varicose vein in the muscle, as if she could tamp it down, maybe she just wants to hide it. Marianne lifts her legs with a certain symmetry, while the ramifications of this action appear again in all their blue and green splendor, like the curly ribbon on a gift, which gives the pallor of this woman a splash of color. This same woman not so long ago was in front of the mirror after putting some rouge on her cheeks and some dye on the roots of her hair. Not even her daughter can keep up with this hair-cutting, and if she can, she cuts her sideburns and leaves the rest undyed. She often cuts so close to the neck that it is impossible to put in a curler. And yet, she asks, who would be interested in my mother if she were to style her hair and put on some nail polish? Maybe just some pensioner who might see a second chance in this bizarre figure. Marianne puts both feet on the floor and makes the motion to stand up, although today it is difficult with these slippers that slide on the floor. A very worn-out sole, the woman would say while she sees that the door to the outside seems to be closed from the other side. But life inside continues, even though now she doesn’t know what to do with herself, even though she knows by heart every wall and every light switch, like a prisoner in her cell.

 

Gabriele returns the menu to the waiter. Then she looks at Silvia, who seems a bit distracted, her gaze on the linen tablecloth. Would you have left with him? The actress suddenly asks. The other lifts her gaze and then says in a low voice: I don’t even know his name. Are you telling me, Gabriele cuts her off, that you never introduced yourselves? Silvia shakes her head no, the other sketches a smile and also shakes her head no. The waiter appears and begins to fill their glasses. Silvia brings hers up to her mouth, takes a sip, puts it back on the table and says: you see, I was fifty-five years old and as naïve as ever, I don’t know, we could have introduced ourselves and spoken about ourselves, but that’s not how it was, if it had been I wouldn’t have hesitated even one day to tell him I was a grandmother, oh, and for years! I was so proud! Although I have no doubt that would have startled him, the fact is that penthouse made me forget about the life I had outside. Anyway, Silvia continues, we both knew the relationship was not based on outside elements, rather the magic was in primary things, I remember one afternoon he sucked on my neck, and well, the thing I most admired about him is that it was not done carefully, but it made me feel desired, although by some miracle my husband did not discover it, once I got it into my head to lower the collar on my blouse while we were eating, I would have liked to know what he thought of me, I was being unfaithful and suddenly I felt like telling him to his face that this man whose name I didn’t even know, knew more about my private life than he had learned in all of his, of course it repelled me at the moment, because I ended up realizing that neither of them knew me, and more so when I found out that the other had gone leaving no more trace than that hickey, the relationship was sputtering out, therefore whether I would have gone with him or not is something I will never know; the possibility never occurred.

 

Gabriele puts a napkin on her skirt, then she puts the spoon into the consommé that is still steaming, she stirs it and says: Bill and I agreed that our relationship would be open, due to work we spent more time apart than together, obviously we got married and just one week later we were already having to say goodbye to each other at an airport, we decided that if some adventure presented itself, it would always remain with the shoot, in the background, and that we would never discuss it at home. One day, she starts talking again, I called him to tell him that there were two days left on the shoot, and he got into the car and came to get me near where we were shooting those last days, you can already see where that freedom we proclaimed was going to go, sometimes he would spend all day and night driving, I often asked myself what Bill would think if he knew that in our entire relationship I never had even one little adventure, the fact is it never even occurred to me, the only thing I wanted was to get back and spend all the time I had with him, sometimes it wasn’t much, but it was enough to warn us that we were prisoners of our work. Then, the actress says, they called Bill to do another movie, and while he was getting ready to go I read his script, who was playing the role of Nora? I asked him, intrigued, but he didn’t respond, I think he had lovers everywhere, he thought I had lovers all over the place, sometimes he would throw it in my face, putting some newspaper right in front of my nose and saying: I’m not the only one, the media thinks it too. And so I let him leave, the car pulled away, I ran after him but it was already too late, Cassandra Finn was playing the role of Nora. I was so furious at that moment, but now I would not want to be in her place, an actress through and through who left everything for him.

 

Silvia leaves the spoon on the plate, passes a napkin over her lips, crosses her hands and then says: the magazines said that Cassandra Finn didn’t say no to anyone, it’s true she was very beautiful, but look, someone always ends up having to pay the piper. The waiter removes the plates and brings the next course. The actress picks up her knife and fork and starts to cut. Silvia does the same. A delicious piece of meat. But the fact is that we actors, Gabriele exclaims suddenly, the public doesn’t forgive us for aging. Then she continues cutting her piece of rare meat, the sauce mixing with the juices and taking on a bloody hue, similar to that of the glass that is close to her hand. Gabriele could close her eyes and let herself be taken by this waltz that she hears so faintly it is almost imperceptible. Or even try to remember the question Silvia asked just before putting another bite in her mouth. Tell me, she had asked, what projects do you have after this? Movie? Theater? Maybe both things? Then Gabriele puts down her glass, which has rocked a couple of tenths of a second with the intention of turning over, the agitated liquid has gained momentum, spilling wine on the tablecloth, filtering quickly through it, small drops have gathered together, absorbed by the white cloth. I’m sorry, Gabriele responds belatedly. Silvia places her hand over the actress’ hand, gives her a wink and says: you’re going to have to loan me one of your blouses. Gabriele approaches her and says: are you crazy, every blouse I have in that room is worth a fortune! So what, Silvia contradicts her softly, you just spilled on mine, I spent days sewing it myself, every stitch, the pockets and this gold drawstring that I had to sew by hand. Fine, Gabriele says intently, if you want one you’ll have to come up and get it.

 

Dear Marianne, said the last letter that she places into the metal box, although it doesn’t have an envelope, another thing misplaced with the move, the woman thinks, which went up and then down in her old house. Today she goes to her daughter, who opens a box on the nightstand and examines some watch, which she will sell shortly thereafter to some antique dealer who gives her cold hard cash in return. Of course there is hardly anything of value in this house, no matter how she looks from box to box, in one wardrobe and another. Except, of course, for some letters from Gabriele that she will also sell someday to the highest bidder. It’s all a matter of waiting for the right moment, the correct person, or the most indecent, whoever gives the most, but someone who is willing to pay for sentences such as the one she reads sidelong from behind her mother, “I planted myself in my manager’s office and the first thing he says to me: look, I told you, you should have taken barbiturates twenty years ago, now it’s too late.” At the same time Marianne folds the paper, as if it had no importance at all. Let me see, the daughter demands as she squats down. This daughter who passes her hand over her mother’s hair, as if it were a mindless gesture. And how many others things she doesn’t skimp on to keep her content. Mother, shall I read you the letter? No, Marianne responds. Now she brings the box closer and closes it with both her arms. It doesn’t matter what it protects; perhaps one day she won’t remember what the box contains. After all, it is nothing more than paper and ink, words that Gabriele wrote when she could, she never wrote a lot, but despite that each letter was expected, each word duly understood. “The theater audience is attentive, night after night I receive flowers for a role that I wouldn’t have hesitated to reject years ago, and then yesterday one of the most important critics came to my dressing room and said to me: Mrs. Bates, don’t you think that so many bouquets together look mournful?” Sometimes there were months and months of silence, then suddenly a sepia-colored envelope would appear in the mailbox of her house from the actress. It’s about time, Marianne said to her friends, taking the envelope in her hands and opening it from the side. How could she forget the thrill that preceded reading it, Marianne remembers, while her daughter moves away and shuffles things around. You never know where there might be money, and she lifts the mattress, or even opens one of her father’s shoeboxes, shoes that are practically new that she could take to her husband; they wear the same size. Although who would wear the shoes of a dead man, the daughter thinks, putting them back in the armoire. “The days fly by like a breeze of pages before a storm, I sit down on one of those stone benches, and like many afternoons the wind takes my hat. Sometimes I run after it and it escapes, my little nomad, I say while I pick it up, and then I look up and again and I see that house that has never been mine. I make an effort to create environments, I move the furniture from one place to another, but nothing satisfies me. A long time ago they instilled in me that what doesn’t fit into a suitcase is completely dispensable. If it truly isn’t the house or the objects, then it is me and that damn need I have to play someone else’s life.”

 

Silvia sits on a corner of the bed and unfastens the cuffs of the blouse that slides down her back, along her trembling skin, leaving a glimpse of a white bra, as if Gabriele has decided to make something more of this trip. A trip that has ended, the actress says while she takes something out of the armoire and sits on the bed. As she does this Silvia looks at the floor. Because here, no matter how you look at it, life is changing from the life of that woman who appeared in the magazines, showing a house surrounded by nature that many dreamed of having. Although not Silvia, who already knows how much dust and shit accumulates in a small one. But now in exchange, as she picks up this piece of clothing in her hands, her defects are on display, like the scar from a cesarean, freckles that grow disproportionately, or that skin under the arms that starts to hang from the muscle. There are people who decide to rejuvenate themselves with plastic surgery, although it is never going to be what it was, but similar to what is remembered. Why deceive ourselves, here is a woman who presses a scrap of cloth with her fingers and would like to be thirty years younger. No doubt her complexes would still be developing, taking away some year, letting her see that her relationship is not forever, one can still flee. Or even pass her entire life thinking that she might leave on the day when it is least expected, and suddenly find herself in a routine that traps her completely, while she asks herself if there isn’t some escape route. Silvia moves one of her feet and touches Gabriele’s foot. Silvia slides her hand over the coverlet and touches Gabriele’s hand. Note the symmetry in the gesture, even throwing herself into the arms of this actress would seem fitting.

 

Perhaps Marianne, looking out the window, could still explain what solitude is for her. Something that looks like a bird lands on the railing, and for a few seconds looks at her, just at her. The bird gives the impression that it is pleading with its beak, calling breadcrumbs to the window. Marianne stands up slowly, walks cautiously so as not to frighten it, goes into the kitchen, gets a scrap of bread from the bread box, and when she returns the bird is still there, just a little restless shaking its wings the whole time, what happiness! Then she makes a move to open the window, but at that precise instant this tiny undisciplined being takes off and flies away. Just like this grandson who is arriving, he moves off down the hall, closes the door to the room and puts his headphones on. He is calm with his things, his cell phone that he suddenly throws into the waste bin, with a table full of books and notes that he casts onto the floor. Because if there is anything that Marianne sees it is that this young boy returns to the dining room, opens the box with the medications, and if he could he would take one after the other, one after the other, and so on forever. So what is stopping him? Could it be that scary look that Marianne gives him as she takes the drugs out of his hands? Or that instant in which he says he is sorry, as if to say: believe me, if you were in my place you couldn’t stand it, I feel very alone, child.

 

You carry it, the manager says to the man whose front is sweating from carrying so much merchandise from one place to another. In the factory, Silvia’s husband is just one more of these hundreds of workers, among whom there is not a single day that they don’t get screwed by the boss, but his fixation on women is a constant theme. There comes an age when he puts it into reverse and stops carrying things yet one more time over this brief trajectory where he stops the machine again and lowers the load, here or there, wherever the hand of the young manager indicates, who has arrived with his studies completed and plenty of pretension, as if one would spend their life at each job. And how he scolds these women who wear caps, coats and white gloves. There are days when Silvia’s husband drives and asks himself how many kilometers he’s driven over this section with its artificial lights, on this cement floor that is stained with grease and is deteriorating, as is the machine, which he stops again to pick up more merchandise, up and down the whole time, wherever it is needed. Sometimes he has the impression that he expected much more of everything, he wouldn’t know how to explain it, he has gone through life like a salad sitting on a table, an insipid taste, as if it were missing the dressing, and life has passed by devoid of any spice. He would have to be reborn to change this destination that seems to be stopped on a curve, and it is only baseball that can get him out of his seat, but there she is, Silvia, saying things like: don’t get so agitated, your heart could give out at any moment! Ah yes, the heart, this thing that he, in a matter of seconds, watched go out in his father’s eye, like a breath that has ended, just one more who has ceased to suffer. Different from the hernia that often greets him with a good twinge in his spine, although it is too late to rectify it, and this steering wheel doesn’t stop moving. Do you see it? Once again he unloads this packaged product that will be exported. Although they don’t ask him where, because now he would answer: don’t ask me, I’m just a peon, ask the bishop over there, he’s the one who knows about this. And so from one place to another he passes the time and lets his imagination wander, which is one of the few free things remaining, although these women don’t differ much from the one he has at home, who today, without a doubt, is not there. And he misses her, he misses Silvia, that woman who long ago used to dissolve into tenderness when she was under him: don’t stop, don’t stop, don’t stop. And how her light went out little by little, like a candle, from one season to another. It is better not to think about it, it is better to think about pretty things, like Silvia and Gabriele entering the women’s restroom together and passing the toilet paper to each other, happy things, he would pay to see something like that. He would also pay to create a place between the two of them. If everything could be bought with money, he would remove his wife and replace her with one who was gentler, then he would remove that one and put another one in her place.

 

Gabriele lifts the skirt and caresses the inside of her thigh. Wait, Silvia says, detaining her hand. She unfastens the buckle on her shoes and lowers her stockings. Gabriele helps, although she isn’t doing much; rather, she moves away the pale hand that is inside the stretched stocking. Silvia has her eyes closed, as if trying to remember that lover who took the initiative, and who would drag her along the floor or imprison her with some handcuffs, while she turned around, with her back to him or on all fours. But wait, she says again in the instant when she looks at the neat nails of the actress, or at the ring that has an imbedded diamond. How many times have you done this? Silvia would say to her closest friends never, never in her life. Stop, the woman says, who seems to be blushing while she raises her hand to her cheek, or even stretches her legs and points them up. Don’t hurry, she says. Suddenly she picks the blouse up off the floor and goes to the sink where she scrubs the spot with a cake of soap, like old times. Silvia has been washing with powdered or liquid detergent for a long time, and the cake slips between her fingers, like the fastener on the bra that she brings up before the mirror, while she scrubs and cleans the blouse. She sees how her lipstick has faded, but how at the same time the pink stands out; it looks fake. She is not aware of what just happened, but here there are signs of pink at the corners of her mouth. Silvia puts the stopper in the sink and leaves the piece of clothing to soak, perhaps it can still be saved. But what about her?

 

Gabriele said nothing else, she just went into the bathroom, picked up a brush and smoothed her hair. No, it would seem she said nothing. In any case, it should be Silvia who says something. No, it seems that Silvia also doesn’t have any intention of saying anything, apart from drying her hands on Gabriele’s towel. Oh, and the actress now asks what she has done wrong. Silvia places the towel on the towel rack. It’s me, she says. Ah, the actress says with her look; she looks down to the sink where a garment is soaking. Look at this most unusual situation; they have just abandoned it for a piece of clothing. Some day she will write about it in her memoirs: yes, I tried it once with a woman, I don’t advise it, they throw you aside like a dog and then they take your blouse that was worth two thousand. Or perhaps Gabriele won’t write a book at all, why would she? It would make enemies with many of those in her agenda, where, curiously, Marianne and Silvia appear on the same page. For a long time she has written people in according to the city where they live. And believe me when I tell you that there are cities more splendid than this one. There are even cities where the people kiss your feet just for coming. She is a beloved actress who will go anywhere, traveling is all she does now, because she has become a wandering actress from head to toe, who tightens her past like a cord, the only thing that could still be held onto. Of course other than that thing with which she brushes her hair. If you’re going to kiss me, she said one day to her dear Bill Engel, don’t ask me if I’ll stay, do it, just do it. How much force that sentence had, like a hurricane before the kiss and the consequent credits: Gabriele Bates in the role of Catherina Miller, Bill Engel in the role of Mr. Miller. What times those were! She leaves the brush on the vanity and says: if you’re going to kiss me, do it before it’s too late. And Silvia throws herself at her, like a soul in torment, in exchange for some warmth that she has never known, but maybe she will today.

 

Marianne eats at this table where someone is always crying, and today it is the youngest one who from his highchair, who spits out a greenish puree that is too hot. Who does the mother think she is dealing with? This boy, as you see him, with his blue bib that today has an embroidered duck on it, will one day end up taking his mother by the arm to some residence, whenever his mother reaches the indicated age. If you watch closely, there isn’t a day that this woman doesn’t say: one day you will annoy me to death. But today it is Marianne who says: your children are annoying you to death. Ah, the daughter turns towards her: where did that come from? She asks. Marianne doesn’t know. Although no one likes puree being thrown in their face, this old woman likes it even less, as she crumples her napkin with her fingers and dries her cheek half-heartedly. What a pack of wretches! Look, she could say a lot today, if they would let her. But of course the daughter turns around again and exclaims: he’s just a baby! Listen mother, as long as you are under our roof I won’t tolerate you insulting my children or telling me how to raise them, understand? Marianne raises a hand as if to say: excuse me. The daughter raises the spoon high again, the boy turns his face the other way, and the old woman watches the oldest grandson come in and sit at the table. There isn’t much to say after all, but with the agility with which he moves the spoon up and down, it seems like the boy has recovered his appetite, he finishes his meal that today was not very abundant, leaving a dry line on the plate’s edge. Not like the love this boy felt for someone, just a few hours ago he had wanted to die, but now he is recovering his strength, what a cure, if it isn’t her then it will be another, otherwise he will continue playing foosball like he’s done up to now. Oh, but now the middle one is back from doing his chores with the backpack on his back! Don’t even think about sitting down at the table without washing your hands, the woman says to him, as she continues to feed the boy in the highchair, because we won’t have any pigs in this house. Although she serves them pork all the time. The meteorologist also appears on the television and explains pleasantly: and tomorrow more rain! But what does it matter, Marianne spends more time at home than outside. It’s the same if it rains or is sunny. In that case, just leave her there if she’s a bother. Nevertheless, perhaps tomorrow, she no longer remembers.

 

Silvia’s husband arrives home and there isn’t even that green mash the baby had eaten that he can put in his mouth. At times like this one misses what one does not have. His hunger ends up leading him to the pantry, which has a type of display. Is there anything you would like, dear diner? Today we have cereal, cookies, baby food, bottles of milk and tomato sauce, perhaps you prefer to look in the cupboard to the side where you will find soup in an envelope, ideal for people like you who are beginners, look how simple it is: put water into a pot, turn on the stove, while you are waiting for the water to boil, pour the contents of the envelope into a cup, add the water, stir, and enjoy. Silvia’s husband sits on the sofa and waits. Perhaps his wife is on her way, but what does this man know, he doesn’t remember anything like this happening in eight or ten years. Perhaps less, he couldn’t say exactly. What he does remember is that week after week passed, every Tuesday and Wednesday, as if she were not in her right mind. And if this man were being sincere, he would say that she came late because of the fucking cross-stitch class she was taking. Looking at the walls in the house he finds it strange that no pictures are hung. He would get bored. Anyway, cross-stitching is tedious, obviously, two movements of a needle are needed to make one cross, imagine the time it takes depending on the design, three years for whoever sews like that, which is how long a war might last, although there isn’t one. A war has been going on here for years, the man seems to say while he places a napkin on the coffee table by the sofa, as if he had suddenly become politely educated. A man who will now have to get by with a glass of water and the artificial powder floating in it. What’s on the television? Maybe some program that will distract him as he lowers the volume and waits for the lock to open. He asks himself things while he stirs and stirs, as if it were an unending liquid, but look, the telephone rings and there is a familiar voice, the voice of his only daughter, who asks if tomorrow they can take care of the grandchildren. Well of course, Silvia’s husband responds. He ends up with the ball back in his court.

 

Silvia trembles in that cosmic delirium in which she breathes very close to a star, which she experiences as if she had learned the role from some other place. Better not to think about that, she will have said at some time. The past is the past. The present slips by like the bedspread that falls to the floor. And the future is closer than she would have imagined. But now she is just in a position that looks like a broken doll that they have to wind up constantly, as if the actress had lost the notion of time. And Silvia is involved, while she looks at her wrist watch, which for hours has been marking the time with its face to the wall. She turns it towards her, but soon Gabriele turns to face her. An actress without urgency, one might say, as she traces her fingers over one of Silvia’s nipples, and, as many will remember from While the beasts slept, whose hands evoked a game of light and shadow, as if the director of that movie were playing with that the whole time, and that was not the first nor would it be the last, but her hands were shown like that, very pale and in the darkness, while she disinfected wounds with gauze, or she crossed them and prayed, or when she left flowers for a dead person. Hands that Silvia watched in the movie theater many years ago, while she asked those in front of her to be quiet. She would have wished to remember entire dialogues, but only images appear in their place, like this, where Gabriele’s fingers flutter very slowly, entering, exiting, and continuing to open old wounds, like the one from that station where Silvia also waited, while she bit her nails and crossed her legs under a bench. A train that was approaching made a stop and moved away again. How long ago that was, as if for all this time she had been waiting for a ghost.

 

Marianne opens her eyes. The first light of day filters down the hall of this middle floor, but not into her room, where for yet another morning she prays for the tether to be removed. If someone doesn’t come quickly she will end up wetting herself. And her daughter has had a lot of practice with this, grabbing her children by the legs, fastening diapers with adhesive tape and absorbing what they can! She cannot say the same about that similar item that the daughter reluctantly places on her each night, and look, a tepid trickle is already dripping down the woman’s thigh, the same woman who not so many years ago was teaching in a school and talking about hygiene, or who moved from school desk to school desk and who suddenly found a small puddle on the floor. And how she berated that student before the whole class. What, she said, do you think the classroom is a public bathroom, at your age, but where on earth were you brought up, in a barn?! Today that young boy is now a man, so much time has passed, every morning he walks by her door and hears her complain yet again, and he walks away. Marianne tries to turn over. She can’t. Marianne tries to stretch the fabric with her hands, she sees the pieces of plastic and gauze in her fingers. A strange feeling, similar to when she taught the children how to make snow dolls with cotton and cardboard, which gave the illusion of snow, you could hang them in windows, on the door of a house, on a tree, on a fireplace, or on the point of your prick, the boy insisted, who never got tired of causing problems.

 

The alarm rings in the house. And like every other day, he puts both his feet down on the carpet and yawns. Then he turns and sees that the other half of the bed is still made up. The man goes towards the shower, the water starts to fall like pressurized rain, he soaps up his chest and arms well. He sings to scare away his problems, but this flow of tepid water suddenly feels like a bucket of cold water. He does not seem aware of what is happening, not even while he rinses his head. But now he opens the shower door and grabs a towel. Silvia? The man calls while he shaves. No answer. Tell me what type of life this is. He spends his whole life working to bring home a salary the first of every month, and not even a thank-you. He would like to hear it every now and again, even just once a year, a simple thanks that would give some meaning to why this alarm clock wakes him up every day, saying get to it! But it’s her, who says at the end of each month: salad and cutlets from the freezer, you should thank me for knowing how to manage things so well and get your paycheck to last so many days. A drop of blood on his cheek when he remembers comments like that. But he rinses the razor and puts it back in its place in the cabinet, next to the brush, lotion and the deodorant. The doorbell rings. The man walks over wearing a towel that covers him from the waist down when he opens the door and his granddaughter slips in, then his daughter leaves the grandson in the grandfather’s arms and rolls the stroller in, which the man then pushes down the hall. He was saying something in that regard to his daughter. But no matter, Silvia stops in the entry, puts her hand on the railing, climbs the three stairs and closes the door from the inside.

 

She leaves the keys on top of the table in the foyer. Then she unfastens her coat and hangs it up. She takes off her shoes in the middle of the living room, walks bare foot, and then hears: where were you, Silvia? Shortly thereafter she comes back from the bedroom wearing slippers and a robe. Then she ties on an apron, sits down at the table, looks at her granddaughter, her grandson, and when her gaze meets her husband’s, she asks very low: shall I prepare breakfast for you? She puts her two hands on the table which has not yet been set, but she stands up and takes out the tablecloth, which she will later remove unhurriedly, as if in slow motion, while she thinks about the question: where were you, Silvia? Do you hear a certain tone of supplication? Yes, Silvia has noted it well. And how her husband says the milk was too hot, as if she hadn’t tried. Look! You can even see the film on the top! Well now it’s not there, she says with a shrug, removing it with a spoon. The granddaughter says: how disgusting, this milk stinks. And how she has been ignoring her the whole time while the little one drinks in silence, that early morning baby bottle, or when she passes a hand over her forehead and says to her husband: go get dressed or you’ll be late. And then she wipes her grandson’s mouth with the bib. She has picked up the boy carefully and has kept giving him little pats on the back, while her husband goes into the other room. Shortly thereafter Silvia puts him back in the highchair before going into the bedroom, yes, at the exact instant when her husband is tying the laces on his shoes, and the woman crouches down to help him. Leave it, the man says firmly, I can still do it myself.

 

Well what a surprise! The woman says with a decided gesture, as Gabriele comes into the dining room where Marianne is seated, dressed in a nightgown and robe while she eats with her back to both of them. Can I get you something? The daughter asks. I’ve already eaten, Gabriele responds as she sits down. Marianne dips a cookie into the cup, it softens and crumbles, she picks it up with a spoon and brings it up to her mouth. Then Marianne turns and sees this woman who won’t take her hands off her arm, as if they knew each other or something, I don’t know, what does Marianne know, if she would just let her eat her breakfast with one hand. She woke up confused today, the daughter says, going on with what she was doing, tacking the hemline of some piece of clothing that she wants to shorten or lengthen. But how can you eat so many cookies, Gabriele suddenly exclaims, and the sugar? Her blood sugar isn’t high, the daughter responds while she threads the needle. Would you let me dye your hair? The actress asks, already putting a hand in her purse. May I brush it too? Yes, Marianne says. The daughter ends up pricking herself with the needle. But what does it matter; today she’s a nothing, round like the drop of blood on her thumb as if she is alone.

 

Gabriele styles Marianne’s hair with the part in the middle, then the daughter hands her a brush but insinuates that this color isn’t the best one for her age. What do you know, Marianne exclaims. That woman who she watches in the mirror, and begins to cover her hair with a gelatinous cream that she would scrape off with the fingers of her hand. Mother, can I bring you a magazine? No, Marianne says. And no, Gabriele also says, we’ll be just fine. How many times has the daughter wanted to have an assistant, and now that she understands what it means, she raises her hands to her head. There is dye dripping down her cheek! Don’t worry, we’ll fix it. And if Marianne gets up and needs to pee, now Gabriele is here to say: don’t worry, we’ll fix it. When they come back, Marianne sits back down, Gabriele puts her head down and continues dabbing on the dye with the brush. Besides that, it’s done. Yes, everything points to it almost being done. What does that tell me about her illness, the daughter starts talking again, we are nothing, if I had dyed her hair she would not have let it be, but since it’s Gabriele, right mother? Precisely when the actress removes the gloves, throws them in the trash and hears: was that Gabriele?

 

And now only silence, Silvia says to herself after dropping her granddaughter off at school and returning home, while she takes her grandson out of the stroller, and he walks by himself through the living room, then he disappears. She has to follow him wherever he goes, because at a minimum he will put small things into his mouth, or break things in an outburst of happiness. In fact there are no more small pieces, and if one still turned up from time to time, it has to be taken away from him. Leave it, she says while she lifts him onto the bed and stretches out as well. Let’s sleep, she says to him. Then she closes her eyes, but she senses a movement her grandson makes that alerts her. Silvia takes off his shoes and says: let’s sleep. A little later she looks at the ceiling and again says: let’s sleep. Strange when she half-closes her eyes, the child snuggles up to her and calls her mama. When he closes his eyes and little by little gives into sleep, she looks up and putting a hand inside her robe she whispers Gabriele.

 

Do you like the color? The actress asks her while she washes out the soap and the hair from the sink. A lot, Marianne answers as she runs her fingers through her hair, but the sleeves on her top are a little damp, as if they had been run under water. As she does this a towel has been placed on her shoulders. Perhaps while Gabriele was plugging in the hair dryer and stretching out the cord, or perhaps now, when she picks up the brush and styles her hair, she remembers a Marianne who was quite different than the one in front of her. Do you like how it looks? Yes, the other responds. There isn’t much to add, apart from the fact that it has started to rain, and perhaps they should change the scenery and return to the dining room, while Marianne’s daughter puts on an anorak and Gabriele raises her hand to say goodbye, she hears the distant sound of a door and she is finally alone with her, watching this woman who goes to the window, who for quite some time does not look at the clock with the same feeling that the other might. And the actress watches the hands of the clock in the silence. How the seconds run in this unending race. And how the words are on the tip of her tongue, but she doesn’t say a thing before she closes her mouth again. Gabriele brings an arm up under her chin. But Marianne is also aware of the incessant rain on the window, her silhouette is backlit, like a photograph in black and white. Do you not remember me? Gabriele says to her as she approaches. She decides to sit very close to the armchair and says: don’t force it, it will come. Then she moves an arm, brings it closer to Marianne’s and adds: I say the same thing when it happens to me, if I don’t remember it, then it wasn’t important. Gabriele picks up a magazine, but at the moment she is picking it up an apron falls, and with it a leaflet that she picks up off the floor.

 

But what have I done for them to treat me like this? Marianne exclaims as she takes the piece of paper. Well, I know of people who say they are better off in a residence than in a house, Gabriele says as she puts on her glasses. And what about this dining room, the actress continues, it looks like a restaurant, or the rooms, they have natural light, plus, this place isn’t very far from here, your daughter will come to visit you, and your son, your son will too. Do you think so? The other asks doubtfully. It is fine here too, Marianne says; suddenly she drops her gaze. And then she starts to talk about birds, bread crumbs, windows and a suitcase she keeps to run away some day, when she gets tired of all of this. Suddenly she separates herself from Gabriele’s arm, as if she didn’t see her, as if she didn’t even see herself. And she urinates on the armchair, she scratches the skin on her arm, and she hits herself in the head with both hands, as if she were trying to reach that point where everything began to fall apart. Yes, Gabriele would also give anything to find that specific point.

 

Silvia puts a pot on the stove, and like every day at this time she chops and peels vegetables, and when the water boils she puts them into the pot and covers it, and the lid starts to rattle, like a sound that brings the kitchen alive. Or the meat that she cooks and seasons, the bread when she cuts it and puts several slices into the bread basket, which she takes to the table, which she sets with two plates, two cups, two napkins and a bottle of water. For a long time Silvia has done this by rote, placing her grandson on the highchair, who looks much more awake, perhaps tonight it will be hard for him to sleep. In any case the boy doesn’t sleep here. Wherever she goes, her grandson is already opening his mouth and swallowing the cough syrup that she spoons into his mouth, just like she does with food, and again they have tricked him like a fool, while he sputters and spits some out with his drool.
