What is certain is that if this little one didn’t spend so many hours here, she would be happy to have him in her house, and she would change the syrup spoon before removing the baby food. But here is Silvia, stirring the whole time, while her grandson looks at his plate and becomes impatient. Wait, Silvia with a gesture. Just wait! She exclaims while she puts the bib on him. Mama, her grandson says, smacking the highchair with his hands. Then he hushes, the woman extends her arm, and today she is silent as she fills the spoon, as if she were not there.

 

Gabriele walks along the avenue with an umbrella in her hand, her shoes somewhat damp on the toe from when she was waiting on the sidewalk, then she moves forward again. Although she hesitates as soon as she gets to the other side of the traffic light, should she turn here or there, left or right. Left or right. That is the question. And as she wanders around, leaving a group of buildings behind, she ends up lowering the umbrella, placing her hand on the railing of a stairway, and then saying: will you make me something to eat? She goes in and takes off her coat. Silvia puts another plate on the table and motions her to sit down. Gabriele! The husband says, returning from the bedroom and kissing her on both cheeks. Wait darling, she says apologizing, I’ve left lipstick on your cheek. One could get off with a sentence like that, and furthermore, a star has fallen from the sky and landed here, in the kitchen of this humble house, when it could have landed in a wealthier house, and he is surprised by the luminosity of this star who is eating a plate of vegetables, she lifts a glass of water to drink, and then, looking first at one and then the other, she asks: don’t you speak to each other? Well, the husband makes a gesture… He thinks I’m seeing another man, Silvia answers. The actress moves on to the entree and then looks at them both again. If we were to speak, the husband continues, there would be a lot to say. Silvia looks at her husband and says: Is that so? Just what exactly? Gabriele cuts her portion of meat, then her gaze seems lost, observing this child who coughs and coughs, as if he were drowning or something. But the grandfather or grandmother is already there, or both at the same time, it makes no difference. Look up, look up, one of them exclaims. Then Silvia looks at Gabriele, and there are so many things she can say in just one glance: get me out of here, no matter how. But the man is already saying goodbye, fastening his jacket, and it would seem going back to what he does, morning and afternoon, what choice does he have. I’m leaving today, Gabriele also says goodbye to him with two more kisses. Yes, Silvia also just found about this.

 

Gabriele, Silvia says while she looks at the tablecloth. The same tablecloth she has been using on the table for a while and that is a little old, it even has some mended spots that the woman passes her fingers over today without folding the cloth. And here is life in all its nudity, undisguised, and anything might startle the other one, Silvia thinks while she sweeps her hand over the cloth. The furniture is not covered with sheets either, because everything here is used, there is nothing extra, in fact there are things missing. And she is not sure how the other one perceives her, this one who is seated in front of her, although perhaps like a woman in a robe and slippers, or even with that emptiness that the room exudes, where one hears the hissing of the coffee maker in the background. But the other woman can see with a brief glance the life she could have had. She wipes her lips with the napkin and hears the cry of the little one who is kicking in the highchair, as if he’s already tired of seeing how life is from such a height, and he is straining to get his body through the gap where he would like to slide down, like in a toboggan. The grandmother lets him down. Gabriele watches how the little one crawls, totters and moves out of the room. Now we’re alone, Silvia says as she serves her coffee. We are alone, she repeats as she sits down. Alone, she adds, as Gabriele watches her.

 

Then come closer, the actress says as she notes how the mug she had in front of her changes direction. And now it is so close to hers that one could almost confuse them. I still feel you inside me, they say. It’s strange, they start up again, I can’t think of anything else except yesterday. Silvia, Gabriele says to this woman who is impressed by the mere fact that she calls her by name. Yes, Silvia responds. As if what were said from this point on were important, perhaps because she expects a lot out of everything, and is accustomed to small things that she keeps, goes over, and places in order in her memory. Fragments that are similar to a broken mirror whose pieces she keeps, including the shards. There are people who lose their memory and one day everything that seemed important becomes dispensable, like the birthdays of children, grandchildren, even one’s own birthday. Marianne no longer knows what to do with those dates that she still drags around with her, because it is precisely the little that she remembers that ends up being a burden. She counts: One, two, three, four, one, two, three, four. And she maintains a sequence of numbers that are really good for nothing, not even to dial some telephone number from among those she used to choose from when she needed to talk. The importance of the telephone ends up being this thread she could wrap around and around her as if it were a necklace. Her misfortune is that she is always being watched by her daughter who darns socks, sews on buttons, and laments in silence because all of this will be over one day. That is how neurons are, they go out like a match that has burned nothing and is now just a charred stick. But say something, say something! Silvia says impatiently. And Gabriele realizes this, precisely when she was going to say to her: I’ve betrayed you… And the actress freezes up, as if she suddenly needed help from the prompter who would whisper: … your blouse. Your blouse! The prompter would repeat if he were here. So have I, she says.

 

Not here, Silvia says closing the door to her bedroom. Better here, she adds opening the blinds to this bedroom that looks like the guest room, although Gabriele is a special guest who suddenly falls on the bed in surrender, and with her gesture the other does not hesitate to come to her. At this precise instant she seeks out her grandson with her glance, who decides to scatter pieces around in here and not somewhere else in the house. But Silvia is already moving closer, she stands very close to the bed, and Gabriele places her hand between her thighs, as if she were going to move up to her crotch, above where her hand is still resting, and in no case should her grandson witness that scene, he has been crawling about everywhere for months, but while he is occupied stacking up pieces from larger to smaller he’s fine where he is. Even though he quickly knocks them down and starts over, and how much practice would he need to become a good architect. At a glance this game in front of him is as new as the game his grandmother is playing, who gets into the single bed without an instruction manual, and somewhat lost, why deny it, she brings a hand up to the other’s panties, as if expecting greater thickness than she apparently notes, and it doesn’t grow, no matter how Gabriele sighs, and at times she has to open her mouth as if it were difficult to breathe, similar to the grandson when he is snotty and needs to breathe through his mouth, with the effort that entails, until someone charitable comes along and helps him. And how many colored pieces fall to the floor, there is every type, but the grandson gets bored and yawns, it’s been a while since the covers on the bed were pulled down and something keeps him there, like the closed door that someday he will know how to open, but not today, because when he tries he falls on his fanny and cries. It is very possible that Silvia ignores his cries while she caresses Gabriele, and the actress brings her mouth close to her neck, and with her eyes half-closed whispers: Do you like it? Do you like it like this? How many times has Silvia been asked what she liked? She would remember! And she would have responded: get it over with. Or even: stop, I’m telling you to stop. The fact is that everything depends on the situation, she wouldn’t have responded like that when she was with her lover, because she came and went freely from that apartment building where she often stayed to scrub the plates that were in the sink, and she would have responded like that to that question that was also never asked of her. And now it would seem she does not answer, as if the question itself were some type of trap and she would have to be careful how she answers. I like men, she says.

 

While Gabriele lies there with her head resting on the pillow, she asks herself if she just got the brush-off. Although she turns on her side and her hair gives off golden reflections, it effectively seems so. However Silvia doesn’t leave and it seems that she stretches out her hand. A hand that the actress holds the only way she knows how. Do it, just do it, that hand that Gabriele brings up to her breasts could say, those breasts that have been admired even when they were covered. She understands that those curves are no longer what they were, she hasn’t even been able to keep her waist the same size, nothing is the same as it was on the screen, because forty years have passed and Gabriele could be embracing this woman when she asks. But how can you ask that of me? Silvia exclaims. And in truth the thing is different from what it was many years before, when the only thing the actress asked was that they let her walk around like just another person. Well you aren’t just another person, they told her, you’re a celebrity! Admit it Mrs. Bates, a veteran actor explained on the matter, when you are my age you will yearn for it, you will even idolize moments like this when we have a drink at some party and wherever you look you will see faces you know, top rate actors and actresses, who wouldn’t want to be here? And you are here, as I am here, everyone is hoping to leave with a new contract in hand, if you thought I have on this tailcoat that is tight in the back because I wanted to see how people fall in and out of love from party to party, but no, we are all willing to abandon our heart in any of these trays that pass by every once in a while, and to throw ourselves only at what interests us. Do you understand what I’m trying to say? You’ll leave today with a contract in hand and I will have to ask the wives of many of these producers and directors, and I will have to remember names, cities and years when I visited, so that you’ll have an idea of the type of night that awaits me.

 

Now is when you realize, Silvia suddenly says, the life you could have had but you left. Would you believe me if I told you, she continues, that on more than one occasion I would have left also, when I look around me? But tell me how! Since all of the income in this house is my husband’s, I would have to keep working, not a day goes by that I don’t regret it, even though at every turn my husband told me not to work and to take care, to take care of our daughter who cries all the time, you spend the entire livelong day sewing for everyone else, he said, and here you don’t even hem my pants, saying it like that, we could get by with just his salary, and look, it wasn’t so bad at home, even though now I can’t do what I used to, I have arthritis and I couldn’t sew at the same speed, who would hire me now? I am older Gabriele, although I spend the mornings and afternoons raising this child, and sometimes there is still someone who says to me: What a beautiful mother! Why would they say something like that to me, I ask myself, if you can tell just by looking at me that at my age I can’t have children, I worried about my crow’s feet years ago, now I’m just interested in finding a moisturizer effective enough to make me look two to five years younger, and what does that get me? I might fool myself a little, believe me, life here is slow by nature, but it’s the one I chose many years ago, anyway, I was the one who wanted to get married and have children.

 

Those were other times, don’t you think? She says as she turns towards Gabriele, who is already fastening her blouse with one hand and putting on her shoes with the other. She reserved a flight two days ago, but she could cancel it, stay here longer. Just a few hours ago the actress was holding Marianne’s hand, and asked her: Do you know that I’m leaving today? And Marianne turned towards her and said: no. No, the woman added, as if she would miss her. And no, she says for the third time, when Gabriele asks if she will write to her. As if she were making a choice, she stands up and, glancing around her, she feels like laying their head on the airplane seat and sleeping a while, if they let her. But what if that were the case, even as she walks down the hall, and she returns to the dining room and fastens her coat. Or somewhat later, when she walks towards the hotel reception area, collects her two suitcases, and as soon as she enters the café hears in the air: taxi for Mrs. Bates. Streets, avenues and streetlights. Stop, Gabriele orders the driver. Silvia walks slowly with the pram and her granddaughter at her side, very close to the school. And Silvia looks at her when she says to her granddaughter: wait for me here. How much the woman had thought to say, and now all she says is: you’re already leaving? Strange what is happening inside her, which is also reflected outside in her tears that do not cease, she wipes a hand under her eye, the other hand under the other eye, and the tears continue to fall. It isn’t you, she exclaims suddenly as she embraces Gabriele, it’s me, who isn’t brave enough to do it.

 

Are you crying grandmother? The granddaughter asks while Silvia again puts her hands on the stroller and walks forward. Grandmother? If she looked closely this young girl might understand this world of adults as something that is not too far off, where one day she will have to live without a new eraser with which she can erase the errors she has committed. Life in school is not the same as life outside of it, although the granddaughter later opens her notebook in her grandparents’ house, and as she copies the first lines of a text book, she watches how her grandmother sits down in front of her and crosses her arms on the table, as if she were suddenly interested in what she is doing. But the granddaughter writes slowly, as she is practicing her handwriting. Something that is not important in the adult world. Silvia sees it every time she leaves the doctor and wants to know what in the hell they have prescribed for her, and not even by putting on her reading glasses can she tell what is written on that piece of paper. She brings a hand up to her cheek, let me see, she says to her. As soon as she reads the first line, she knows she can’t help her. She would have wanted to study, anything, to be able to feel useful at times like this. But she returns the notebook to her granddaughter, who she admires in a way, for having her whole life ahead of her.

 

Silvia goes to the door and says goodbye to the grandchildren. Then she returns to the table and looks steadily at her husband. Her look is strange, like when someone looks through the progressive lenses of another, without being aware that the only clear thing that can be seen close up is in the distant truth. She can extend her index finger on this table where they are eating dinner, and suddenly the husband’s hand is on top of that finger, at the moment she was bringing a slimming cookie up to her mouth, which she now chews slowly, trying to fool her stomach, washing it down with water, letting the water absorb the cookie, which on the package promised it was chocolate but it does not taste like it, rather it is an empty space that she distills beyond her body, which is still wearing street clothes as if she were going to go out again at some point. She again fills the glass and drinks without pause, as if she sought to drown herself. But now the left hand that her husband had been holding near his heart appears again. A hand that has been wearing a wedding band for thirty something years. She has lost count. Today she would need a good squirt of soap to be able to get something like that off. And nevertheless, it would be a painful moment for her, similar to extracting a molar. Although Silvia looks at her husband’s hand that is moving away, in this incessant blink where her field of vision is reduced and dark, as if she were watching life through a kaleidoscope.

 

It is nothing at all like the green puree they serve Marianne again. Everyone talks about mashing or mincing food. I wouldn’t even dare to make sure that she swallows. Eat, or it’s going to get cold, the daughter says. The same one who every evening serves her food early, as if she were going to take her away from the table where the rest of the diners would later appear, with whom the daughter might talk, laugh, and embrace her children, grabbing them by the nape, as if they were good friends, because now they act like that, there is some sort of guest in the house who is going to stay no one knows how long. And that’s how it is, like when one climbs the stairs, and suddenly there is a narrower one. Up, down, and always tripping on the same stair. But now that step becomes her armchair and she looks at the television without excitement, while she watches how everyone else sits at the table. And they have a discussion on this middle floor. There are days that Marianne would prefer to be deaf and not turn around every time someone hits the table. And be startled when this happens, or when either of the little ones gets up from their chair and moves away, as if they sensed that things could get even worse. She used to write on the chalkboard, she would turn around, wait a prudent amount of time and erase it. Or she would sit in a chair, turn the page and continue reading what she needed to that week. Her glance often went to the door and waited for a moment for Arthur’s face to appear, because during certain seasons he worked nearby. And so she was left to look out the window. Excuse me, Marianne said suddenly as she stood up. Always with the same expression, which she now also uses to go to the bathroom, as if she didn’t realize that her daughter must go after her to lift her skirt, or pull down her stockings and underwear before she gets everything wet.

 

There are those who admire the starry darkness of nighttime flights, or when the distant splendor appears of a city left behind, the face of the moon or the intermittent light of other airplanes. The actress has been sitting in the same position for a while. It is possible that she is sleeping, but the flight attendant makes sure that the seatbelt is fastened before descending, which is the only moment at which she alters her natural state, when she takes the mask from her eyes to see what time it is. “Dearest Bill, how could we throw away what we had?” Gabriele exclaimed just two nights ago. And now she feels a certain nostalgia, as if for a moment that image of Bill coming back to the house had returned, and she closed the script that she was reading in the near-dark, with just a small table lamp on. But Bill went towards the office, his steps slow, so slow that Gabriele stood up and placed a hand on the table as she said: I’ve been waiting for you for two days. Did that Cassandra get her hooks into you? And the actor moved towards her and said: I didn’t want this. Well of course it’s what he wanted! Gabriele remembers while the plane lands. Otherwise how do you explain Cassandra coming up to her during that party, and pinching her eyelashes with her fingers, with nothing more to say other than they were not false eyelashes. No, Gabriele added just before taking a sip and throwing her glass on that dress that looked new. It was an accident, she said. Just like this trip might be, just another mistake. She climbs onto the minibus that takes travelers to the airport from the plane, and that sentence goes through her head again: I didn’t want this. And what did she do? She slapped his cheek and said: you’re an idiot. There are some who would have fought to keep him, but that’s how it goes, she opened a box, filled the table with papers, handed him a pen and told him to sign here, there, and on I don’t know what other pages. Then she took off the engagement ring, the wedding band, left them both on the table, and then picked them up again and said: the rings aren’t to blame. You’re an idiot, the woman said one day. And now, while she waits for her two suitcases, this sentence alternates with the slap that followed. She becomes vulnerable again, goes through the airport until she enters one of the bathrooms, then she goes to the sink, takes a lipstick out of her purse and outlines her upper lip before filling it in. Unimportant things, like the eye shadow that she puts on with a brush. Then she looks at her eyes, her lips, she breathes deeply, but she is unable to look at herself in the mirror when she says: let the performance begin.

 

Because here, in this house, the performance ends earlier every day. And it is always the same. Mother, I have you secured with a tether so you don’t fall, you know how fragile bones are at your age, if you were to fall you could break a hip or a femur, and I am the first person interested in this not happening, but now you rest, just rest, it’s the only thing I ask of you, that you rest, you’ll see how tomorrow will be another day, we’ve gone through it all, good days, bad days, and at the end it’s all for nothing, such a close friendship you had with Gabriele, the actress, mother, the actress who spent a few days here, how much you wrote her, and now you can’t even remember that she was here, today you look beautiful mother. Do I? Marianne exclaims. Little by little Marianne starts to drift off to sleep. It would be better for me to die, the old woman exclaims when she awakens, while she closes and opens her eyes, as she joins her hands over her chest, or separates them. Nails painted today that shine so little, although they have sparkles no one would kiss her hands in a fit of passion, not even Gabriele has done that. The actress stops at the exit, very close to those taxi drivers who are so friendly and they would take her wherever she wanted to go. Wherever she says, although she has to take out her agenda to see the address, and a little later she catches the taxi driver looking at her in the rearview mirror. Would you sign an autograph for me? He says to her when the car turns off onto a tree-lined and bushy road, as if they had been given poor directions by Cassandra and Bill. But Gabriele is already getting closer. At the house the last preparations are being made and several little outfits are taken out that have been hanging in the closet for quite some time like two interchangeable pieces, although now it doesn’t seem like it. We’ve gotten heavier, Bill says. I believe it, says Cassandra, putting on a jacket and trying to conceal the zipper of her dress that won’t go up or down. Oh, remember that today we aren’t talking about horses, Cassandra says, you know that they’ll think we’re boring, and definitely not about your kidney stone, because she’ll say we’re the same as always… Okay, Bill says with a gesture. Take it seriously when they tell you that, Cassandra starts talking again, dotting perfume on her neck, the fact is they can’t help but mention deterioration with nice words. Nonsense, Bill says, looking at her profile, and if I wear tennis shoes? Do what makes you happy, Cassandra says, but let me remind you that we have guests. Guests? He exhales, for me Gabriele could never be just a guest. Well! She says, then what exactly is she? Should I apologize to her? You would be stooping very low if you did. Do you realize, the actor exclaims, that she hasn’t even arrived yet and we are already arguing? Yes, she says, but now the only thing I ask of you is that you don’t leave me out, or I promise you there will be a high price to pay. At least we can talk about movies, that was a question, he says. You know that we can, she answers, but don’t get emotional, because then she’ll say that we only know how to talk about those years. Look, she’s arrived.

 

Gabriele walks through the garden with two suitcases in her hand. Welcome! Welcome to my home! Bill Engel exclaims as he opens the door. Do you have horses? Gabriele asks just before she embraces him, although somewhat more disinterestedly than in any of the scenes they filmed together. Yes, he responds, several. Are you well? She asks. Yes, he exclaims. Oh, but you look the same. You haven’t changed, dear Bill! You look the same too she says, saying hello to Cassandra. Thank you, she says, and lets her in, you look great too. Can I get you something to drink? Meanwhile, the actor is still very close to Mrs. Bates, and he ends up begging her pardon. Excuse me for not knowing how to do things in another way. And now they are here, he and she, one and the other, with Cassandra taking charge of preparing three margaritas. Gabriele looks at Bill and says: It seems like yesterday. A yesterday that in an instant is again long past. But at the same time it was so long ago, she then says. A long time, Bill says. Cassandra, Gabriele says, influenced by this atmosphere where everything is how she expected, sorry about that glass of wine I threw on you. Forgiven! The other exclaims as she turns around with three glasses in her hands.

 

And what one would expect is exactly what happens in this bed. The husband still has his undershirt on when he finally says: do I know him? Silvia puts on her nightgown on the other side of the bed, rubs a hand over her thigh, and says: are you aware of what you’re asking me? The husband takes off his undershirt, picks up his pajamas, and yes, it seems that he is indeed aware of what he is asking, it is a very simple question, of whether or not he knows him. The husband already knows the answer down deep, he pretends every time they lie down and she looks the other way, with her mind far away from here, will the man realize that while they do it she is thinking about buying something, or who knows what. He wouldn’t put that into a woman’s head, not even in jest, he would be deceived at how it works, thinking about one thing or another the whole time, the fact is that the entire day is full of verbiage, and later, when it is about something important, she becomes mute. Well, you wouldn’t understand, Silvia says suddenly. Why beat a dead horse. Let’s go to sleep, Silvia says, let’s go to sleep. And out of habit the man gets into the bed, turns towards her and pulls her close, today he doesn’t even need a pill. Don’t women say all men are alike? What does it matter who it is who brings on this surprising ardor when there isn’t enough sausage in the house. He thinks, he thinks, he thinks. Until the light bulb goes on. Precisely when she said to go to sleep. What sleep? How is he going to sleep with this erection that has appeared out of nowhere, in the blink of an eye, “wait darling, I left lipstick on your cheek,” do they remember? And neither one of them is showing the least bit of interest in him. Leave it, says Silvia, I’ll help you. I’ll help you, she has stressed with great sorrow, as if he were an invalid or something like that. And Silvia commits to him with one hand, as if she were in a hurry.

 

The three of them, creating a moment with margaritas in hand, sit down at the table in this living room where a butler makes sure their drinks are full, he takes away plates, brings others and serves. Gabriele picks up her fork, knife, and cuts. Bill does the same. And Cassandra. They bring their forks up to their mouths, chew, and cut again. Or they bring their glasses up, take a sip, and put them back down on the table, which is long, ideal for hosting more guests. Gabriele eats a piece of bread. So does Bill, so does his wife. Then Cassandra does something, she lights a cigarette, exhales the smoke and comments: I read that you left Ronald Parks right in the middle of a function; you’ve sure got a pair. Gabriele wipes her mouth with a napkin, then says: he had owed me money for three months, no one works for free. Then she eats a bit of a side dish and leaves her fork on the plate, as if she felt full. Do you still have the same manager? He asks. No, Gabriele responds looking at him, Daniel died. Bill says: I didn’t know. And then she says: it’s been a long time, at least eight years. So who is handling you now? Gabriele is lifting her glass to drink when she says: what does it matter, I change them constantly. Damn, Cassandra says as she puts out her cigarette and looks at the two of them, but especially at Bill Engel, this actor who did The long winter of Catherina Miller, the same one who looked out at a horizon practically in flames, the same one who, it would seem, did not notice that this woman, who had left without any explanation at all, was getting closer and reaching a point when she said: a magnificent sunset, don’t you think Mr. Miller? That ending was not clear to Cassandra, the one about don’t ask me if I’m back to stay. They’ll tell me what a crap sentence, you should always be prepared in advance, but the way things are, today there would still be some fool willing to repeat it, or at some ceremony where the career of some professional would be awarded, they always find that film. And her applauding and with a little smile on her face, her hands close to this man, as if to say: I had nothing to do with it, and despite that it turned out very well. Because it is this actor they usually applaud for those ten seconds, although in the last scene he doesn’t utter a word. But now Bill says that that sentence without Gabriele wouldn’t have been what it is. He says that that instant when her look loses intensity produces an emotional charge in the viewer because for the first time Catherina is fully aware that she might be rejected, and so that look that could turn into crying recovers its strength when she says: if you’re going to kiss me, don’t ask me if… Bill pauses a moment. Then he raises his cup and says: to you, Gabriele. And then he toasts with Mrs. Bates, who at the same time looks at Cassandra and adds: if you allow me… Go ahead, Cassandra says a little incredulously, as she observes how Gabriele’s kiss on Bill’s cheek is brief but sincere. As if she did not know that this actress, who is popularly known as the earthquake, can cause a little uproar on the very day of her arrival.

 

For quite a while words in this remote house have alternated in the inhabitants’ mouths, little conversations that are born and form a part of other times, for better or for worse, the fact is they are very well remunerated, and they yearn for it, just like those airplanes that the press awaited, even if they were delayed, and then they finally appeared smiling, because each step they took had the characteristic taste of fame that increased with each flash, just like at those parties that are still held, except they are no longer invited, it must be because of something, is what I say. People who are part of history, and on the day when you least expect it the page is turned. Some should go back many pages to advise them that one day those films would go out of fashion. And from out of nowhere now Cassandra appears, a woman with whom Gabriele would have given up a slew of years to change producers so she could have a scene with her in which they could pull each other’s hair, rip each other’s clothing, scratch each other with their nails, or fall on the floor after several kicks to the legs, and nevertheless they both would have kept at it, sticking a finger in the eye or pinching each other like wild beasts, while the beau of the day, seated at the bar with a double whiskey, would turn around, show a miniscule smile, and he would be thinking about which one he wanted to take to bed, the blonde or the brunette. And now here they are, sitting by the fire in a small room. Cassandra says she feels some nostalgia when she remembers the number of movies she made, even some bad ones, why deny it, they pigeonholed her and she ended her days as a prostitute in the movies, as if she were not counting the awards that she won when she acted in other types of roles. Bill seems to agree with this. And yes, Gabriele as well, who is seated in one of the armchairs, very close to this couple who she says she admires for staying together all of these years. Naturally Gabriele does not think about that scene now, which wasn’t even filmed, perhaps because Bill suddenly lowers his gaze and says: what’s unfortunate is that we were unable to have children. And Cassandra goes on: we spent half our lives going to doctors and doing fertility treatments, but nothing worked, I managed to get pregnant one time but I lost it at two weeks, the doctors told me I should rest, I abandoned all of my projects, the life I had led up until that time, but the much-desired child did not come. Gabriele asks a question, and as she does Bill shakes his head no and says: what about adopting a child? No, we wanted our own. Well of course, our own, the woman agrees.

 

They say that prisoners never end up getting used to the small size of their cells, which they often have to share with others, but Marianne is accustomed to hers, although she has to make a real effort to turn around and find the switch with her hand, but it’s no use, there is nothing like that on the wall where she was looking for it. Can someone explain to her why she is here and not in her house, where she already would have gotten up a while ago, and like on so many occasions she would prepare Arthur’s breakfast while she asks mournfully, why don’t you do something about that? Or better yet: WHY DON’T YOU DO SOMETHING ABOUT THAT? Yes, the days pass by here, not even if she looked at a calendar could she be sure how long she has been here. She thought she was going somewhere for a visit, and now she is ending her days bound to this apartment, where the smallest one cries in the morning, and she is startled, as if suddenly her memory spoke to her and reminded her that she had children, THAT SHE HAD CHILDREN! Which, if she had to go out and get them she wouldn’t know where to start, and then she remembers how she combed one child’s hair every morning and braided it into pigtails, and sometimes she complained when she combed through tangled hair, ow! ow! the creature complained, like she did just before wetting her diapers, or scratching her cheek. And in the first light of day she asks a question of the first person who walks down the hallway. Thursday, the son-in-law answers. Thursday of what month? The woman asks again, she who every morning gets them to fasten a watch on her wrist, when they have already dressed her and brushed her hair, or even in the middle of breakfast: my watch, I’m missing my watch. And the daughter fastens it on her one more day, before cleaning the room, or later, when she scrubs the floor with hot water. What would I be without my watch? Marianne says, who in a little while pushes up her sleeve and taps it.

 

Gabriele is wearing a dressing gown, so far no one has come to her room, and she is hanging clothes in the closet, but now she opens the door to the balcony and looks at the view. It is a piece of land covered by pastures where cattle graze, not very far away. She yawns while she looks straight ahead, and she sees one of the horses cantering, then it turns around as soon as it gets far away. Suddenly she closes the balcony door and continues taking clothing out of one of her suitcases. Someone knocks on her door with the impression that she is already up. Cassandra asks if she slept well. Bill asks the same thing a little later, while they have breakfast. Very well, she says still in her dressing gown, that tightens from time to time, as if the piece of clothing were sliding on one side, but she controls it with the belt and makes sure it stays in place. A little more coffee? The butler asks. Gabriele lifts her cup, and she spreads butter on her bread while she cuts into a well-done egg with her fork, as if it had been fried on both sides. Outside the sun has been up for a while, just like Cassandra’s look when she glances towards the back of the room and exclaims: what do we have here? And two dogs come in to greet them, tongues lolling, they shake their tails, then they approach and sniff to see whether Gabriele is intruding on their owners. They are like our children, Bill says, feeding them. Like children, adds the other. Later a moment comes in which they give her a choice: horses or shopping? Horses, Gabriele has said.

 

Silvia removes the sheets, the pillow cases, and she makes the bed, which has a leg that squeaks, an irritating noise that she muffles with a piece of cardboard, and she cuts it into a circle so that no one can see it. What does it matter, this bed has been here a very long time, long before she and her husband had their wedding night in it. Yes, they had to deal with their in-laws in this house too, who were closed-minded and suspicious of the new addition, although they made a place for her and put another chair at the table. And now this house is her husband’s, as is the bed and the wardrobe. And wherever she looks, only the sheets and the curtains are hers. The grandson as well, who is walking around and who suddenly grabs onto his grandmother's robe, as if he were about to fall over, and once again she says: late, too late. No, looking closely that is a mistake, this child is not hers, although she takes care of him, changes his diapers and does everything as if he were, but he isn’t. This forced proximity often raises certain questions, and this little one has his own. Mama, he says from time to time. And if you ask him how old he is he holds up one or two fingers, and he actually gets it right frequently, and he claps in the lap of this woman, because since Silvia sees him every day she teaches him things like this. Just like she teaches him not to touch. Not to touch anything that could break. But how to say it, it’s been quite a while that this heaviness has been inside of her, her heart knows it, her heart that beats with the broken remains of an entire life but that is not yet tired, like the tick-tock of a bomb that hasn’t gone off, but there’s no other way. However, a few days ago a neighbor said to her: Silvia, your house is so organized. It’s as neat as a pin! No plates piled up in the sink, no splatters anywhere, as if no one even lived here. And maybe that’s it, who can say. But now, in her room, Silvia opens the closet and the folded clothing appears, placed delicately; that’s how she was taught. She looks into the closet, everything is ironed and hung up, as she thought. Only that blouse of Gabriele’s stands out, she realizes. Is it the color? The cloth? Is it the price of the piece? Or only that instant when the woman exclaimed: but how can you ask that of me?

 

And now is when Gabriele puts on her riding boots, because high heels are not very useful here in the stable, as you might imagine. It is likely that the actress doesn’t know much about the mammals in the equine family, although she once shared a scene with one, which they unloaded from the horse trailer, and it bent its front leg, as if it had its doubts or stage fright, what do I know. It bared its teeth before it fell over on its side, and in fact that horse didn’t stand up again and they took it away, because it was supposed to pull a cart with three or four people inside. Get up! Said the actor of the day, after the owner lashed it furiously because that horse stood up again, and he charged his money, which should have been much more because of the number of times he hit it. The fact is that Gabriele remembers that now, although Bill affirms that the horses here are treated with respect, he says as he brushes one, then he lets the actress continue; that’s how she will get comfortable with it. He opens an outside door and takes out a saddle. Later he teaches the actress how to mount the horse. And although Gabriele makes a little jump and tries, here comes Bill, ready to catch her before she hurts herself. Look, the man says. He makes three deliberate motions, he gets momentum and mounts, holds on and secures his feet. But then overcome by the surprising inexperience of his first wife, he makes a fourth: extend your hand and put your weight back. Hold on tight, he tells her. And in the act Gabriele embraces Bill from behind. But Cassandra is also looking at her husband, and it would seem that she has decided to get a step stool. They each gesture their approval, and then the woman’s horse stands up. A race to the mountain, the winner of which seems to have no importance at all, after all Bill’s horse has to bear more weight, so it is likely that he will lose, or even get lost. Everything depends on this equestrian being able to keep up. An actor who also spent his life at a gallop. But that’s another story.

 

Marianne is alone and is putting her slippers together. Suddenly she makes a gesture of disgust, as if she meant to join one piece to the other, and now she doesn’t know where one ends and where the other begins. They ended up leaving the sewing kit in plain view, it seems impossible they don’t realize that this woman can open the top, put her hand in and take out a needle and sew together these two pieces, which apparently bother her. This old woman sews without her shoes on, only her socks, but she stitches and stitches, and again passes the needle through this rigid material, and it is difficult to get the point in and get it to come out the other side. She intends to baste one slipper to the other. There comes a moment when she raises her hand to her head when she realizes that she didn’t thread the needle. But no matter how hard she tries to thread the needle, it spins around on the floor, she stretches it up, in this mission that could be summarized as the spool of thread that jumps and jumps and never ends, it folds from one side of the chair to the other, an object that diverts her while it circles towards her, until with a last pull it breaks. And although she has to sit down and try again, there is nothing here other than a woman who has just fallen down.

 

Gabriele said to Bill upon dismounting from the horse: the fact is that when I met you I was green and I still couldn’t believe where I was, you don’t know what it’s like to be treated with respect all of a sudden, here Ms. Bates, there Ms. Bates, when my whole life I had been poor Gabi, the one who was always contradictory, someone who could not adapt, a psychiatrist once said, and what did that little word mean, my mother repeated at every step, what are we going to do with you? They scolded me, you don’t know how to cook, you don’t know how to make the bed, you don’t even know how to iron your own clothes, a disaster of a woman, but someday you’ll see, when you marry and you have to do everything without question. And suddenly, Gabriele continues as she passes her hand down the horse’s back, I was sitting in a dressing room, people were coming in with any old excuse and everyone assured me they liked you, the whole day there was someone or another talking to me about you, even the director came up to me to say: I spoke with him, the fact is that love unsettles him. In fact, she starts again as she turns towards Bill, if I had known that they were saying the same things to you that they were saying to me, or that it would end up being a big set-up by our producer, he wouldn’t have dared, but we fell into his trap, they wanted us to talk and we talked, we also discussed it, but in the end we left things clear and love triumphed. Do you regret it, Bill? Not at all, he says. Neither do I, the actress responds forcefully. She decides to sit down close to a tree, which is also very close to the actor, at whom she looks and says somewhat nostalgically: we had some good times together. Gabriele pauses, then looks at him more intently and says: right now I’m thinking about a very special moment in the shower. I remember, he responds. Dear Bill Engel, she exclaims as she puts her arm around his back, I hope to be remembered for other things! Obviously those two years weren’t so much time, if you think about it, it was four to five months maximum they gave us between filming, or when we filmed together, it wasn’t always like that, and especially with everything that your change in producer meant, that offer was so unreasonable that we didn’t even think about the consequences, but everything started to fall apart after that, even before we starting getting tired of each other. Bill looks her in the eye and says: I didn’t want that. Everyone around us, Gabriele starts speaking again, knew that besides being a great actress, Cassandra was an expert in, shall we say, breaking up couples, her weapons of seduction, down deep I should be grateful that she didn’t leave you for someone else. Bill smiles and adds: the truth is that I still love Cassandra a little. At the same time Gabriele looks off in the distance and says: well she’s getting close, do you think she made it to the mountain? Precisely when Bill says: the only thing I know is that she was afraid we were going to get lost. Is that so? Gabriele says with that half smile I don’t know how to describe.

 

Silvia. Every day at the same time she gets a pot out of the cupboard and fills it with water. She might turn around a few times and look at the little one who, seated on the high chair, is giving in to sleep with his head a little off to the side. She also tilts her head when she cuts vegetables in the kitchen, and her gaze gets blurred from the steam from the boiling water when she adds the food she has chopped or sliced. And only the vegetables change, some softer than others, just as the meat is different depending on the day of the month; the month starts with meat from the butcher shop, and ends with meat from frozen bags. Thus the money that could have been saved ends up being eaten or spent on clothing and small appliances that she uses every day, like so many women do. Of course she’s not the only one, but it is a pity that such things don’t alleviate anything for her at all. Then she wakes up the little one who is going to swallow syrup again and she waits for his plate to cool a little that she has so nicely stirred, because today she has strained it. She holds the plate up high, brings the spoon close, and, as if it were something normal, catches the puree that is running down her grandson’s chin. Perhaps one day she’ll resort to one of the strategies she used with her daughter, but not today, because she gets tired quickly and corners the puree, or she prompts him, she gets up and puts in into the refrigerator while once again she gets the baby food that she purchases at the store out of the cupboard, and it would seem that it has the taste of something lower in the pecking order, somewhere that this spoiled child doesn’t belong. Down deep everyone fools him, but some day he will realize it when Silvia stops placating him, or his mother stops. Of course she doesn’t even come into this house, she just stops the car, and sometimes she doesn’t even get out, she leaves two unwrapped gifts here, and as you will understand, it isn’t much of a surprise, but despite this Silvia never ceases to be surprised. Sometimes time seems to stop when she hears the sound of the door, with another step they come close to her, and there is the grandson, who the grandmothers greets with a caress on his cheek, as if she found nothing else of worth in the living room.

 

Marianne is seated in the chair and watches how they bring the fork to her mouth. What else can you do? She says with resignation as she chews. That’s for sure, the daughter thinks. She looks once again at Marianne’s bandaged arm and a couple of bruises on her face. Chew slowly, the daughter says, who today is feeding on both sides, first the little one, then her mother. Perhaps with such a busy agenda she can find a bit of time to eat lunch, this woman who cooks, serves, and receives so little in return. But she is not the only one. Yes, there are others like her, and blah, blah, blah. But no one would want to be in her place, starting with her brother whom she calls to tell him about anything new, when the only new thing would be that everything is the same. Because Marianne has hurt herself today, and the daughter got frightened, she didn’t know whether to turn her over, call an ambulance, or what to do in a situation like that. She called her brother, told him to come right away, and evidently Marianne’s son thought the worst, that she had died, what else could it be? Of course they made him come for something ridiculous, as if he could just leave work at any old time, and there was his mother, stretched out on the floor and saying: son, is that you? And the son: yes mother. It is yet another slap in the face for the daughter who Marianne has so often called young lady or ma’am. Together they turned her over, picked her up in their arms and took her to the emergency room where she was cared for, meanwhile the daughter told the brother that she can’t do it anymore. And the subject of a nursing home has come up, either that or each one has her fifteen days on, fifteen days off. You know best, the brother said, who has taken out his wallet and left a couple of bills before leaving. But Marianne remains, and the daughter, who suddenly looks into her eyes, brings the fork close and says: do you see? It was nothing after all.

 

Silvia tells him about her day. Silvia asks her husband how work was. Good, he says, furrowing his brow as he drinks water, as if he were irritated by the question. Yes, she has gotten her husband to talk, but now he only hopes that she will leave him alone for a while, because today he does not feel like talking. Better to turn on the television, but see, with every advertisement she asks him a question. Do you still drive the machine? Do they still make you drive the machine? Yes, he says. He looks at his plate again or looks at the television, as does Silvia, who looks at her husband again: they still haven’t fired him? Who, the husband asks. Who do you think! She exclaims while she clears away the plates, your subordinate. No, the man says. She brings a tray to the table and serves him a portion of meat, the other is for her. Something will have to be done some day, Silvia says, putting the napkin in her lap, and if not, then after some time passes, it won’t be the first and it won’t be the last, someone better will come along. Or worse, the man says. He looks at her from time to time, and would certainly like to understand her in this willy-nilly conversation where one day he might be considered important. That’s right, she said, you’ve spent your life working for others and they haven’t been able to see it. What, the man asks. Silvia holds up her fork and says: that you had great worth. But now she brings up the fork, chews when she realizes what she has said. Strange that she wasn’t completely aware, but what’s done is done.

 

Cassandra looks at the actress who is just visiting and who is wearing a checked shirt, pants and riding boots, who helps her cut carrots that she will be feeding to the cattle. She has a pile of raw vegetables in the bucket, which is how Cassandra takes food to the troughs, this is how the foreman taught her to do it, and this is how she shows Gabriele to do it in turn. Cassandra is the type of person who believes in full integration of guests, and as she returns with the bucket empty, she lights a cigarette and offers one to Gabriele. No, Gabriele says, I quit. Wow, the other says in surprise. She comes closer and says: can you tell me what happened today with Bill? And the other turns and says: of course, we were talking. About what? She asks, intrigued. This actress knew how to intimidate men on the big screen, even the most robust of them, those who were twice her size, and now she does the same thing with Gabriele, but more simply, because she doesn’t even need to draw her dagger. About what? The woman repeats. After Bill and Gabriele broke up the newspapers tended to compare Cassandra and Gabriele, as if black and white had a point of comparison. Obviously Cassandra has trophies all over the living room. You took him away from me once, Gabriele says, and I never even asked you why. Ah! The other exclaims with a laugh, Bill was old enough to know what he was doing. I’ll give you that, the actress says, washing out the bucket and leaving it on the ground. But then Cassandra approaches her, and crossing her arms, says: men fall very easily, then they say that we women are the weaker sex, when they have more weaknesses than us, what can I tell you that you don’t already know? We were young, attractive, and we spent entire months closed up with them, we went over scripts, we talked, we laughed, we became confidants, we became accomplices, and I lived every second intensely because I knew that everything I had would one day be gone, and then one day I decided to leave it, but I remember it as a fantastic period of my life! Although the work was also very hard and they shouted at us, but I only regret one thing, the actor who I most wanted avoided me at all costs, they said about me that I always got whatever I wanted, that I always had someone else in mind, but of course not the one I wanted, not him, oh, but on occasions I surprised him taking a dip in the pool, I fell madly in love, and I watched him while I swam, my heart racing, one day I got out of the pool, I went up to him and there I had him, so close and also so far away when he jumped in, he went under my legs without stopping, and so, it was never to be. Gabriele smiles when she says: and who was it, tell me, who was it? John Faison, the other says, but long before he married you, when he and I shared a producer.

 

Silvia takes her grandson in the pram, covers him, and then she buttons her coat by the door. Sometimes she sees a neighbor whom she greets, and they always chat about something. I’m coming from buying eggs, the woman says, today my daughter is coming and I’m making the apple pie that she likes so much. And life here is like this sidewalk, long and narrow and often inconvenient with this pram, which, without her wanting to, causes others on the sidewalk to step down into the street, but it doesn’t matter, because some man who is already walking away exclaims, what a healthy boy, and from this country too. And Silvia goes from one sidewalk to another until she stops at the hairdresser. In this empty seat where as soon as she sits down they wash her hair and rinse it with warm water. Hair that the hairdresser dries with a towel, while she indicates to the client to move to one of the revolving chairs. Which does not mean that Silvia is going to spin the chair like her granddaughter would do if she were here. At the same time, the hairdresser untangles her hair with a comb, an instrument she keeps in the pocket of her robe, when she asks: the same as always? And Silvia nods with her head and says: I saw the sign outside. If you need someone to help you with the shop, you just found her.

 

Gabriele is in her room when she sees the man who so often escapes her. It would seem that she leaves off what she was doing, which wasn’t much, and she starts to follow him through the house, which is large and full of paintings, which the woman looks at, if they leave her alone for a while, her hands behind her, which she often tenses up because everything is as expected. But if she turns, there is only one bust of stone towards which she tilts her head a little, and at the same time the actor appears looking younger, as she remembers him. Because that’s more or less how Bill was, and she ends up placing her hand on the sculpture’s cheek, whose face is partly shadowed, and her hand is also covered by the shadow created by the gunman’s hat. A cold touch, as cold as Bill’s walk, who is approaching her silently, and he stops before this piece that is Gabriele, and who has been acquiring more value as each hour passes. But she says nothing, apart from looking at him, and she straightens the collar of his shirt. Bill is no longer the same, he has seemed like a dream for some time, although not the most agreeable one. He takes her hand, removes it from the sculpture, which it would seem she has latched onto like a magnet, and he brings it up to his face, which is warmer, and in turn pricks her with his very short beard, which Gabriele passes one of her fingers over, as if she were answering a question that neither of them has formulated. Bill’s hand, coming close to her face, is saying that she is also no longer what she was, but similar, and it might be that it does not matter to him. He takes a small step, as if he wanted to hold her in his arms which are, how can one say it? Gabriele would say that Bill’s arms seem to be slower than what she remembered, when formerly they melted into each other, and this silence didn’t matter because it didn’t exist, and the words came out without pause when she threw herself in his arms and said: I love you, Bill! And he often said back to her: and I love you, sweetheart. Although now there is not this impression, although Gabriele watches how he slowly encircles her waist with both arms. A hard actor who has become sentimental, and who scrunches her blouse between his fingers. A piece from Cassandra’s closet, she could add, and today without any makeup she looks better than the blouse’s owner, Bill thinks, who moves her hair off the collar with his chin and is about to kiss her neck. They can see us, Gabriele whispers. She says this very close to him, he was about to do it but now he moves discreetly away. Nothing has happened here, and the actress puts the blouse back in place.

 

Yes, something has happened, Silvia guesses as she gets her wallet, looking once again at the mirror and she pays. She pushes the pram until she gets to the park and lifts the boy out, who, as usual, goes directly to the slide. It is a place where his hair blows back for a few seconds, or he gets a scratch on his knees. Silvia puts her purse on her lap, or with a finger touches the shape of the handle, which used to be gold, but over the years it has flaked off and now the moldy metal shows through. Just that. Because if she stops her finger, well, they’ve made her feel old, as if she couldn’t learn anything, it doesn’t matter that she’s washed hair her entire life, hers, her daughter’s, and sometimes her grandchildren’s as well, there are three of them, although who knows where the oldest one is. Just once she would like to feel like that: disappeared off the face of the earth. Then they would take an interest in her, all of those who keep her here, including in the park, where they would take this boy away from her in a trice. No, if she saw herself in the mirror of the hairdresser’s saying things like: no ma’am, of course it doesn’t bother me, yes I understand. Perfectly, she had added. But why did I feel like I had to add that? The woman thinks. And she gets angry with herself, she takes out a Kleenex and crushes it in her hand, as if she could choke her regrets in that two-layer piece of paper. A moment later she looks off in the distance and shouts: come back to grandmother! And although the grandson falls down, he climbs the ladder and slides down the slide again, he doesn’t go to her, and she is obliged to go drag him up off the grass, a little muddy. She sits him down on the bench, cleans his hands, shoes, and puts him back in the pram, and she will keep looking for places that need people, and she might be that person. Provided that someone needs this new Silvia, because no one understands everything these days, not even in a clothing store where she would not buy anything, she wouldn’t even take in a purchase voucher.

 

Poor Cassandra, the young butler will think as he wipes the glasses with a towel, there aren’t many, three, and even so there is an extra one, but which one? And he smiles to himself as he watches how the lady of the house reads the press, as if she had decided to let down her guard due to fatigue. It is truly exhausting to spend the entire day waiting on them. Since last night the actress has had a hunch. He wouldn’t want to be in her shoes. Although of course, as an actress who has won numerous awards, she can fake it and pretend like it doesn’t matter to her. Cassandra turns the page and sees that the news continues to happen without cease, although now she takes a pause along the way, but she will start up again when Gabriele leaves. To tell the truth, as long as she is here, she feels things that she thought were forgotten, like not trusting Bill; the other one, she has never trusted in her life. But she turns another page and finally stops on some article. What was it some director said, she can’t remember who, when he saw that she was tense? Just breathe slowly. And she does this while she half-closes her eyes, although suddenly she opens them and sees Bill go stealthily by, so she closes them and continues with her breathing exercises, and she does this until she hears a question: are you okay? Perfectly, Cassandra nods her head, she watches as Gabriele comes into the kitchen, and suddenly she feels like helping out the butler.

 

The telephone rings on the middle floor. As it does, someone leaves off what they were doing and goes to the phone, which is in the hall, and frequently, depending on who is calling, the door to the dining room is closed slightly, seeking some privacy. Another thing for which Marianne has ceased to fight, since the very moment at which her daughter started to dress her, to undress her, to put diapers on her, to take them off, to wash her with a sponge, and when she isn’t doing these things she leaves her alone and closes the door of the room from the outside. She couldn’t lift a finger without someone knowing, with the little privacy that remains, and now she can’t lift her arm, which is protected by a stretchy bandage and a scarf that someone has tied with a knot. And there is no way to explain it, better to sleep in her stockings, maybe also because of these pills that promise to preserve her neurons, she strains to hear who her daughter is talking to as she runs a hand over the bandage, which she tries to lift with a nail, and perhaps with some patience she will manage. Marianne would like to have things her way, she has managed to do much more complicated things than this, like have a profession and be respected for her work, although Arthur made less money than her, and by the way her husband saw this as the greatest failure of his life. A strange sensation that was appeased just by getting on his motorcycle and venting adrenaline on the street below. Sometimes Marianne returned to the house after an exhausting day, and there was Arthur, waiting at a light not having seen her, then he would hit the gas and head off. My dear Gabriele, she writes in this dream, when will you write me, remind me of who I was, I would give anything to know.

 

Silvia goes towards the door of the elementary school, where she waits for her granddaughter. The granddaughter comes over, gives her her arm, and hums some song she learned at noon, so many hours without seeing her and she is full of new things, it would seem there is a girl at school who has a very pretty bracelet made of leather with two daisies in the center, and I want one just like it! Yes, there is so much to tell, and you see, that’s how it is, it is the little girl that tells you. She sticks her tongue out. One after the other, how many more are there, they are almost identical at this elementary school, it is easy to confuse them, if it weren’t for that bracelet that her granddaughter doesn’t yet have on her wrist, and that she hides with her other hand. Silvia goes down the avenue, just a little more slowly than if she were walking alone, since she doesn’t usually stop to look at shop windows, even though they grab her sleeve and say: grandmother, will you buy the bracelet for me? And the woman inclines her head as if she senses what awaits another twenty steps ahead, sticks of lead for the pencil, faster grandmother, it’s down another street! As if life were like that. If everything she asked for were objects with a limited life, Silvia knows it each time she opens her jewelry box and thinks about the things she could have had, but instead there is every type of bracelet there, purchased to alleviate the monotony of her daily life with whimsical little things, but in the end the metal tarnishes or they make her skin itch. She should scrub the silver with some product so that it will shine again! As soon as she goes back home she cleans everything she can find, asking her daughter if she likes this one. No! The granddaughter says on that day, when she sees it is not the day.

 

Bill, Gabriele says to this man who turns from the stable door, surprising him while he enjoys the cool air and looks at the open sky. And the things they see off in the distance, it seems that the actress looks her first husband directly in the eyes and says: it’s best that I leave. Bill takes off a leather jacket, puts it under his arm and says: come with me. He steers Gabriele to the back, where there is a large covered porch. The actor opens the door and assures her that he has spent many years here, reading scripts and rejecting more than one, a few, he says, and with a decided gesture invites her to sit down. He brings a second chair close and also sits down. As you can see, Bill says, we have a very large house, however, this is the only place where I have been able to truly concentrate. Sometimes, he continues, we are incapable of leaving our humble origins aside, and we looks for spaces similar to those that we remember, at times I have asked myself if winning was worth it, and ceasing to be one of so many anonymous individuals who can walk around peacefully and who don’t get stopped at every step. For me, Bill confesses, success was too much, although you adapt to it, and I was lucky that many directors trusted in what I was doing, where there were horses they thought of me, I enjoyed everything we filmed, then there came a point when I didn’t fit in anymore, they needed someone younger for those types of roles, and that took me by surprise, it was as if everyone had come to an agreement and they told me they no longer had me in mind for projects with horses, that I should do something else. Bill lets his arm fall on the table and says: I didn’t always go around on horseback, and as for the rest, the last two films were bad and there was nothing to do, they didn’t try again, my movie career lasted several decades, although there comes a day when failure starts to erase the success I had, I couldn’t stand that and now that it’s no longer there, I miss it, do you feel the same? Gabriele looks at Bill and says: it happens to us all, one day they lead us to believe that we are unique, and the next day we no longer are.

 

As for Cassandra, Bill says, she was on top and had major projects that one day she started to cancel, although that didn’t turn out as she planned, she lost the son we were expecting, no one ever imagined that a child was the reason that Cassandra was saying goodbye to the big screen and that she wouldn’t act again, after she lost the baby she wasn’t the same, having a child became her obsession, she started buying objects and clothing willy-nilly for newborns for when it arrived, and she ended up spending all of her time with relatives just so she could have a niece or nephew in her arms for a few minutes. Bill touches her wrist and says: I would have liked to have had children, but not in that way, when we were hopeful every month, with the consequent disappointment, one day the doctors said that if she got pregnant again it could put her life in danger, but that didn’t seem to matter to her, she said that was rubbish, but for me that was the most prudent thing. Work, and what it meant to spend entire months away from her, Bill starts speaking again, meant that I ended up forgetting that feeling of blame that still envelops this house, and that has to do with the children that we didn’t have, but at the same time that was the rudder that led me back to her when I was done filming.

 

Silvia sees her daughter’s car parked in the parking space in front of the door. She tells her granddaughter, who picks up her things up off the table with a certain parsimoniousness. Silvia helps her. She helps her grandson too, picking him up off the floor with one arm, and when she puts him in the pram she smells him. Well of course he just did it again, that’s how it always is, although this grandmother is tired of doing this dance here, there and everywhere. Her daughter is capable of changing diapers on some part of the sofa, and making sure everything goes back to normal. Mama, the boy says to his mother. That grandson who frequently moves away and runs on tiptoes down the hallway with a finger in his mouth. And now he only has eyes for his mother, who talks with Silvia about having to get a move on with the potty training, besides the syrups and baby food from the store, and how little it costs to make this creature happy, who asks for the bracelet again. Yes, her mother says. She glances around the living room before leaving, and sometimes says: is everything okay? And Silvia nods her head yes and answers: as always. But today she is daring, tells her she shouldn’t buy such a thing because she has behaved badly and hit her grandmother on the arm because she was so angry she didn’t get what she wanted. Yes, Silvia knew her daughter would say something like: did you do that? And that would be it. But they are leaving, they go quietly and she is alone. She opens the door of the refrigerator, leaves two meals on the countertop, and while before she would have paid for a little silence, now she would pay not to have it. Will you make me something to eat? Gabriele said to her at least thirty hours ago. An eternity, and despite that sometimes she still turns, as if expecting to see her sitting at that same corner of the table where she had eaten lunch. And Silvia said, heating up a skillet before throwing in the ingredients she was chopping: now we’re alone. And Gabriele: then come closer. With that insolent gaze of hers, as if she didn’t believe what she was doing, however there she was, very close to her face and saying: I still feel you inside me. And Silvia thinks about that, while she sets the table and moves around, her hand grabbing an edge of the chair, then she moves away, as if she could still feel it, but not in the same way. She is startled with this silence that speaks inside of her, similar to the sound of a seashell, and if she looks again, a long golden strand of hair is in her hand, but then she loses it. Even turning off the stove she finds herself stuck at the part when she’d said to her: do you like it? Do you like it like this? And she had responded: I like men. But then one appears at the door.

 

Do you remember, Gabriele exclaims, when we were swimming in the pool and we had those neighbors, what were their names? It doesn’t matter, but while we were sunbathing we knew their kids were on the other side of the garden, with all that racket they made. I know, says Bill, they were unbearable. And us, she says, we looked at ourselves and said, you don’t know how good we’ve got it! Yes, Bill nods his head. He suddenly half-closes his eyes and remembers how he put sunscreen on her back. Gabriele continues, we thought more about the little time we could be together than about children; they would have tied us down and forced us to stay together, even if we didn’t feel like it. We didn’t have any ties other than what we felt for each other, we didn’t even promise to be faithful to each other, we pretended like we were free individuals, but at that time we were afraid that if we were together it would end, we were too vulnerable and we stopped trusting each other, admit it Bill, while it lasted we were more aware of who the other was working with than we were of ourselves, I should have implored for our marriage to last longer, but then I ask myself: what good would that have done, if it was going to end anyway? There was a moment, Bill says, when you put the divorce papers on the table, when I wanted to tear them up and kiss you. Well, she says standing up, I wasn’t aware of that at all. I know, he responds, also standing up, there were a lot of mixed emotions. Gabriele walks along this interior room that darkens in places. Only her silhouette moves away, stops, then returns. Well then, she says as she lifts one hand to his elbow, more or less like now. Yes, he says. Bill bends down to her, this woman who just a moment ago was able to stand firm on high heels, can you see? Now she has to hold onto him as tightly as she can. Other than this, there are just goodbye kisses. But look at Gabriele, she goes back to Bill’s mouth as if her train were about to leave, and then another, another, two more. Or him, in his way, encircling one woman in his arms although the other is not very far away. And Bill wouldn’t trade the other one for this one, he’s been settled for a long time. But one day in his memoirs, he might explain how he had the longest embrace that he can remember with Gabriele.

 

Mother, the daughter says to Marianne, we have to talk. She looks at the table that today seems to be full with her son-in-law and her grandchildren. No one wanted to miss how the old woman would react, and everyone is looking at her, as if she had the last word: in a home? You can see she has said it. Yes, the daughter replies, they just told us there is space. Marianne looks at one, then another, and at the step stair in height of the three grandchildren. And what am I going to do there? The woman asks, who then hears: nothing that you don’t want to do. And how is that? The old woman insists, who suddenly looks at the middle grandchild and says: what’s his name doesn’t come to the window anymore, I go for breadcrumbs, but I do this, that, and there’s nothing left for me, then my husband comes on the motorcycle, whistles through the window or accelerates in the street below, my God! Please don’t let him get into an accident, but look, I write and I write on the blackboard, something will work out if he doesn’t return, ah, but then he’s after me the whole day saying, Marianne do this, Marianne do that, Marianne, Marianne, what would you do in my place? Arthur has already died? What do you know, she replies to her grandson, you don’t know anything about anything, I’m the only one who knows, and then there’s the other one, the one with the cards, one day she comes and the next day… Fuck all of you! You, you, you, you and you.

 

Silvia fills her glass and drinks. Her husband picks up a slice of bread and says: you’re not going to be with your lover today? She puts the glass back on the table and shakes her head no. The man puts the bread on his plate, and as he does so he sees a crack on the edge, although the woman’s plate is also nicked, because these are old dishes, but they’ll make it a bit longer, they might even last a long time if they are treated with care. And the husband says: what’s going on, one day he comes, one day he doesn’t, or is he already tired of you? How do I know, Silvia exclaims. She fills up her glass again and drinks. The husband lifts his own glass. She passes him the pitcher, and then turns on the television. But it’s strange, the husband stands up, turns off the television and says: I see you sulking all the time so I guess your affair is over, and what do I do, put up with you, put up with you, and I support you. Silvia looks steadily at him and says quietly: thank you for reminding me of that. You’re welcome, the husband says with a gesture. And let me just make an interjection here, since it’s now out in the open: you’ve spent your whole life complaining, and look how well you live, I would like to spend the whole day sleeping, taking care of the grandchildren, talking with the neighbors, and when I’m not doing that looking for lovers. Silvia puts her hands on her head and says: I didn’t go looking for anything, it just appeared. How, the husband asks, how did it just appear? She clears away the plates and puts them in the sink, turns around, and looking at one of the chairs says: I miss Gabriele, that’s all. Yes, the husband does as well.

 

Marianne goes by car with an old suitcase in her lap. Just a moment ago the son-in-law ordered her to put the suitcase in the trunk. But in the end the old woman got her way. The daughter as well, who looks at traffic signals, or tucks a strand of hair behind her ear when she says: I hope we didn’t forget anything, we were in such a hurry. What does it matter, Marianne is indifferent if she takes two outfits or three, if she takes new or used underwear. It’s not like she’s going to show it to anyone. Ultimately, they end up labeling each piece of clothing in her suitcase, so when they do the laundry at the residence, someone will put it into the correct box when it’s dry. It’s like those campouts when she accompanied and guided the students from mountain to mountain with a compass in her hand so as not to lose which way was north, and on the way back, from town to town and by the road. They hung damp clothing on a long cord extended from one point to another, she and the other teachers, or some father who went along, or a mother, as usual, hanging their clothing by the seams so it doesn’t wrinkle. And Marianne looks from one side to another, she might have even walked down to the road, but Arthur didn’t appear, although he had promised, he didn’t go, not even if it was Sunday. That cold air did have beneficial properties, because her cheeks got pink, and as she breathed, it seemed like her heart might freeze. An agreeable sensation, as if nothing existed other than grass and clothing that the wind ruffled before a storm, not even her own children who were tumbling around down below, could manage to get her out of that type of lethargy, and everything seemed predictable, only the air was unpredictable, and the drops of rain when everyone got alarmed and ran to bring the clothing inside. And she watched from afar. But, the daughter says, who has suddenly picked up her suitcase, we’ll see you Sunday. Yes, Marianne has understood this. Arthur said that too, and look, it’s better not to think about that.

 

Cassandra snacks from different trays this evening, where there is plenty of food to choose from. Small delicacies that the palate tastes slowly and each time perceives a different taste, acidic, salty, bitter and sweet appear and get confused in her throat with the help of a good wine. Cassandra looks at her husband and gives him one of her newer looks, that of: I’ll give this back to you two-fold.
